Ecotype_references: A detailed explanation for the aquatic affinities of crocodylians is given in our Supplementary Text.
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_06519
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Mounted
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: These lizards often use water as a means of escape, and have clear morphological adaptations to suit this purpose (Bauer and Jackman 2007 [Bauer AM, Jackman T (2007). Global diversity of lizards in freshwater (Reptilia: Lacertilia). In: Balian, E.V., Lévêque, C., Segers, H., Martens, K. (eds) Freshwater Animal Diversity Assessment. Developments in Hydrobiology, vol 198. Springer: Dordrecht. pp. 581–586. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-4020-8259-7_55.]). However, while individuals have been observed residing in a river, they are most often observed and mainly reside on land (Denzer et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.11646/zootaxa.4747.2.3]; O’Shea et al. 2021 IUCN assessment for Hydrosaurus amboinensis). As a result, we treat them as “Transiently Aquatic”. Reasonably, however, Watkins-Colwell 1994 [Watkins-Colwell G (1994). Cladistic biogeography of the water dragons, Hydrosaurus and Physignathus (Lepidosauria: Agamidae). Master of Science Thesis, Department of Biological Sciences, Ohio University. Bell & Howell Information and Learning Company: Ann Arbor. 68 pp.]  calls these lizards “routinely semiaquatic” (p. 24), and a “Moderately Aquatic” designation may be preferred depending on the frequency of their submerged activity. In the absence of additional behavioral data, we assume recurring but typically short forays into the water for this species, which then tentatively merits a “Transiently Aquatic” designation.
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved H. pustulatus specimen YPM_HERR_012716 and H. amboinensis specimens YPM_HERR_012886 and YPM_HERR_010046 show that hands and feet both possess laterally projecting scale fringes, which are far more prominent on the feet. We tentatively score both fore- and hindlimbs as having interdigital integument, though developmental work is required to confidently distinguish the pedal fringes from lobate webbing.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Mounted
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths present on the unguals measured, though it was difficult to tell.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: Green iguanas spend most of their time on land, but retreat into the water for great stretches of time when threatened; however, they have also been observed entering the water without provocation and may spend considerable time there, even occassionally  engaging in intertidal foraging behavior (Censky et al. 1998 [https://doi.org/10.1038/26886]; Walter and Smith  2004 [Walter Jr E, Smith HT (2004). Colonization success by green iguanas in Florida. Iguana 11: 154–161.]; Krysko et al. 2007 [Krysko KL, Enge KM, Donlan EM, Seitz JC, Golden EA (2007). Distribution, natural history, and impacts of the introduced green iguana (Iguana iguana) in Florida. Iguana 14:142–151.]; Furness 2021 [https://doi.org/10.17161/randa.v28i3.15892]). For these reasons, they are Transiently to Moderately Aquatic. The frequency and duration of this behaviors leads us to tentatively call the species "Transiently Aquatic", though a Moderately Aquatic designation may be more appropriate for certain populations.
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_12217
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Mounted
Ungual_measurement_notes: Presence of keratinous sheath(s) prevented accurate ungual bone measurements.

Ecotype_references: Chameleons are collectively described as occupying terrestrial habitats, with no reference to semi-aquatic habits (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, pp. 583–584: Chamaeleonidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). To our knowledge, there are no known extant chameleon species with even transiently semi-aquatic lifestyles. Oceanic dispersal from Madagascar to mainland Africa has been suggested for some species (Raxworthy et al. 2002 [https://doi.org/10.1038/415784a]), but this was presumably accomplished by floating on vegetation rafts, rather than swimming.
Limb_type_references: Chameleons have stiff syndactylous finger pairs / triads (see Molnar et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jmor.20708]). We verified the presence of these "pincer-palms" with several fluid-preserved chameleon specimens (Bradypodion fischeri: YPM_HERR_009976, Brookesia perarmata: YPM_HERR_011451, Calumma brevicornis: YPM_HERR_009788, Chamaeleo deremensis: YPM R 10314).
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA
proxp_measurement_notes: Mounting position prevented feasible width measurements.

Ecotype_references: Anolis equestris is highly arboreal and retreats into canopies when startled, generally avoiding the ground (Meshaka 1993 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/24320560]; Fong 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-3.RLTS.T203882A2771981.en]). We find no reference to aquatic habits in this species.
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved Anolis equestris specimen YPM_HERR_007028: This species lacks true interdigital webbing, but has lobe-like toe pads, which we tentatively score as lobate webbing (although these less clearly reach to the base of the digits and it remains unclear whether they're formed by the same developmental program as lobate webbing).
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: We find no reference to aquatic habits in this desert-dwelling species (Hammerson et al. 2007 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2007.RLTS.T64081A12734740.en]).
Limb_type_references: Phrynosoma spp. scored from live individual of Phrynosoma_hernandesi observed at a distance in AZ, USA, in May 2019. This and research-grade iNaturalist observations of P. platyrhinos (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152262105; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/150776729; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/149912695) support the claim that this genus lacks interdigital webbing.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. The earliest Phrynosoma spp. date earlier. We give the date here for those confidently referred to P. solare.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: Chelydra resides on the bottoms of shallow freshwater basins and seldom bask out of the water, but will make extended overland journeys to find nesting sites (Obbard and Brooks 1980 [http://www.jstor.com/stable/3891480]; Whitfield and Luhring 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1016/B978-012370626-3.00197-6]; Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 534, Chelydridae  [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.])
Limb_type_references: Chelydra species scored as having complete, extensive webbing, based on stage 25 embryos figured in Yntema 1968 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jmor.1051250207], plate 10.
Included_unguals?: Digit-dependent (all digits except pD5 are clawed)
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: Lue and Chen 1999 report data for exclusively terrestrial habits in this species, while others (eg, the IUCN Asian Turtle Trade Working Group 2000 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2000.RLTS.T5960A11965283.en]) suggest semi-aquatic habits associated with ponds and rice paddies. Due to this contradiction, we consider the ecotype of this species ambiguous.

Ecotype_references: This species routinely basks and nests on land, and may travel several hundred meters overland to reach the nesting site, but it spends the vast majority of its time in the water; although it is reported to sometimes inhabit fast-moving rivers, Apalone spinifera seems to predominantly occupy shallower, more slow-moving waters (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 531: Trionychinae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Joyce and Gauthier 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2003.2523]; van Dijk  2011 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2011-1.RLTS.T163451A5607536.en]).
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_10088 and research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/151938636; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/151314935) show that this species has soft-tissue paddles encompassing the zeugopodium. Online clips (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=thbo70OUW74; https://www.youtube.com/shorts/L8YFNAd1gCI) show that its digits are independently mobile and visibly distinguishable.

Ecotype_references: All chelonioids are highly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle, and spend the vast majority of their lives in the water, but nevertheless spend an extended period of time on land at least once each breeding season to nest on shorelines, over which they may travel great distances (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 531, Cheloniidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). As a result, we consider them "Highly" rather than "Fully" aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All crown chelonioids (“sea turtles”) were scored as having strongly flippered fore- and hindlimbs, following Wyneken 2001 [Wyneken J (2001). The anatomy of sea turtles. Seattle, WA: U.S. Department of Commerce NOAA Technical Memorandum NMFS-SEFSC-470.]. This was supported by direct observation of fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_02957 (Caretta caretta).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: The limb morphology of this taxon alludes to a predominantly terrestrial arboreal/scansorial lifestyle (Simões et al. 2017: Zool J Linn Soc), but we cannot rule out a Transiently Aquatic lifestyle. As a result, its ecotype remains ambiguous.
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: For femur, did proxmost point along anterior margin to distmost point along same margin. Hand length taken on ImageJ (original caliper measurement included ungual). This specimen is a cast of the holotype, BSPG 1937 I 1a (Simões et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1111/zoj.12487]: Zool J Linn Soc).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Moon 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1080/14772019.2017.1394922]. Note that we assume monophyly of Ichthyosaurus genus here, which is highly dubious. The actual topology of this specimen within Neoichthyosauria remains unclear and requires clarification.
Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered and demonstrably viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1986, p. 73 [Romer AS, Parsons TS (1986). The Vertebrate Body, Sixth Edition (First Edition 1949). CBS College Publishing: New York. 679 pp.]; Thewissen and Taylor 2007 [Thewiseen JGM, Taylor MA (2007). Aquatic Adaptations in the Limbs of Amniotes. Chapter 18, in: Hall BK (ed.), Fins into Limbs: Evolution, Development, and Transformation. The University of Chicago Press: Chicago. pp. 310–322]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines as “Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: All ichthyosaurs are commonly described as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (eg, Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773]; Motani 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2005)025[0462:DTMIAD]2.0.CO;2]; Maxwell 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jez.b.22459]; Gutarra and Rahman 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1111/brv.12790]); Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]. These flippers were highly integrated structures [Fernández et al. 2020 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2020.0281], and presumably encased in dense connective tissue like those of modern-day cetaceans (Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]), so that their digits were not independently mobile.
Notes on occurrences: Many species were historically labeled "Ichthyosaurus", but today this genus only contains 4–5 valid species: I. communis, I. breviceps, I. conybeari, I. anningae, and perhaps I. intermedius (Lomax and Massare 2015 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2014.903260]). Only entries for these five species were considered in setting the occurrence range shown left.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: Testudinids are typically described as being fully terrestrial, and (with the possible exception of species in the genus Manouria), we find no written evidence for even transiently aquatic habits in the clade Testudinidae (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 537: Testudinidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Natchev et al. 2015 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.1172]; Young et al. 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2016.0732]).
Limb_type_references: Testudinoids scored based on taxidermied adult Geochelone gigantea specimen YPM_HERR_016084, fluid-preserved testudinid specimens (Geochelone carbonaria: YPM_HERR_005961; Gopherus polyphemus: YPM_HERR_007115) and CT scan of fluid-preserved Psammobates tentorius specimen MVZ Herp_69192. Testudinoids have stiff connective tissue encasing each set of manual and pedal digits in an immobile extended stub-like palm, with only the ungual claws protruding.
Notes on occurrences: The earliest extinct crown testudinids are from the Priabonian (Vlachos and Rabi 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1111/cla.12227]), which has an older bound of 37.8 Ma (https://stratigraphy.org/timescale/). Because the species ID of this Recent specimen is unresolved, we conservatively date the upper bound for its divergence accordingly.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Hand length is either 6.28 or 12.16, depending on whether unclear bone is metacarpal or distal carpal. Specimen is uncatalogued. It's a tortoise so it's part of Testudinidae. Need ask Jacques or someone else whether bone is carpal or metacarpal. I need to check whether I have a photo of the specimen to show him.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Gauthier 1994 [https://doi.org/10.1017/S247526300000129X], Neenan et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms2633 ], and Simões et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-020-17190-9] place Choristodera as a basally branching lineage of stem archosaur.
Ecotype_references: Choristoderes were highly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle. Many but not all of them gave birth to live young (Ji et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-010-0654-2]; Jiang et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41559-023-02074-0]), and the necks of champsosaurs in particular were more limited in dorsiflexion than modern crocodilians, casting doubt on their ability to ambush terrestrial prey (Matsumoto et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1111/joa.13600]). However, it remains disputed whether they were capable on walking on land (see Ji et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-010-0654-2] and references therein). As a result, their current designation remains tentative. Choristoderes have only been found in freshwater paleoenvironments (Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]).
Limb_type_references: At least one specimen of the choristodere Monjurosuchus splendens preserve skin impressions that show complete, extensive pedal webbing and complete moderate-to-extensive manual webbing (Gao et al. 2000 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2000)020[0417:EFMOAS]2.0.CO;2]). However, to our knowledge, no known champsosaur specimens show similarly direct evidence of interdigital webbing. Based on exceptional soft-tissue preservation, webbing has been clearly identified in one species of choristodere (Monjurosuchus splendens [GMV 2135]: Gao et al. 2000 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2000)020[0417:EFMOAS]2.0.CO;2]), and suggested for Hyphalosaurus on the basis of scale morphology in PKUP V1052 (Gao and Ksepka 2008 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1469-7580.2008.00907.x]), though the latter claim has yet to be tested with other modalities of data. To our knowledge, interdigital webbing has not been directly demonstrated in any choristodere taxa except for Monjurosuchus, so the webbing phenotypes of individual species within Choristodera remain unclear.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: 80% sure this is hindlimb and not forelimb

Ecotype_references: The morphology of this taxon suggests a fully terrestrial lifestyle, but cannot rule out transient excursions into an aquatic medium (Olson 1975), so its ecotype remains ambiguous.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Rhacheosaurus_gracilis replaced the binomial designation “Geosaurus_gracilis”, to which it was synonymized by Young and De Andrade 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-3642.2009.00536.x]. Our phylogenetic placement of geosaurs was derived from Young and De Andrade 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-3642.2009.00536.x], Young et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-3642.2011.00734.x], and Johnson et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.9808].
Ecotype_references: Metriorhynchids show several adaptations for a fully pelagic marine lifestyle (Romer 1956, p. 345 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Young et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-3642.2011.00734.x]; Foffa et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rsos.231951]; Johnson et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.9808]). Their flippered limb morphology casts doubt on their ability to walk on land for extended periods, and their lack of dorsal ornamentation with thermoregulatory osteoderms suggests that they did not engage in routine basking behavior and perhaps instead relied on muscle contraction associated with near-constant swimming for heat production (Spindler et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.26879/1099]; de Araujo Sena & Cubo 2023 [https://doi.org/10.26879/1099]). However, we do not know whether they came onto land to lay their eggs, so while we do know that they were either highly or fully aquatic, we cannot resolve which of these designations is more accurate.
Limb_type_references: Geosaurs have highly derived "paddle-like" limbs with reduced perichondral ossification (Fraas 1902 [Fraas E (1902). Die Meer-Crocodilier (Thalattosuchia) des oberen Jura unter specieller Berücksichtigung von Dacosaurus und Geosaurus. Palaeontographica (1846-1933) Band 49 Lieferung 1 (1902), pp. 1–72.]; Caldwell 1997 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/4523834]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ajmg.10773]). Both its fore- and its hindlimbs are highly modified for swimming. However, the relative extremity, in the forelimbs, of joint surface reduction and integration and zeugo- and autopodial elements into a single unit—based on observation of Dakosaurus maximus (Rygol specimen) and Geosaurus specimen photographs (WDC CSG-203), along with reconstructions in Caldwell (1997, 2002, both cited above) lead us to suspect a relatively greater independent mobility of digits in the hindlimbs, so we tentatively score the forelimbs as Strongly Flippered and the hindlimbs as ambiguous.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Listed as Geosaurus gracilis, but since reclassified as belonging to genus Rhacheosaurus (https://paleobiodb.org/classic/checkTaxonInfo?taxon_no=243936&is_real_user=1)

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Gauthier 1994 [https://doi.org/10.1017/S247526300000129X] and Simões et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-020-17190-9] place Choristodera as a basally branching lineage of stem archosaur.
Ecotype_references: Choristoderes were highly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle. Many but not all of them gave birth to live young (Ji et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-010-0654-2]; Jiang et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41559-023-02074-0]), and the necks of champsosaurs in particular were more limited in dorsiflexion than modern crocodilians, casting doubt on their ability to ambush terrestrial prey (Matsumoto et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1111/joa.13600]). However, it remains disputed whether they were capable on walking on land (see Ji et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-010-0654-2] and references therein). As a result, their current designation remains tentative. Choristoderes have only been found in freshwater paleoenvironments (Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]).
Limb_type_references: At least one specimen of the choristodere Monjurosuchus splendens preserve skin impressions that show complete, extensive pedal webbing and complete moderate-to-extensive manual webbing (Gao et al. 2000 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2000)020[0417:EFMOAS]2.0.CO;2]). However, to our knowledge, no known champsosaur specimens show similarly direct evidence of interdigital webbing. Based on exceptional soft-tissue preservation, webbing has been clearly identified in one species of choristodere (Monjurosuchus splendens [GMV 2135]: Gao et al. 2000 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2000)020[0417:EFMOAS]2.0.CO;2]), and suggested for Hyphalosaurus on the basis of scale morphology in PKUP V1052 (Gao and Ksepka 2008 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1469-7580.2008.00907.x]), though the latter claim has yet to be tested with other modalities of data. To our knowledge, interdigital webbing has not been directly demonstrated in any choristodere taxa except for Monjurosuchus, so the webbing phenotypes of individual species within Choristodera remain unclear.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Neenan et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms2633 ] and Simões et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-020-17190-9] recover a Mesozoic marine reptile superclade with the following topology: (Ichthyosauromorpha, (Thalattosauria, (("Saurosphargids" possibly polyphyletic), Sauropterygia))).
Ecotype_references: Although clearly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle (Romer 1956, pp. 344, 524 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Rieppel et al. 2005), most thalattosaurs seem to retain well-developed jointed limbs, suggesting that they may have retained the ability to make limited trips on land. The degree to which they traveled onto land remains unclear, so we cannot resolve whether thalattosaurs were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Because they lack obvious adaptations for a pelagic existence, most thalattosaurs are considered to have occupied shallower water basins (Müller 2005), but some thalattosaur genera seem more highly adapted to aquatic habits than others, so we consider their Ecotype2 score ambiguous. Thalattosaurs were marine, rather than freshwater (Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]).
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: Bever and Norell 2017: Morphological features, and the phylogenetic proximity of this group to the aquatic pleurosaurids, together suggest a semiaquatic lifestyle. While a Moderately Aquatic lifestyle seems most plausible, given the high degree of skeletal pedomorphosis in this taxon, we cannot rule out more Transiently Aquatic habits, so the ecotype of this taxon remains ambiguous. This aquatically adapted species was found in a marine deposit, and was probably residing in shallow near-shore marine waters.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Joyce et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1186/s13358-020-00211-x]  recover Thalassochelydia as a near-crown stem turtle lineage.
Ecotype_references: Like all thalassochelydian turtles, Eurysternum was highly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle (Anquetin et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.3374/014.058.0205]). However, it has an isotopic signature suggestive of brackish/fresh (rather than marine) aquatic habitat, which has led to the suggestion that they occupied more liminal aquatic environments (Anquetin et al. 2017). In the absence of additional data, we cannot resolve whether Eurysternum spp. were Moderately, Highly, or Near-Fully Aquatic.

Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered and demonstrably viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1986, p. 73 [Romer AS, Parsons TS (1986). The Vertebrate Body, Sixth Edition (First Edition 1949). CBS College Publishing: New York. 679 pp.]; Thewissen and Taylor 2007 [Thewiseen JGM, Taylor MA (2007). Aquatic Adaptations in the Limbs of Amniotes. Chapter 18, in: Hall BK (ed.), Fins into Limbs: Evolution, Development, and Transformation. The University of Chicago Press: Chicago. pp. 310–322]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines as “Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: All ichthyosaurs are commonly described as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (eg, Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773]; Motani 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2005)025[0462:DTMIAD]2.0.CO;2]; Maxwell 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jez.b.22459]; Gutarra and Rahman 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1111/brv.12790]); Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]. These flippers were highly integrated structures [Fernández et al. 2020 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2020.0281], and presumably encased in dense connective tissue like those of modern-day cetaceans (Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]), so that their digits were not independently mobile.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Caldwell 1997 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/4523834]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773];  Frey et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.18268/BSGM2017v69n1a5]; Vincent et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-017-1472-6]). Musculoskeletal reconstructions suggest that plesiosaurs could not move their digits independently (Krahl and Witzel 2021 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.12537]), so we score them as "Strongly Flippered".
Notes on occurrences: Specimen variably labeled Alzadasaurus kansasensis and Styxosaurus_snowii which are nomen dubium synonyms for Elasmosaurus ischiadicus. The type specimen of A. Kansasensis is from the Early//Lower Campanian (https://paleobiodb.org/classic/displayCollResults?collection_no=118875), so that was chosen as the age range for this species.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Distalmost phalanges restored/drawn on but I trust it. Not going to skew results. Length very predictable. Transverse split in cast through acropod region between two phalanges was left out of measurements (I measured the acropod in separate sections before and after the split) to keep it from influencing the digit length meaasurement.

Limb_region_measurement_notes: Penultimate phalanx of digit 3 missing, so hand length was measured up to proximal-most point of ungual phalanx. Not sure if radius is complete or not. May well be,  but I measured to the lateral-most point on what was there was of the distal epiphysis, which if anything overestimated its length. Thus, if anything, error in this measurement will reduce AZR and not artificially support our hypothesis.

Limb_region_measurement_notes: I think it's a forelimb, but need check with Dan (send him picture). If it's a hindlimb, obviously I'll move the humerus measurements to the femur column, etc. Hand measurement is  a minimum value. It's unclear whether or not these are distalmost phalanges, but if they are not, then it only minimizes AZR, and thus does the opposite of artificially supporting our hypothesis.

Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered and demonstrably viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1986, p. 73 [Romer AS, Parsons TS (1986). The Vertebrate Body, Sixth Edition (First Edition 1949). CBS College Publishing: New York. 679 pp.]; Thewissen and Taylor 2007 [Thewiseen JGM, Taylor MA (2007). Aquatic Adaptations in the Limbs of Amniotes. Chapter 18, in: Hall BK (ed.), Fins into Limbs: Evolution, Development, and Transformation. The University of Chicago Press: Chicago. pp. 310–322]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines as “Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: All ichthyosaurs are commonly described as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (eg, Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773]; Motani 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2005)025[0462:DTMIAD]2.0.CO;2]; Maxwell 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jez.b.22459]; Gutarra and Rahman 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1111/brv.12790]); Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]. These flippers were highly integrated structures [Fernández et al. 2020 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2020.0281], and presumably encased in dense connective tissue like those of modern-day cetaceans (Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]), so that their digits were not independently mobile.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured ulna down to latmost point of what I interpreted as distal epiphysis. Wasn't same as latmost point. But if I was wrong aand latmost point overall waas distall epiphysis, measurement for ulna would have been even smaller.

Notes on occurrences: Many species were historically labeled "Ichthyosaurus", but today this genus only contains 4–5 valid species: I. communis, I. breviceps, I. conybeari, I. anningae, and perhaps I. intermedius (Lomax and Massare 2015 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2014.903260]). Only entries for these five species were considered in setting the occurrence range shown left.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Acropod measurement conservative. Very ambiguous distal phalanges may have been present, which would have increased my footproxy measurement to 51.38.

The University of Chicago Press: Chicago. pp. 310–322]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines as “Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: Mosasaurines had fully fledged flippers, as supported by both osteological evidence and exceptional soft-tissue preservation (eg, Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773]; Lindgren et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms3423]), but the independent mobility of the individual digits within each flipper (and thus whether they should be "strongly flippered" or "weakly flippered") remains uncertain and may have varied among species.
Notes on occurrences: Only those entries for Platecarpus tympaniticus were used to determine this range.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: This fore-flipper cast is mislabeled in the collections as either Lestosaurus simus or Platecarpus ictericus, but correctly labeled in the YPM Collections Database as Platecarpus tympaniticus. If this specimen was re-articulated by YPM staff, it shouldn’t affect measurements because my measurements didn’t factor in spaces between limb regions, and phalanges were closely spaced together.

Ecotype_references: Teleosaurus is typically considered a semi-aquatic or semi-terrestrial taxon, given that it retains a dorsal raiment of thermoregulatory osteoderms, which indicate a continued reliance on basking, and lacks obvious morphological adaptations for a pelagic lifestyle (Foffa et al. 2019; Johnson et al. 2020; Johnson et al. 2022, Data S2). However, whether this semi-aquatic taxon was Moderately or Highly Aquatic remains unclear. Because it was not specialized for an aquatic lifestyle, we tentatively assume it was not traveling great distances over sea or occupying fast-moving oceanic waters. Teleosaurus did not live in freshwater habitats (Johnson et al. 2022). However, the reassignment of T. longipes to Steneosaurus bollensis further complicates the ecotype score for this taxon, so its ecotype remains ambiguous.
Limb_type_references: NA
Notes on occurrences: Specimen listed as "Teleosaurus longipes", which was synonymized with Steneosaurus bollensis. The age range given here is for the correct taxon name.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Cast in slab ("measurements match cast of imperial cabinet of Vienna specimen"). Unable to measure femur because proximal epiphysis covered by scute.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Due to the ongoing uncertainty of this taxon's placement (see Evans 1988 [Evans SE (1988). The Upper Permian reptile Adelosaurus from Durham. Palaeontology 31: 957–964.]), we treat it as Diapsida_indet, in a polytomy at the base of Diapsida.
Ecotype_references: Evans 1988 [Evans, S. E. 1988. The Upper Permian reptile Adelosaurus from Durham. Palaeontology 31(4), 957–964.]: The limited existing Adelosaurus material has different features suggestive of terrestrial and aquatic habits, though its overall morphology makes a terrestrial lifestyle more likely. However, in the absence of more complete material and data, we cannot rule out a Transiently or even Moderately Aquatic lifestyle for this taxon. As a result, its ecotype remains unclear.

Ecotype_references: All chelonioids are highly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle, and spend the vast majority of their lives in the water, but nevertheless spend an extended period of time on land at least once each breeding season to nest on shorelines, over which they may travel great distances (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 531, Cheloniidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). As a result, we consider them "Highly" rather than "Fully" aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All crown chelonioids (“sea turtles”) were scored as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs, following Wyneken 2001 [Wyneken J (2001). The anatomy of sea turtles. Seattle, WA: U.S. Department of Commerce NOAA Technical Memorandum NMFS-SEFSC-470.]. This was supported by direct observation of fluid-preserved chelonioids (Caretta caretta: YPM_HERR_002957, Chelonia mydas: YPM_HERR_018243).
Included_unguals?: Digit-dependent (mD1 and pD1 are clawed; other digits lack claws) [SOURCE: Sea Turtle Conservancy species pages: https://conserveturtles.org/information-about-sea-turtles-an-introduction/]
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Zeugopod length estimated using radius alone. mD1 clawed, other manual digits unclawed.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Pritchard and Nesbitt 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsos.170499], Pritchard and Sues 2019 (J. Systematic Palaeontology), and Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.] place Claudiosaurus within Neodiapsida crownward of Weigeltisauridae.
Ecotype_references: Morphological features suggest that Claudiosaurus had an aquatic lifestyle (Carroll 1981 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1981.0079]; Sues 2019, p. 41 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]). However, it retains well-developed jointed appendages, and it remains unclear whether and to what extent it traversed on land. As a result, we cannot resolve whether this taxon was Moderately or Highly Aquatic. Even so, it is thought to have resided in near-shore environments without adaptations to a pelagic lifestyle (Carroll 1981 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1981.0079]; Houssaye 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1749-4877.2009.00146.x]).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: The phylogenetic placement and monophyly of parareptiles have recently been disputed (see Ford and Benson 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41559-019-1047-3]; Simões et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.abq1898]: Science Advances). Our placement of Mesosaurs and other parareptiles within Pan-Reptilia varies in our different supertrees to reflect existing disagreements (see Methods and Supplementary Text). Piñeiro et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.4072/rbp.2021.3.04] clarify that Mesosaurus tenuidens is the only valid taxon of mesosaurid currently known, so we have renamed all the mesosaurid samples in our data set accordingly.
Ecotype_references: Although mesosaurs were viviparous (Piñeiro et al. 2012 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1080/08912963.2012.662230]) and clearly adapted for aquatic life in marine systems (Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Demarco et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.3389/fevo.2018.00109]), aspects of their vertebral morphometry have alluded to a more semi-aquatic lifestyle, with considerable time spent both in water and on land (Demarco et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.3389/fevo.2018.00109]; Demarco et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.3389/frym.2019.00039]). Adult mesosaurs may have occupied pelagic environments (Holmes 1953 [https://doi.org/10.1038/171669a0]; Verriere and Frobisch 2022 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.13866]), but we lack direct evidence concerning whether and to what extent they actually traversed on land, so it remains unclear whether mesosaurs were Moderately, Highly, or Fully Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All mesosaurids were scored as having extensive, complete pedal webbing, based on exceptional soft-tissues preserved on the hindlimb skeletons of the following mesosaurid specimens: SMNK_PAL_3806, SMF_R_4710, BSPG_1964_I_268, and URCR_064. Scorings were based on high-resolution photographs of the specimens, provided by Antoine Vérriere. Less soft-tissue preservation was discernible on forelimbs, but manual webbing phenotype was tentatively scored based on specimen NHMW_1987_0067_0001, which appears to have complete extensive webbing. Because the taxonomic breadth covered by these specimens remains unclear, the application of these scores to all sampled mesosaurids remains a tentative first approximation, as mesosaurids may have more variability in webbing phenotype than we can currently discern based on available specimens.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Piñeiro et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.4072/rbp.2021.3.04] clarify that Mesosaurus tenuidens is the only valid taxon of mesosaurid currently known, so we have renamed all the mesosaurid samples in our data set accordingly. This specimen is catalogued in the collections as “Mesosaurus tenuidens”.
proxp_measurement_notes: Proximal phalanx measurements very feasible but skipped to avoid adding 2D phalanx measurements into the dataset. However, if necessary, these could be added and would be reliable.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: De Braga and Reisz 1995 [de Braga M, Reisz RR (1995). A new diapsid reptile from the uppermost Carboniferous (Stephanian) of Kansas. Palaeontology 38: 199–212.] place this species within Araeoscelidia, but it has not been scored in a phylogenetic analysis since, so its placement remains dubious.
Ecotype_references: Although Spinoaequalis appears adapted for aquatic life (De Braga and Reisz 1995  [de Braga M, Reisz RR (1995). A new diapsid reptile from the uppermost Carboniferous (Stephanian) of Kansas. Palaeontology 38: 199–212.] ), it remains unclear whether and to what extent it traversed on land. The high degree of articulation of this fragile specimen and the high sedimentation rate of the saline paleoenvironment in which it resided suggest that this species lived in a near-shore relatively low-energy marine or brackish environment (De Braga and Reisz 1995  [de Braga M, Reisz RR (1995). A new diapsid reptile from the uppermost Carboniferous (Stephanian) of Kansas. Palaeontology 38: 199–212.] ).

Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: Cuora galbinifrons is a predominantly terrestrial high-elevation species that eats terrestrial vegetation and invertebrates, and is not typically associated with streams; though individuals of this species seldom enter the water (Xiao et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1139/cjz-2016-0218]), they are known to be able swimmers and will readily take to the water in captivity (Li et al. 2020 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2020-2.RLTS.T97357437A123816666.en]). As a result, we tentatively call this species "Transiently Aquatic", but additional observations of their habits in the wild are required to determine whether it would be best described as "Transiently Aquatic" or "Terrestrial".
Limb_type_references: Cuora spp. scored from fluid-preserved Cuora flavomarginata specimen YPM_HERR_011922: complete extensive webbing on all digits
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: Though the natural history of Leucocephalon yuwonoi is poorly understand (Ives et al. 2008 [https://doi.org/10.2744/CCB-0718.1]), it is often observed both in and out of water (Stanford and Hamidy 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021- 1.RLTS.T40761A2933851.en]), and is typically found in or near streams, creeks, and other shallow aquatic habitats. Its common appearance both in and out of water lead us to tentatively score it as “Moderately Aquatic” in the absence of more precise information.
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved specimen YPM_HERR_014520 suggests that this species has complete, slight webbing on both hands and feet, with some pedal snowshoeing.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.

Ecotype_references: All chelonioids are highly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle, and spend the vast majority of their lives in the water, but nevertheless spend an extended period of time on land at least once each breeding season to nest on shorelines, over which they may travel great distances (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 531, Cheloniidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). As a result, we consider them "Highly" rather than "Fully" aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All crown chelonioids (“sea turtles”) were scored as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs, following Wyneken 2001 [Wyneken J (2001). The anatomy of sea turtles. Seattle, WA: U.S. Department of Commerce NOAA Technical Memorandum NMFS-SEFSC-470.]. This was supported by direct observation of fluid-preserved chelonioids (Caretta caretta: YPM_HERR_002957, Chelonia mydas: YPM_HERR_018243).
Citation for occurrences: Zug et al. 2001 [https://doi.org/10.5479/si.00810266.90.1]; Dowsett et al. 2021
Notes on occurrences: Extant. Although no fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database, specimens likely referable to Lepidochelys kempii have been identified from the Yorktown Formation (Zug et al. 2001 [https://doi.org/10.5479/si.00810266.90.1]), which recent stratigraphic work dates to the mid-Piacenzian Warm Period (3.264 to 3.025 Ma) of the Pliocene.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Acropod measurements taken on ImageJ because original measurements excluded D3 unguals even though they're unclawed.

Ecotype_references: Although this species spends much of its time in water (van Dijk 2011 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2011-1.RLTS.T163467A5608383.en]), neonates may spend their first winter hibernating in burrows on land (Packard and Packard 1993 [https://doi.org/10.1007/BF00263600]), and adults may engage in extensive non-nesting terrestrial activity (see Bowne 2008 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/25140779]).
Limb_type_references: Research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/149628943; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/149669713) show that this species has complete, extensive webbing on hands and feet, with some pedal snowshoeing.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: Testudinids are typically described as being fully terrestrial, and (with the possible exception of species in the genus Manouria), we find no written evidence for even transiently aquatic habits in the clade Testudinidae (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 537: Testudinidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Natchev et al. 2015 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.1172]; Young et al. 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2016.0732]).
Limb_type_references: Testudinoids scored based on taxidermied adult Geochelone gigantea specimen YPM_HERR_016084, fluid-preserved testudinid specimens (Geochelone carbonaria: YPM_HERR_005961; Gopherus polyphemus: YPM_HERR_007115) and CT scan of fluid-preserved Psammobates tentorius specimen MVZ Herp_69192. Testudinoids have stiff connective tissue encasing each set of manual and pedal digits in an immobile extended stub-like palm, with only the ungual claws protruding.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: Mali et al. 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1643/CH-15-367] report significant overland interpond travel in this species. Ryan et al. 2008 [http://doi.org/10.1007/s11252-008-0049-8] report that this species spends extensive time basking and nesting on land, though it spends the majority of its time in the water. They usually reside in ponds and other small or slow-moving bodies of freshwater (van Dijk et al. 2011 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2011- 1.RLTS.T22028A9347395.en]

Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Joyce and Gauthier 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2003.2523]).
Limb_type_references: YPM_HERR_005934; online clips https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5dccCfUGXpM and https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4hYp55iG6x0: Soft-tissue paddle encompasses zuegopodium but individual digits are distinguishable in ventral view and independently mobile.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: Pelodiscus sinensis is highly aquatic, spending the majority of its time in low-energy freshwater systems, and typically coming onto land only to bask or breed (see Joyce and Gauthier 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2003.2523]; Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 531, Trionychinae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Kong et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.2744/CCB-1402.1]).
IUCN_source: Asian Turtle Trade Working Group 2000 (Pelodiscus sinensis: http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2000.RLTS.T39620A10251914.en)
Limb_type_references: Research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/151049242; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/148161836; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/147255648; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/147177098) and morphology research (Delfino et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s13127-010-0019-x]) show that this species has soft-tissue paddles encompassing the zeugopodium. Videos online (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XJsPQG1iv14) show that this species has digits that are individually distinguishable and independently mobile.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: This species routinely basks and nests on land, and may travel several hundred meters overland to reach the nesting site, but it spends the vast majority of its time in the water; although it is reported to sometimes inhabit fast-moving rivers, Apalone spinifera seems to predominantly occupy shallower, more slow-moving waters (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 531: Trionychinae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Joyce and Gauthier 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2003.2523]; van Dijk  2011 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2011-1.RLTS.T163451A5607536.en]).

Online clips (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=thbo70OUW74; https://www.youtube.com/shorts/L8YFNAd1gCI) show that its digits are independently mobile and distinguishable.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: The pes is called "paddle-like" by Sheil 2003.

Ecotype_references: Pelusios species are primarily bottom-walkers in slow-moving freshwater bodies, and seem to spend the majority of their time submerged, only coming onto land to bask or nest (Kuchling and Garcia 2003 [https://doi.org/10.3854/crm.6]; Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 527, Pelomedusidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Luiselli et al. 2019, IUCN Red List Assessment of Pelusios Niger [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2019-1.RLTS.T163440A1008712.en]).
Limb_type_references: Research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/67621539; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/93030296) suggest the presence of complete, extensive webbing on both hands and feet.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.

Ecotype_references: Except for during nesting periods, this species seldom leaves the water, and typically resides in large, high-energy river systems >2 m deep (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 528, Chelidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Ecotype_references: Sung et al. 2016, pp. 9–10. This species is usually found within streams, but is also often found on land, and the extent of its terrestrial forays is likely underestimated (Sung et al. 2016).

Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). As a result, we consider this species Highly Aquatic.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: Green iguanas spend most of their time on land, but retreat into the water for great stretches of time when threatened; however, they have also been observed entering the water without provocation and may spend considerable time there, even occassionally  engaging in intertidal foraging behavior (Censky et al. 1998 [https://doi.org/10.1038/26886]; Walter and Smith  2004 [Walter Jr E, Smith HT (2004). Colonization success by green iguanas in Florida. Iguana 11: 154–161.]; Krysko et al. 2007 [Krysko KL, Enge KM, Donlan EM, Seitz JC, Golden EA (2007). Distribution, natural history, and impacts of the introduced green iguana (Iguana iguana) in Florida. Iguana 14:142–151.]; Furness 2021 [https://doi.org/10.17161/randa.v28i3.15892]). For these reasons, they are Transiently to Moderately Aquatic. The frequency and duration of this behaviors leads us to tentatively call the species "Transiently Aquatic", though a Moderately Aquatic designation may be more appropriate for certain populations.
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_12217
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: Anolis equestris is highly arboreal and retreats into canopies when startled, generally avoiding the ground (Meshaka 1993 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/24320560]; Fong 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-3.RLTS.T203882A2771981.en]). We find no reference to aquatic habits in this species.
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved Anolis equestris specimen YPM_HERR_007028: This species lacks true interdigital webbing, but has lobe-like toe pads, which we tentatively score as lobate webbing (although these less clearly reach to the base of the digits and it remains unclear whether they're formed by the same developmental program as lobate webbing).
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: Basiliscus lizards forage along the borders of streams, typically reside near water, and (as juveniles) often run on the surface of water as a means of escape when startled; however, they also consume extensive terrestrial food and occasionally travel greater distances from water (Laerm 1973 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/2424037]; Vaughan et al. 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1163/157075407781268327]; Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 590, Corytophanidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). We find no reports of hunting while submerged for this species, and Vaughan et al. 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1163/157075407781268327] report few observations of Basiliscus individuals in the water unless startled. As a result, we default to calling species in this near-water genus “Transiently Aquatic”, though a "Moderately Aquatic" designation may be more appropriate if they enter the water more frequently.
Limb_type_references: Lateral toe fringes are present on this species (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/151862763) but it lacks manual interdigital tissue (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/151862763; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/151538923).
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: This species is predominantly terrestrial (Wilms et al. 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-2.RLTS.T172799A1374221.en], and apparently only enter the water by accident during the rainy season, from which they struggle to escape (Azeez and Oyewale 2010 (Azeez OI, Oyewale JO (2010). Changes in osmotic fragility of erythrocytes of the rainbow lizard (Agama agama) resulting from swimming exercise. Russian Journal of Herpetology 17:185-188.]). Assuming that they do only fall into the water by mistake and avoid entering the water whenever possible, we consider this species Terrestrial.
Limb_type_references: Research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/149482176; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/148622979; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/148359493) show that this species lacks interdigital webbing.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: A "Caliper1" measurement set, originally taken 7/12/21,  replaced by remeasurements on 10/28/21.
Ungual_measurement_notes: Presence of keratinous sheath(s) prevented accurate ungual bone measurements.

Ecotype_references: Lophosaurus dilophus is a predominantly arboreal species that consumes terrestrial insects and vegetation; we find no reference to aquatic habits (Oliver et al. 2018 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018-1.RLTS.T22528853A125055879.en]).
Limb_type_references: Research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/132146659; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/72872876; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/40244233) show that this species lacks interdigital webbing. Very slight digital scale fringing is apparent, but too slight to count as truly interdigital integument.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Digit 3 didn't capture full length of foot (digit 4 much longer)

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: These lizards often use water as a means of escape, and have clear morphological adaptations to suit this purpose (Bauer and Jackman 2007 [Bauer AM, Jackman T (2007). Global diversity of lizards in freshwater (Reptilia: Lacertilia). In: Balian, E.V., Lévêque, C., Segers, H., Martens, K. (eds) Freshwater Animal Diversity Assessment. Developments in Hydrobiology, vol 198. Springer: Dordrecht. pp. 581–586. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-4020-8259-7_55.]). However, while individuals have been observed residing in a river, they are most often observed and mainly reside on land (Denzer et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.11646/zootaxa.4747.2.3]; O'Shea et al. 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-3.RLTS.T195296A2376976.en]). As a result, we treat them as “Transiently Aquatic”. Reasonably, however, Watkins-Colwell 1994 [Watkins-Colwell G (1994). Cladistic biogeography of the water dragons, Hydrosaurus and Physignathus (Lepidosauria: Agamidae). Master of Science Thesis, Department of Biological Sciences, Ohio University. Bell & Howell Information and Learning Company: Ann Arbor. 68 pp.]  Learning Company: Ann Arbor. 68 pp.] calls these lizards “routinely semiaquatic” (p. 24), and a “Moderately Aquatic” designation may be preferred depending on the frequency of their submerged activity. In the absence of additional behavioral data, we assume recurring but typically short forays into the water for this species, which then tentatively merits a “Transiently Aquatic” designation.
IUCN_source: Ledesma et al. 2022 (Hydrosaurus pustulatus [Ledesma M, Brown R, Sy E, Rico E, Binaday J, Lagat R, Pitogo K, Santos G, de la Cruz C, Lorenzo A, Sarmiento M, Maglangit EP (2022). Hydrosaurus pustulatus. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2022: e.T190349178A180276751. Accessed on 14 June 2024.])
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved H. pustulatus specimen YPM_HERR_012716 and H. amboinensis specimens YPM_HERR_012886 and YPM_HERR_010046 show that hands and feet both possess laterally projecting scale fringes, which are far more prominent on the feet. We tentatively score both fore- and hindlimbs as having interdigital integument, though developmental work is required to confidently distinguish the pedal fringes from lobate webbing.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: This species is a terrestrial sand-burrowing specialist; we find no reference to aquatic habits (Els et al. 2012 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2022- 1.RLTS.T164651A217762229.en]).
Limb_type_references: Research-grade iNaturalist observations suggest that this species lacks interdigital webbing but has scaly finger and toe fringes (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/145256454; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/122465909; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/32722135; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/1311413).
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No taxon page on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: Although V. salvadorii is predominantly terrestrial (Shea et al. 2016 [V. salvaroii]), individuals make ready use of water in captivity, and often enter large water bodies within their enclosure when hot or when threatened by conspecifics (Reh et al. 2022 [V. salvadorii]). It is thought to reside along streams in the wild, but the extent to which it submerges in the wild (if it does at all) is unknwon (Reh et al. 2022 [V. salvadorii]). Given that, in captivity, this animal  readily takes to water but spends the overwhelming majority of its time on land, we tentatively score this species as Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Varanus spp. were scored from fluid-preserved YPM VZ specimens (V. beccarii: YPM_HERR_010047; V. exanthematicus: YPM_HERR_010408; V. griseus: YPM_HERR_003865; Varanus dumerilii: YPM_HERR_010122; V. salvator: YPM_HERR_009466). All observed Varanus specimens appear to lack interdigital webbing.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA
Ungual_measurement_notes: Presence of keratinous sheath(s) prevented accurate ungual bone measurements.

Ecotype_references: Although the common blue-tongued skink is predominantly terrestrial and tends to avoid water, it occupies a range of habitats across its native range (Shea 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2017- 3.RLTS.T109481538A109481555.en]), and Tiliqua species have been reported to occasionally flee into the water or swim across large (several-hundred-meter) bodies of water when necessary (Shea 1992 [https://hdl.handle.net/2123/27611.2]). As a result, we consider all Tiliqua species Transiently Aquatic.

Ecotype_references: Although it spends a lot of its time on land, P. lesueurii regularly traverses and feeds within the water (Ringma and Salisbury 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1670/12-041]; Tallowin et al. 2018 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018-1.RLTS.T22566194A22566245.en]). As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved Physignathus lesuerii specimen YPM_HERR_012224 and research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152202358; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152185003; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152012751) show that this species lacks interdigital webbing but has a short, slight row of fringed scales on pD4 and pD5. This toe-fringing morphology is noted by Watkins-Colwell 1994 [Watkins-Colwell G (1994). Cladistic biogeography of the water dragons, Hydrosaurus and Physignathus (Lepidosauria: Agamidae). Master of Science Thesis, Department of Biological Sciences, Ohio University. Bell & Howell Information and Learning Company: Ann Arbor. 68 pp.]  as more minimal than that seen in Hydrosaurus spp., but not identified by Ringma and Salisbury (2014) as an aquatic adaptation. We treat it here as a very minor form of interdigital integument. We find no such interdigital fringing on the hands.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Pyron et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2148-13-93]: Tupinambis is paraphyletic when it excludes Dracaena.
Ecotype_references: Tegus are predominantly terrestrial in the wild (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, pp. 575–576, Tupinambinae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]), where their range is often restricted by water (Betancourt et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1111/ahe.12786]). However, Tupinambis teguixin is apparently a good swimmer and will readily take to the water and swim in captivity (Hall 1978 [Hall BJ (1978). Notes on the husbandry, behaviour and breeding of captive tegu lizards Tupinambis teguixin. International Zoo Yearbook 18: 91–95]). Other tegu species are known to readily swim in the wild as well (Pernas et al. 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1656/058.011.0414]; Abrahão et al. 2019 [in Veitsch et al: https://doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.CH.2019.SSC-OP.62.en]). Given this tendency, we tentatively call tegus "Transiently Aquatic", though we caveat this designation by noting the dearth of Tupinambis teguixin swimming observations in the wild.

Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Nguyen et al. 2022 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2022-1.RLTS.T57287221A217811129.en]), so we consider it Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Existing iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/39531-Shinisaurus-crocodilurus; https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/39531-Shinisaurus-crocodilurus/browse_photos) show that this species lacks interdigital webbing.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). We find no reported evidence of aquatic habits in this species (Wilms et al. 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021- 1.RLTS.T198537A2531561.en]).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Schools et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2022.107577] recover Caribicus as sister to (Celestus+Panolopus+Comptus). Ecotype_references: Caribicus/Celestus warreni is a burrowing, ground-dwelling species; with no reference to aquatic habits (Landestoy et al. 2015 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016- 3.RLTS.T39260A71739879.en]; Schools et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2022.107577]).
Limb_type_references: Research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/104178436; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/102766311) suggest that this species lacks interdigital webbing.

Ecotype_references: The majority of gekkonid species typically dwell on rocks, in trees, or on/under terrestrial substrates (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 562, Gekkonidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). Researchers have reported a transiently aquatic lifestyle in just one gekkonid species: Hemidactylus platyurus, which has been observed to commonly employ a unique swimming gait to travel between trees (Nirody et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cub.2018.10.064]). To our knowledge, no comparable forays into water have been described for Gekko siamensis in the wild. Zaaf and Van Damme 2001 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s004350100044] describe Gekko gecko individuals as tree/rock climbers within varied terrestrial habitats.
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved Gekko gecko specimens YPM_HERR_016062, 14441, and 14564 have lobate webbing on all digits. Research-grade iNaturalist observations of Gekko siamensis (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/140074427; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/139388492; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/139096447) support the presence of similar lobate webbing in G. siamensis.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.

Ecotype_references: Chameleons are collectively described as occupying terrestrial habitats, with no reference to semi-aquatic habits (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, pp. 583–584: Chamaeleonidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). To our knowledge, there are no known extant chameleon species with even transiently semi-aquatic lifestyles. Oceanic dispersal from Madagascar to mainland Africa has been suggested for some species (Raxworthy et al. 2002 [https://doi.org/10.1038/415784a]), but this was presumably accomplished by floating on vegetation rafts, rather than swimming.
Limb_type_references: Chameleons have stiff syndactylous finger pairs / triads (see Molnar et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jmor.20708]). We verified the presence of these "pincer-palms" with several fluid-preserved chameleon specimens (Bradypodion fischeri: YPM_HERR_009976, Brookesia perarmata: YPM_HERR_011451, Calumma brevicornis: YPM_HERR_009788, Chamaeleo deremensis: YPM R 10314).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: Anolis sagrei is a predominantly terrestrial species (Lee et al. 2020 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2020-3.RLTS.T197443A2484119.en]). Although, to our knowledge, Anolis sagrei has never been observed swimming in the wild, its typical habitat and wide geographic range make it likely for the species to encounter water bodies, and it has been shown to run with a quadrupedal gait over water in experimental settings (Cheu et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1093/icb/icac031]). Because this behavior has never been observed in the wild, we tentatively call Anolis sagrei "Terrestrial", but if this water-running behavior is one day observed in the wild, we would revise its designation to "Transiently Aquatic".
Limb_type_references: In-person observation of brown anoles in Fort Lauderdale (FL, USA) and research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152206098; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152205488; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152202793; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152188317; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152186655; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152171208; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152140651) show that this species lacks interdigital webbing. While there is very slight lobing about the penultimate phalanges and unguals, it is too minimal for us to count it as lobate webbing.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Specimen listed as “Norops sagrei”, a synonym for “Anolis sagrei”.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: Although it spends a lot of its time on land, P. lesueurii regularly traverses and feeds within the water (Ringma and Salisbury 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1670/12-041]; Tallowin et al. 2018 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018-1.RLTS.T22566194A22566245.en]). As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved Physignathus lesuerii specimen YPM_HERR_012224 and research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152202358; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152185003; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152012751) show that this species lacks interdigital webbing but has a short, slight row of fringed scales on pD4 and pD5. This toe-fringing morphology is noted by Watkins-Colwell 1994 [Watkins-Colwell G (1994). Cladistic biogeography of the water dragons, Hydrosaurus and Physignathus (Lepidosauria: Agamidae). Master of Science Thesis, Department of Biological Sciences, Ohio University. Bell & Howell Information and Learning Company: Ann Arbor. 68 pp.]  as more minimal than that seen in Hydrosaurus spp., but not identified by Ringma and Salisbury (2014) as an aquatic adaptation. We treat it here as a very minor form of interdigital integument. We find no such interdigital fringing on the hands.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: These lizards often use water as a means of escape, and have clear morphological adaptations to suit this purpose (Bauer and Jackman 2007 [Bauer AM, Jackman T (2007). Global diversity of lizards in freshwater (Reptilia: Lacertilia). In: Balian, E.V., Lévêque, C., Segers, H., Martens, K. (eds) Freshwater Animal Diversity Assessment. Developments in Hydrobiology, vol 198. Springer: Dordrecht. pp. 581–586. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-4020-8259-7_55.]). However, while individuals have been observed residing in a river, they are most often observed and mainly reside on land (Denzer et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.11646/zootaxa.4747.2.3]; O’Shea et al. 2021 IUCN assessment for Hydrosaurus amboinensis). As a result, we treat them as “Transiently Aquatic”. Reasonably, however, Watkins-Colwell 1994 [Watkins-Colwell G (1994). Cladistic biogeography of the water dragons, Hydrosaurus and Physignathus (Lepidosauria: Agamidae). Master of Science Thesis, Department of Biological Sciences, Ohio University. Bell & Howell Information and Learning Company: Ann Arbor. 68 pp.]  calls these lizards “routinely semiaquatic” (p. 24), and a “Moderately Aquatic” designation may be preferred depending on the frequency of their submerged activity. In the absence of additional behavioral data, we assume recurring but typically short forays into the water for this species, which then tentatively merits a “Transiently Aquatic” designation.
IUCN_source: Ledesma et al. 2022 (Hydrosaurus pustulatus [Ledesma M, Brown R, Sy E, Rico E, Binaday J, Lagat R, Pitogo K, Santos G, de la Cruz C, Lorenzo A, Sarmiento M, Maglangit EP (2022). Hydrosaurus pustulatus. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2022: e.T190349178A180276751. Accessed on 14 June 2024.])
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved H. pustulatus specimen YPM_HERR_012716 and H. amboinensis specimens YPM_HERR_012886 and YPM_HERR_010046 show that hands and feet both possess laterally projecting scale fringes, which are far more prominent on the feet. We tentatively score both fore- and hindlimbs as having interdigital integument, though developmental work is required to confidently distinguish the pedal fringes from lobate webbing.

Ecology of frillneck lizards, Chlamydosaurus-Kingii (Agamidae), in Tropical Australia. Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: Basiliscus lizards forage along the borders of streams, typically reside near water, and (as juveniles) often run on the surface of water as a means of escape when startled; however, they also consume extensive terrestrial food and occasionally travel greater distances from water (Laerm 1973 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/2424037]; Vaughan et al. 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1163/157075407781268327]; Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 590, Corytophanidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). We find no reports of hunting while submerged for this species, and Vaughan et al. 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1163/157075407781268327] report few observations of Basiliscus individuals in the water unless startled. As a result, we default to calling species in this near-water genus “Transiently Aquatic”. B. vittatus is sometimes described as less aquatic than B. plumifrons, but both species reside near water and juveniles of both species use water as an escape medium (Laerm 1973 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/2424037]).
Limb_type_references: Research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152101075; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/151842214; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/151465482) suggest that this species has the same webbing phenotype as that of B. plumifrons, with slight interdigital integument on the feet but none on the hands.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Digit III didn't capture full foot length (digit IV was much longer).

Ecotype_references: Although the green tree monitor is primarily terrestrial (Shea et al 2018 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018- 2.RLTS.T42485751A101752115.en]), it is an excellent swimmer (Baldwin 2006 [Baldwin B (2006). Successful care and reproduction of green tree monitors (Varanus prasinus) at the San Diego Zoo. Iguana 13: 283–287.]), which is thought to occasionally swim or wade in the water in the wild (Shuter 2014 [Shuter AD (2014). A novel underwater foraging behavior observed in Varanus prasinus at the Wildlife Conservation Society’s Bronx Zoo. Biawak 8: 61–63.]), and one individual has even once been observed foraging underwater in captivity (Shuter 2014 [Shuter AD (2014). A novel underwater foraging behavior observed in Varanus prasinus at the Wildlife Conservation Society’s Bronx Zoo. Biawak 8: 61–63.]). Assuming this is a very rare occurrence, but that very occasional swimming is typical for the species, we consider this taxon Transiently Aquatic.
