Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Neenan et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms2633 ] and Simões et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-020-17190-9] recover a Mesozoic marine reptile superclade with the following topology: (Ichthyosauromorpha, (Thalattosauria, (("Saurosphargids" possibly polyphyletic), Sauropterygia))).
Ecotype_references: Although clearly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle (Romer 1956, pp. 344, 524 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Rieppel et al. 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1139/E05-076]), most thalattosaurs seem to retain well-developed jointed limbs, suggesting that they may have retained the ability to make limited trips on land. The degree to which they traveled onto land remains unclear, so we cannot resolve whether thalattosaurs were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Because they lack obvious adaptations for a pelagic existence, most thalattosaurs are considered to have occupied shallower water basins (Müller 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1139/E05-030]), but some thalattosaur genera seem more highly adapted to aquatic habits than others, so we consider their Ecotype2 score ambiguous. Thalattosaurs were marine, rather than freshwater (Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]).
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Cast

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: Green iguanas spend most of their time on land, but retreat into the water for great stretches of time when threatened; however, they have also been observed entering the water without provocation and may spend considerable time there, even occassionally  engaging in intertidal foraging behavior (Censky et al. 1998 [https://doi.org/10.1038/26886]; Walter and Smith  2004 [Walter Jr E, Smith HT (2004). Colonization success by green iguanas in Florida. Iguana 11: 154–161.]; Krysko et al. 2007 [Krysko KL, Enge KM, Donlan EM, Seitz JC, Golden EA (2007). Distribution, natural history, and impacts of the introduced green iguana (Iguana iguana) in Florida. Iguana 14:142–151.]; Furness 2021 [https://doi.org/10.17161/randa.v28i3.15892]). For these reasons, they are Transiently to Moderately Aquatic. The frequency and duration of this behaviors leads us to tentatively call the species "Transiently Aquatic", though a Moderately Aquatic designation may be more appropriate for certain populations.
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_12217
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Mounted in the teaching collection.
Ungual_measurement_notes: Presence of keratinous sheath(s) prevented accurate ungual bone measurements.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: Although komodo dragons are predominantly terrestrial (Ciofi et al. 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1643/0045-8511(2007)7[462:PAOHRS]2.0.CO;2]; Jessop et al. 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-2.RLTS.T22884A123633058.en]), they have been observed to wade through and cross larger water bodies (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=y33tYaoV5sk; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/44176726). As a result, we consider this species Transiently Aquatic. It is worth noting, however, that these online-posted observations have not (to our knowledge) been vetted by herpetologists, so the taxon's Transiently Aquatic designation remains dubious and requires verification.
Limb_type_references: Varanus spp. were scored from fluid-preserved YPM VZ specimens (V. beccarii: YPM_HERR_010047; V. exanthematicus: YPM_HERR_010408; V. griseus: YPM_HERR_003865; Varanus dumerilii: YPM_HERR_010122; V. salvator: YPM_HERR_009466). All observed Varanus specimens appear to lack interdigital webbing.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Mounted in the teaching collection.
Ungual_measurement_notes: Presence of keratinous sheath(s) prevented accurate ungual bone measurements.

Ecotype_references: All chelonioids are highly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle, and spend the vast majority of their lives in the water, but nevertheless spend an extended period of time on land at least once each breeding season to nest on shorelines, over which they may travel great distances (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 531, Cheloniidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). As a result, we consider them "Highly" rather than "Fully" aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All crown chelonioids (“sea turtles”) were scored as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs, following Wyneken 2001 [Wyneken J (2001). The anatomy of sea turtles. Seattle, WA: U.S. Department of Commerce NOAA Technical Memorandum NMFS-SEFSC-470.]. This was supported by direct observation of fluid-preserved chelonioids (Caretta caretta: YPM_HERR_002957, Chelonia mydas: YPM_HERR_018243).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Worked great! Leatherback flippers lack claws, so the unguals were included in all relevant measurements.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype_references: Tegus are predominantly terrestrial in the wild (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, pp. 575–576, Tupinambinae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]), where their range is often restricted by water (Betancourt et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1111/ahe.12786]). However, Tupinambis teguixin is apparently a good swimmer and will readily take to the water and swim in captivity (Hall 1978 [Hall BJ (1978). Notes on the husbandry, behaviour and breeding of captive tegu lizards Tupinambis teguixin. International Zoo Yearbook 18: 91–95]). Other tegu species are known to readily swim in the wild as well (Pernas et al. 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1656/058.011.0414]; Abrahão et al. 2019 [in Veitsch et al: https://doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.CH.2019.SSC-OP.62.en]). Given this tendency, we tentatively call tegus "Transiently Aquatic", though we caveat this designation by noting the dearth of Tupinambis teguixin swimming observations in the wild.
IUCN_source: Ines Hladki et al. 2015 (Tupinambis teguixin: https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021- 3.RLTS.T44579781A44579792.en)
Limb_type_references: Research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/107725956; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/112061515; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/139180428) show that this species lacks interdigital webbing.

Ecotype_references: This species routinely basks and nests on land, and may travel several hundred meters overland to reach the nesting site, but it spends the vast majority of its time in the water; although it is reported to sometimes inhabit fast-moving rivers, Apalone spinifera seems to predominantly occupy shallower, more slow-moving waters (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 531: Trionychinae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Joyce and Gauthier 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2003.2523]; van Dijk  2011 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2011-1.RLTS.T163451A5607536.en]).
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_10088 and research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/151938636; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/151314935) show that this species has soft-tissue paddles encompassing the zeugopodium. Online clips (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=thbo70OUW74; https://www.youtube.com/shorts/L8YFNAd1gCI) show that its digits are independently mobile and distinguishable.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype_references: Testudinids are typically described as being fully terrestrial, and (with the possible exception of species in the genus Manouria), we find no written evidence for even transiently aquatic habits in the clade Testudinidae (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 537: Testudinidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Natchev et al. 2015 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.1172]; Young et al. 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2016.0732]).
IUCN_source: Rhodin et al. 2010 [Rhodin AG, van Dijk PP, Iverson JB, Shaffer HB (2010). Turtles of the world, 2010 update: annotated checklist of taxonomy, synonymy, distribution, and conservation status. Chelonian Research Monographs 5: 000–085.]
Limb_type_references: Testudinoids scored based on taxidermied adult Geochelone gigantea specimen YPM_HERR_016084, fluid-preserved testudinid specimens (Geochelone carbonaria: YPM_HERR_005961; Gopherus polyphemus: YPM_HERR_007115) and CT scan of fluid-preserved Psammobates tentorius specimen MVZ Herp_69192. Testudinoids have stiff connective tissue encasing each set of manual and pedal digits in an immobile extended stub-like palm, with only the ungual claws protruding.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Hand length difficult to obtain, but with small margins of error ≤1 mm for each phalanx.
Ungual_measurement_notes: Presence of keratinous sheath(s) prevented accurate ungual bone measurements.

Ecotype_references: This species routinely basks and nests on land, and may travel several hundred meters overland to reach the nesting site, but it spends the vast majority of its time in the water; although it is reported to sometimes inhabit fast-moving rivers, Apalone spinifera seems to predominantly occupy shallower, more slow-moving waters (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 531: Trionychinae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Joyce and Gauthier 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2003.2523]; van Dijk  2011 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2011-1.RLTS.T163451A5607536.en]).
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_10088 and research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/151938636; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/151314935) show that this species has soft-tissue paddles encompassing the zeugopodium. Online clips (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=thbo70OUW74; https://www.youtube.com/shorts/L8YFNAd1gCI) show that its digits are independently mobile and distinguishable.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Humerus length ambiguous, within 2 mm margin of error depending on placement of proximalmost point.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Simões et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1186/s12915-020-00901-5] place Kallimodon pulchellus within Sphenodontia crownward of (Pleurosaurus+Palaeopleurosaurus). Bever and Norell 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsos.170570] recover Pleurosaurus, Vadasaurus, and Kallimodon as a monophyletic grouping.
Ecotype_references: Although this species occupied a terrestrial depositional environment (The Paleobiology Database: https://paleobiodb.org/classic/checkTaxonInfo?taxon_no=122301&is_real_user=1), a transiently aquatic lifestyle cannot be ruled out, so its precise ecotype remains ambiguous.
Limb_type_references: NA
Notes on occurrences: According to The Paleobiology Database, this taxon was reassigned to Leptosaurus pulchellus, so the range for that taxon is given here.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Cast was poor reslution but hand measurement repeated twice and within a half mm margin of error.

Ecotype_references: All chelonioids are highly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle, and spend the vast majority of their lives in the water, but nevertheless spend an extended period of time on land at least once each breeding season to nest on shorelines, over which they may travel great distances (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 531, Cheloniidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). As a result, we consider them "Highly" rather than "Fully" aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All crown chelonioids (“sea turtles”) were scored as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs, following Wyneken 2001 [Wyneken J (2001). The anatomy of sea turtles. Seattle, WA: U.S. Department of Commerce NOAA Technical Memorandum NMFS-SEFSC-470.]. This was supported by direct observation of fluid-preserved chelonioids (Caretta caretta: YPM_HERR_002957, Chelonia mydas: YPM_HERR_018243).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Humerus length was taken from proximal base of the anterior process. If taken from the proximalmost point of the articular surface, it would measure 74.87 mm. Hand length included the ungual since this is unclawed.

Ecotype_references: Chelydra resides on the bottoms of shallow freshwater basins and seldom bask out of the water, but will make extended overland journeys to find nesting sites (Obbard and Brooks 1980 [http://www.jstor.com/stable/3891480]; Whitfield and Luhring 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1016/B978-012370626-3.00197-6]; Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 534, Chelydridae  [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.])
Limb_type_references: Chelydra species scored as having complete, extensive webbing, based on stage 25 embryos figured in Yntema 1968 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jmor.1051250207], plate 10.
Included_unguals?: Digit-dependent (all digits except pD5 are clawed)
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Hindlimb measurements possible but not taken for sake of time.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Sander 1989 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1989.0103] was used for interrelations of Neusticosaurus species.
Ecotype_references: Although viviparity (which permits a Fully Aquatic lifestyle in many modern amniotes) has been demonstrated in at least one species of pachypleurosaurs (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), and suggested in some species of nothosauroids (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]), these eosauropterygians retain jointed limbs that may have been able to amble onto land for short periods—it remains unclear to what degree (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]). As a result, while they were clearly adapted to spend much of their time in the water, we cannot resolve whether they were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Pachypleurosaurs and nothosaurs are thought to have lived in shallow near-shore environments (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]).
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: Although viviparity (which permits a Fully Aquatic lifestyle in many modern amniotes) has been demonstrated in at least one species of pachypleurosaurs (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), and suggested in some species of nothosauroids (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]), these eosauropterygians retain jointed limbs that may have been able to amble onto land for short periods—it remains unclear to what degree (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]). As a result, while they were clearly adapted to spend much of their time in the water, we cannot resolve whether they were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Pachypleurosaurs are thought to have lived in shallow near-shore environments (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), but whether larger nothosaurs invaded larger or faster water bodies remains unclear.
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Left and right foot lengths slightly different, as both were measured and averaged. Unclear if unguals clawed, so they were excluded in acropodial measurements to be safe. Proximal epiphysis of radius was obscured, so forelimb zeugopod measurement is from ulna alone.
proxp_measurement_notes: Proximal phalanx measurements feasible but on a cast and should be treated with caution.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Neenan et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms2633 ] and Simões et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-020-17190-9] recover a Mesozoic marine reptile superclade with the following topology: (Ichthyosauromorpha, (Thalattosauria, (("Saurosphargids" possibly polyphyletic), Sauropterygia))).
Ecotype_references: Although clearly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle (Romer 1956, pp. 344, 524 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Rieppel et al. 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1139/E05-076]), most thalattosaurs seem to retain well-developed jointed limbs, suggesting that they may have retained the ability to make limited trips on land. The degree to which they traveled onto land remains unclear, so we cannot resolve whether thalattosaurs were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Because they lack obvious adaptations for a pelagic existence, most thalattosaurs are considered to have occupied shallower water basins (Müller 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1139/E05-030]), but some thalattosaur genera seem more highly adapted to aquatic habits than others, so we consider their Ecotype2 score ambiguous. Thalattosaurs were marine, rather than freshwater (Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]).
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Excluded unguals, given possibility that they're clawed.
Ungual_measurement_notes: Ungual phalanx measurements very feasible but skipped to avoid adding 2D phalanx measurements into the dataset. However, if necessary, these could be added and would be reliable.
proxp_measurement_notes: Proximal phalanx measurements very feasible but skipped to avoid adding 2D phalanx measurements into the dataset. However, if necessary, these could be added and would be reliable.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Pritchard and Nesbitt 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsos.170499], Pritchard and Sues 2019 (J. Systematic Palaeontology), and Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.] place Claudiosaurus within Neodiapsida crownward of Weigeltisauridae.
Ecotype_references: Morphological features suggest that Claudiosaurus had an aquatic lifestyle (Carroll 1981 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1981.0079]; Sues 2019, p. 41 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]). However, it retains well-developed jointed appendages, and it remains unclear whether and to what extent it traversed on land. As a result, we cannot resolve whether this taxon was Moderately or Highly Aquatic. Even so, it is thought to have resided in near-shore environments without adaptations to a pelagic lifestyle (Carroll 1981 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1981.0079]; Houssaye 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1749-4877.2009.00146.x]).
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Proximalmost point of humerus is dubious. Scalebar (3cm) taken from McMenamin et al. 2019, larger than I would expect).

Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered and demonstrably viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1986, p. 73 [Romer AS, Parsons TS (1986). The Vertebrate Body, Sixth Edition (First Edition 1949). CBS College Publishing: New York. 679 pp.]; Thewissen and Taylor 2007 [Thewiseen JGM, Taylor MA (2007). Aquatic Adaptations in the Limbs of Amniotes. Chapter 18, in: Hall BK (ed.), Fins into Limbs: Evolution, Development, and Transformation. The University of Chicago Press: Chicago. pp. 310–322]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines as “Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: All ichthyosaurs are commonly described as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (eg, Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773]; Motani 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2005)025[0462:DTMIAD]2.0.CO;2]; Maxwell 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jez.b.22459]; Gutarra and Rahman 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1111/brv.12790]); Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]. These flippers were highly integrated structures [Fernández et al. 2020 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2020.0281], and presumably encased in dense connective tissue like those of modern-day cetaceans (Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]), so that their digits were not independently mobile.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: No scalebar, so length measurement units are arbitrary, using ImageJ default (should have no effect on AZR estimates).

Ecotype_references: Although viviparity (which permits a Fully Aquatic lifestyle in many modern amniotes) has been demonstrated in at least one species of pachypleurosaurs (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), and suggested in some species of nothosauroids (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]), these eosauropterygians retain jointed limbs that may have been able to amble onto land for short periods—it remains unclear to what degree (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]). As a result, while they were clearly adapted to spend much of their time in the water, we cannot resolve whether they were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Pachypleurosaurs and nothosaurs are thought to have lived in shallow near-shore environments (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Simões et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1186/s12915-020-00901-5] and Bever and Norell 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsos.170570] were used for the interrelations of Rhynchocephalia.
Ecotype_references: Pleurosaurs are highly adapted for an aquatic lifestyle (Dupret 2004 [Dupret V (2004). The pleurosaurs: Anatomy and phylogeny. Revue de Paleobiologie vol. spéc. 9:61–80.]; Jones 2008; Bever and Norell 2017). The extreme reduction of the limbs and elongation of the body cast doubt on their ability to travel on land for extended periods. However, we have no data to resolve whether pleurosaurs were Highly or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic, so their ecotype remains ambiguous. Pleurosaurs are thought to have occupied shallower waters (see Bever and Norell 2017), so we tentatively score them as preferring small or slow-moving water bodies.

Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered and demonstrably viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1986, p. 73 [Romer AS, Parsons TS (1986). The Vertebrate Body, Sixth Edition (First Edition 1949). CBS College Publishing: New York. 679 pp.]; Thewissen and Taylor 2007 [Thewiseen JGM, Taylor MA (2007). Aquatic Adaptations in the Limbs of Amniotes. Chapter 18, in: Hall BK (ed.), Fins into Limbs: Evolution, Development, and Transformation. The University of Chicago Press: Chicago. pp. 310–322]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines as “Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: All ichthyosaurs are commonly described as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (eg, Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773]; Motani 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2005)025[0462:DTMIAD]2.0.CO;2]; Maxwell 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jez.b.22459]; Gutarra and Rahman 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1111/brv.12790]); Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]. These flippers were highly integrated structures [Fernández et al. 2020 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2020.0281], and presumably encased in dense connective tissue like those of modern-day cetaceans (Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]), so that their digits were not independently mobile.
Photo credit: Provided by Gunnar Riedel (Senckenberg Naturmuseum Frankfurt)
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Single point of disarticulation in ofrelimb autopod. Manual digit 3 was measured before and after this disarticulation and theen the two values added to give the final measurement. Hindlimb acropodium may be incomplete, so AZR_hindlimb likely underestimated. Points of disarticulation in forelimb acropodium made Acropod_LM measurements to dubious to take.

Ecotype_references: Pelusios species are primarily bottom-walkers in slow-moving freshwater bodies, and seem to spend the majority of their time submerged, only coming onto land to bask or nest (Kuchling and Garcia 2003 [https://doi.org/10.3854/crm.6]; Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 527, Pelomedusidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Luiselli et al. 2019, IUCN Red List Assessment of Pelusios Niger [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2019-1.RLTS.T163440A1008712.en]).
Limb_type_references: Research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/93030296; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/67621539) suggest that this species has complete, extensive webbing on both hands and feet.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: Green iguanas spend most of their time on land, but retreat into the water for great stretches of time when threatened; however, they have also been observed entering the water without provocation and may spend considerable time there, even occassionally  engaging in intertidal foraging behavior (Censky et al. 1998 [https://doi.org/10.1038/26886]; Walter and Smith  2004 [Walter Jr E, Smith HT (2004). Colonization success by green iguanas in Florida. Iguana 11: 154–161.]; Krysko et al. 2007 [Krysko KL, Enge KM, Donlan EM, Seitz JC, Golden EA (2007). Distribution, natural history, and impacts of the introduced green iguana (Iguana iguana) in Florida. Iguana 14:142–151.]; Furness 2021 [https://doi.org/10.17161/randa.v28i3.15892]). For these reasons, they are Transiently to Moderately Aquatic. The frequency and duration of this behaviors leads us to tentatively call the species "Transiently Aquatic", though a Moderately Aquatic designation may be more appropriate for certain populations.
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_12217
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Pritchard and Nesbitt 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsos.170499], Pritchard and Sues 2019 (J. Systematic Palaeontology), and Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.] place Claudiosaurus within Neodiapsida crownward of Weigeltisauridae.
Ecotype_references: Morphological features suggest that Claudiosaurus had an aquatic lifestyle (Carroll 1981 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1981.0079]; Sues 2019, p. 41 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]). However, it retains well-developed jointed appendages, and it remains unclear whether and to what extent it traversed on land. As a result, we cannot resolve whether this taxon was Moderately or Highly Aquatic. Even so, it is thought to have resided in near-shore environments without adaptations to a pelagic lifestyle (Carroll 1981 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1981.0079]; Houssaye 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1749-4877.2009.00146.x]).
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Moon 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1080/14772019.2017.1394922]. Note that we assume monophyly of Ichthyosaurus genus here, which is highly dubious. The actual topology of this specimen within Neoichthyosauria remains unclear and requires clarification.
Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered and demonstrably viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1986, p. 73 [Romer AS, Parsons TS (1986). The Vertebrate Body, Sixth Edition (First Edition 1949). CBS College Publishing: New York. 679 pp.]; Thewissen and Taylor 2007 [Thewiseen JGM, Taylor MA (2007). Aquatic Adaptations in the Limbs of Amniotes. Chapter 18, in: Hall BK (ed.), Fins into Limbs: Evolution, Development, and Transformation. The University of Chicago Press: Chicago. pp. 310–322]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines as “Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: All ichthyosaurs are commonly described as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (eg, Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773]; Motani 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2005)025[0462:DTMIAD]2.0.CO;2]; Maxwell 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jez.b.22459]; Gutarra and Rahman 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1111/brv.12790]); Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]. These flippers were highly integrated structures [Fernández et al. 2020 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2020.0281], and presumably encased in dense connective tissue like those of modern-day cetaceans (Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]), so that their digits were not independently mobile.

Limb_region_measurement_notes: Could be forelimb or hindlimb. Assumed forelimb. For Hand, I measured the digit associated with Metacarpal 3, but due to the presence of supernumerary phalanges, this was not the third digit from the front.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Pritchard and Nesbitt 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsos.170499], Pritchard and Sues 2019 (J. Systematic Palaeontology), and Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.] place Claudiosaurus within Neodiapsida crownward of Weigeltisauridae.
Ecotype_references: Morphological features suggest that Claudiosaurus had an aquatic lifestyle (Carroll 1981 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1981.0079]; Sues 2019, p. 41 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]). However, it retains well-developed jointed appendages, and it remains unclear whether and to what extent it traversed on land. As a result, we cannot resolve whether this taxon was Moderately or Highly Aquatic. Even so, it is thought to have resided in near-shore environments without adaptations to a pelagic lifestyle (Carroll 1981 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1981.0079]; Houssaye 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1749-4877.2009.00146.x]).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: The phylogenetic placement and monophyly of parareptiles have recently been disputed (see Ford and Benson 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41559-019-1047-3]; Simões et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.abq1898]: Science Advances). Our placement of Mesosaurs and other parareptiles within Pan-Reptilia varies in our different supertrees to reflect existing disagreements (see Methods and Supplementary Text). Piñeiro et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.4072/rbp.2021.3.04] clarify that Mesosaurus tenuidens is the only valid taxon of mesosaurid currently known, so we have renamed all the mesosaurid samples in our data set accordingly.
Ecotype_references: Although mesosaurs were viviparous (Piñeiro et al. 2012 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1080/08912963.2012.662230]) and clearly adapted for aquatic life in marine systems (Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Demarco et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.3389/fevo.2018.00109]), aspects of their vertebral morphometry have alluded to a more semi-aquatic lifestyle, with considerable time spent both in water and on land (Demarco et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.3389/fevo.2018.00109]; Demarco et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.3389/frym.2019.00039]). Adult mesosaurs may have occupied pelagic environments (Holmes 1953 [https://doi.org/10.1038/171669a0]; Verriere and Frobisch 2022 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.13866]), but we lack direct evidence concerning whether and to what extent they actually traversed on land, so it remains unclear whether mesosaurs were Moderately, Highly, or Fully Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All mesosaurids were scored as having extensive, complete pedal webbing, based on exceptional soft-tissues preserved on the hindlimb skeletons of the following mesosaurid specimens: SMNK_PAL_3806, SMF_R_4710, BSPG_1964_I_268, and URCR_064. Scorings were based on high-resolution photographs of the specimens, provided by Antoine Vérriere. Less soft-tissue preservation was discernible on forelimbs, but manual webbing phenotype was tentatively scored based on specimen NHMW_1987_0067_0001, which appears to have complete extensive webbing. Because the taxonomic breadth covered by these specimens remains unclear, the application of these scores to all sampled mesosaurids remains a tentative first approximation, as mesosaurids may have more variability in webbing phenotype than we can currently discern based on available specimens.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Piñeiro et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.4072/rbp.2021.3.04] clarify that Mesosaurus tenuidens is the only valid taxon of mesosaurid currently known, so we have renamed all the mesosaurid samples in our data set accordingly. This specimen is catalogued in the collections as “Stereosternum tumidum”, and was thus renamed here.

For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1986, p. 73 [Romer AS, Parsons TS (1986). The Vertebrate Body, Sixth Edition (First Edition 1949). CBS College Publishing: New York. 679 pp.]; Thewissen and Taylor 2007 [Thewiseen JGM, Taylor MA (2007). Aquatic Adaptations in the Limbs of Amniotes. Chapter 18, in: Hall BK (ed.), Fins into Limbs: Evolution, Development, and Transformation. The University of Chicago Press: Chicago. pp. 310–322]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines as “Fully Aquatic.”

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Non-avian dinosaur interrelationships synthesized from the following sources: Carpenter et al. 2005 [Carpenter K, Miles C, Cloward K (2005). New Small Theropod from the Upper Jurassic Morrison Formation of Wyoming. Chapter 2, in: Carpenter K (ed), The Carnivorous Dinosaurs. Indiana University Press: Bloomington. pp. 23–48.]; Brusatte et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2010.04.001]; Rauhut et al. 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1203238109]; Brusatte et al. 2014 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cub.2014.08.034]; Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]; Pol and Goloboff 2020 [http://hdl.handle.net/11336/170477]; Madzia et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.12362]; Fabbri et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04528-0].
Ecotype_references: The first complete skeleton of Heterodontosaurus was uncovered from the Upper Red Beds of the Stormberg Group in South Africa (Santa Luca et al. 1976 [https://doi.org/10.1038/264324a0]), which represent a distinctly terrestrial (arid or semi-arid) paleoenvironment. It has a relatively elongate manus and pes, but these seem adapted for grasping and running, respectively (Santa Luca et al. 1976 [[https://doi.org/10.1038/264324a0]; Norman et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-3642.2011.00697.x]), and nothing else in its skeleton clearly suggests aquatic habits. As a result, Heterodontosaurus is probably terrestrial, and we score it accordingly. However, in the additional data, we cannot rule out occasional forays into an aquatic medium, and its precise ecotype remains ambiguous.

Ecotype_references: Barasaurus is varyingly described as aquatic (McMenamin 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-74256-4], McMenamin 2019 [https://doi.org/10.20944/preprints201908.0033.v1]) and terrestrial (Ketchum and Barrett 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1139/E03-084]), in either case without much justification. It is found in the same stratum as Claudiosaurus and Hovasaurus (Ketchum and Barrett 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1139/E03-084]), but it has no osteological features obviously indicating adaptation to an aquatic lifestyle (see Meckert 1995 [Meckert D (1995). The Procolophonid Barasaurus and the Phylogeny of Early Amniotes. PhD Thesis, Biology Department, McGill University. 149 pp.]). Thus,  the extent of its aquatic habits remain unclear.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Labidosaurus is a captorhinid (Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]). The phylogenetic placement of captorhinids has recently been disputed (Simões et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.abq1898]: Sci Adv), but we assume their more longheld placement as basal eureptiles (Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]).
Ecotype_references: The morphology of Labidosaurus suggests a predominantly Terrestrial lifestyle (Sumida 1989). However, Labidosaurus is found within the Arroyo Formation (Modesto et al. 2007) within the Clear Fork Group (Abilene Area, Leonardian Series), which is dominated by limestones and shales (Mazzullo 1982). Given these depositional environments, and in the absence of additional data, we cannot rule out routine transient forays into an aquatic medium. As a result, the ecotype of Labidosaurus remains ambiguous.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Limnoscelis paludis is a diadectomorph (https://paleobiodb.org/classic/checkTaxonInfo?taxon_no=113848&is_real_user=1), and thus a stem amniote crownward of Seymouria (Ford and Benson 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41559-019-1047-3]).
Ecotype_references: Aquatic or semi-aquatic habits are often suggested for Limnoscelis (Williston 1914; Carroll 1967; Fracasso 1983). However, in the absence of additional data, we cannot resolve whether this taxon was Transiently, Moderately, or Highly Aquatic. As a result, its ecotype remains ambiguous.

Ecotype_references: Though it is predominantly aquatic, Lontra canadensis is known to make long-distance overland journeys. It occupies a wide range of fast and slow-moving aquatic habitats, mostly freshwater but also occassionally brackish or marine (Reid 2006, pp. 471–472 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]; Serfass 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-3.RLTS.T12302A164577078.en]).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Family-level interrelations of Carnivora derived from Van Valkenburgh et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ar.23026]. Species-level interrelations of mustelids derived from Law 2019 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2019.0155].
Ecotype_references: This highly aquatic species seldom leaves the water, only venturing onto land for short periods during intense storms; although primarily near-shore marine, this species may inhabit high-energy coastal systems, often around 30m deep, and max diving depths have exceeded 90m, so we consider this species to occupy large/fast water bodies (Reid 2006, pp. 472–473, Sea Otter [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]; Doroff et al. 2021[https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-3.RLTS.T7750A219377647.en]).
Limb_type_references: Hands have moderate complete webbing. Feet have complete, very extensive webbing and the acropodium is flipper-shaped. The feet are so highly webbed as to be near-flippered. Indeed, Reid (2006, p. 472 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]) calls their hindlimbs flippers, but we score them as non-flippered, given that the webbing does not functionally integrate the auto- and zeugopodia into a continuous unit. Scored based on high-resolution photographs provided by Michael Durham (Oregon Zoo, Oregon, USA).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Humerus length slightly overestimated (had to measure humerus length diagonally, down to distalmost point on *posterior* margin, rather than anterior margin, because of how the specimen was mounted. Assumed pedal phalangeal formula of 2-2-2-2 for D1–D4

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Genus-level interrelations of Glires were derived from Blanga-Kanfi et al. 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2148-9-71].
Ecotype_references: This species generally inhabits arid, terrestrial habitats and consumes exclusively terrestrial vegetation (Amori and De Smet 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-2.RLTS.T10746A22232484.en]). However, at least some Hystrix cristata individuals have been strongly suggested to occassionally cross small bodies of water (e.g., an 8m stream) as needed (Pigozzi and Patterson 1990 [Pigozzi G, Patterson IJ (1990). Movements and diet of crested porcupines in the Maremma Natural Park, central Italy. Acta Theriologica. 35: 173-80.]). Assuming this behavior is rare but part of the typical repertoire for the species, we tentatively score  Hystrix cristata as "Transiently Aquatic".
Limb_type_references: Hystrix africae australis possesses complete slight manual webbing and complete slight-to-moderate pedal webbing (Pocock 1922 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-3642.1922.tb02149.x]). The scorings for other Hystrix spp. are based on this one, and thus remain tentative and require additional verification.

i1.2331]). As a result, we call this species Transiently Aquatic.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Family-level interrelations of Carnivora derived from Van Valkenburgh et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ar.23026]. Species-level interrelations of mustelids derived from Law 2019 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2019.0155].
Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: American martens are primarily ground-dwelling, and while they do not typically hunt in the water, they readily swim across lakes or streams as needed (Clark 1987 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3503918]; Reid 2006, American Marten: pp. 462–463 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]). As a result, we consider them Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Martens and nearly all other mustelids have complete interdigital webbing, which varies in extent but may reach the proximal end of the toe pads (Kitchener et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780198759805.003.0003]). We directly examined Martes skin specimens AMNH_70236 (Martes foina) and AMNH_120687 (Martes origenes), but were unable to determine their webbing phenotype due to their stiffness and the extent of fur cover. We tentatively scored this species as having complete, slight webbing, given that their webbing was insufficiently prominent to identify from dried skin specimens.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Family-level interrelations of Carnivora derived from Van Valkenburgh et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ar.23026].
Ecotype_references: Ocelots are apparently great swimmers, but “will not enter the water unless absolutely necessary” (Murray and Gardner 1997 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504082]). Other Leopardus species (L. geoffroyi and L. guigna) do readily travel over large water bodies when necessary, making them Transiently Aquatic (Mattern and McLennan 2000 [https://doi.org/10.1006/clad.2000.0132]; Kaiser et al. 2022 [http://dx.doi.org/10.4067/S0717-65382022000100013 ]). However, L. pardalis typically does not (Murray and Gardner 1997 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504082]; Mattern and McLennan 2000 [https://doi.org/10.1006/clad.2000.0132]), so we consider it Terrestrial.
Limb_type_references: Leopardus spp. have complete webbing that spans all digits (Pocock 1917 [https://doi.org/10.1080/00222931709487018]). No zoological specimens of Leopardus were available, so we analyzed taxidermied Panthera pardus (leopard) specimen YPM_MAM_13544, but the stiffness of and extent of fur cover on the specimen prevented us from resolving its webbing phenotype directly. We tentatively scored this species as having complete, slight webbing, given that their webbing was insufficiently prominent to identify from dried skin specimens.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Humerus length slightly overestimated (had to measure humerus length diagonally, down to distalmost point on *posterior* margin, rather than anterior margin, because of how the specimen was mounted.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Family-level interrelations of Carnivora derived from Van Valkenburgh et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ar.23026]. Species-level interrelations of mustelids derived from Law 2019 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2019.0155].
Ecotype_references: Skunks occupy terrestrial habitats and have terrestrial food sources (Reid 2006, Striped Skunk: p. 459 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]). Although skunks can swim (Wade-Smith and Verts 1982), and often reside near water (Reid 2006, Striped Skunk, p. 459 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]), they typically avoid it (Wade-Smith and Verts 1982), so we consider them Terrestrial.
Limb_type_references: Skunks have complete webbing up to the proximal ends of their digit pads (Kitchener et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780198759805.003.0003]). Direct observation of skin specimens YPM_01975 (Mephitis mephitis mesomeles) and YPM_01503 (Mephitis mephitis nigra) support this claim: Their hands and feet both had complete webbing—slight but spanning all digits.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Radius measurement was very difficult to collect on mounted specimen.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Family-level interrelations of Carnivora derived from Van Valkenburgh et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ar.23026].
Ecotype_references: Although they are predominantly terrestrial, African civets typically reside near permanent water sources (Ray 1995; Do Linh San et al. 2019). They are apparently able swimmers and “do not fear water” (Ray 1995, p. 488). However, we cannot find more information about whether these occasional forays into the aquatic medium are extended or recurrent, so we cannot resolve whether they are Terrestrial or Transiently Aquatic.
IUCN_source: Do Linh San et al. 2019 (Civettictus civetta)
Limb_type_references: Skin specimen YPM_01515 and fluid-preserved specimens YPM_MAM_14925 and YPM_MAM_14931: partial webbing, between D3 and D4, on both hands and feet (all specimens labeled as “Viverra civetta”, a synonym of “Civetticus civetta”).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Family-level interrelations of Carnivora derived from Van Valkenburgh et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ar.23026].
Ecotype_references: This species occupies terrestrial environments and spend the overwhelming majority of their time on land (Storm et al. 1976 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/3830425]; Reid 2006, Red Fox, pp. 447–448 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]). However, they are apparently good swimmers (Larivière and Pasitschniak-Arts 1996 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504236]), and though they will avoid submerging when they can, at least a few foxes have been observed crossing large water ways during dispersal events, which seems to be a typical low-frequency occurrence (Storm et al. 1976 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/3830425]). As a result, we consider this species ever so slightly Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: No Vulpes_vulpes_fulva specimens were available, so we scored webbing phenotype based on taxidermied Vulpes_vulpes specimen (YPM_MAM_13512), and a skin specimen of Vulpes_vulpes_niloticus (YPM_03335), which both appeared completely unwebbed.

Ecotype_references: This species primarily inhabits precipitous slopes and mountainsides (Reid 2006, Mountain Goat, pp. 504–505 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]). However, it is also known to swim through major rivers and other bodies of water to establish new territories (Rideout 1974; Kavicic et al. 2020). As a result, we consider this species Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Taxidermied Oreamnos_americanus specimen YPM_MAM_013634: proximal phalanges of D3 and D4 are completely encased in a muscular soft-tissue “palm”, but no soft interdigital tissue is present distal to the extended palm.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: Although this species is primarily terrestrial (Reid 2006, Wild Boar, pp. 487–488 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]), and most individuals presumably spend little time in the water, wild boars are great swimmers (Kavicic et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1080/03949370.2020.1733669]), and multiple reports describe extended voyages at sea spanning several kilometers (Yong et al. 2010 [https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusID:150368393] and references therein; IUCN Wild Pig Specialist Group 2014 [https://www.researchgate.net/publication/264898388_Suiform_Soundings_131__Suiform_Soundings_Newsletter_of_the_IUCN_SSC_Wild_Pig_Peccary_and_Hippo_Specialist_Groups]). Because these journeys are common for the species as a whole, but presumably rare events for individual animals, we consider the wild boar Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Google image searches suggest that the proximal phalanges of pigs, like most other artiodactyls, are embedded in a soft-tissue “palm”, without soft interdigital tissue distal to them. However, no zoological specimens of Sus scrofa were available for direct observation, so this scoring remains tentative.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Femur slightly underestimated (couldn’t access proximal most point of femoral head) due to articulation.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Species-level interrelations of sampled Perissodactyls derived from Steiner and Ryder 2011 [http://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-3642.2011.00752.x].
Ecotype_references: Equus ferus caballus naturally resides in arid or semi-arid landscapes (King et al. 2015 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2015-2.RLTS.T41763A45172856.en]). We find no reference to aquatic habits in this species.
Limb_type_references: Equus species are functionally monodactyl, and their autopodia have a complicated evolutionary history that makes it challenging to assign them a webbing phenotype. Historically, Equus was thought to have a complete digit III and reduced metacarpal remnants of digits II and IV (Solounias et al. 2018 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsos.171782]). However, a recent study has suggested that the dominant digit of Equus is a composite, with distal remnants of digits I, II, IV, and V expressed about the ventral and ventrolateral aspects of the digit III hoof. This complicates any interpretation of webbing in the taxon. One might consider the species Stubbed, given that these digit remnants are all enclosed together into a single unit functionally representing a single digit. Alternatively, one might reject that these digit remnants constitute separate phalangeal digits, in which case horses would have only one digit, precluding the presence of a connective tissue palm between adjacent digits. Given this ambiguity, we do not assign a webbing phenotype to the species.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: To account for their modified zeugopod anatomy without artefactually underestimating zeugopod length, ulna length was measured from the proxmost point of the ulna to the laterodistalmost point of the radius (so this latter point was used for both forelimb zeugopod bone measurements).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Marsupial interrelations derived from Mitchell et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1093/molbev/msu176].
Ecotype_references: Didelphis species are strong but inefficient swimmers, which will readily take to the water, even as an occasional means of escape, but tire and chill quickly when submerged (McManus 1974 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3503783]; Fish 1993 [https://doi.org/10.2307/1382382]). Given that this behavior is typical, if perhaps rare, we tentatively call the Virginia opossum “Transiently Aquatic”. However, if the Virginia opossum's use of water as a means of escape is so vanishingly rare as to be highly atypical, we would revise its designation to "Terrestrial".
Limb_type_references: Skin specimens YPM_14365, YPM_15326, and YPM_15327 had extensive palm girth overhang, particularly on hands, but no true interdigital webbing.

However, their behavioral flexibility warrants closer investigation of this designation.

Limb_type_references: Based on skin specimen YPM_MAM_04413 (Euphractus villosus) and research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/150602472; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/150001599; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/142608266; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/133621364). Webbing phenotype difficult to assess. Tentatively scored as unwebbed with some very slight ventral palm and finger girth and some dorsal scale invasion of interdigital space, but no true webbing. Euphractus sexcinctus specimens were unavailable, and the available Euphractus sp. skin specimen was contorted, making this species’ webbing scores tentative.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Tibia mounted in position where I couldn't fit my calipers in to measure it.

Ecotype_references: Although rhinos are predominantly terrestrial, they will readily cross large, fast-moving rivers and other water bodies when necessary, typically when colonizing new areas or in response to intense floods (Dutta and Mahanta 2015 [http://dx.doi.org/10.11609/JoTT.o4024.6864-77]; Dutta et al. 2021 [Dutta DK, Bora PJ, Sharma R, Sarmah A, Sharma A, Kumar J, Dekaraja K, Saikia RB, Halliday A (2021). Greater one-horned rhino (Rhinoceros unicornis) behaviour during high floods at Kaziranga National Park and the Burhachapori Wildlife Sanctuary, Assam, India. Pachyderm 62: 63–67. Accessed online via URL=https://pachydermjournal.org/index.php/pachyderm/article/view/447]).
Limb_type_references: Taxidermied Rhinoceros unicornis specimen YPM_MAM_007907: digits appeared to be encased up to base of ungual in extended palms of connective tissue.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Humerus length is probably slight overestimate (exceeded lenngth of calipers, so had to measure in two spans, which may have made it greater than the single straight line length of the element). Same with ulna.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Species-level interrelations of sampled Perissodactyls derived from Steiner and Ryder 2011 [http://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-3642.2011.00752.x].
Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: Although primarily ground-dwelling, tapirs are excellent swimmers; they readily cross wide streams and enter the water both to flee or hide from predators and to feed on aquatic vegetation (Meijaard and van Strien 2003 [Meijaard E, Strien NV (2003). The Asian Tapir (Tapirus indicus). In: Briefing Book, Malay Tapir Conservation Workshop. Krau Wildlife Reserve, Malaysia. 22 pp. Accessed online via URL = https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Erik-Meijaard/publication/236898616_Der_Schabrackentapir_Tapirus_indicus/links/58338f4008aef19cb81cb43e/Der-Schabrackentapir-Tapirus-indicus.pdf.]; Garcìa et al. 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-1.RLTS.T21471A45173340.en]; Naranjo 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-28868-6]). As a result, we tentatively score all tapirs in this study as being Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Tentatively scored based on skin specimen of Tapirus_bairdii (YPM_14313): some degree of webbing to mid-digit on both hand and foot, difficult to distinguish from interdigital girth and hard to score given close appresion of digits. Webbing "subtype" too difficult to score.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Femur length approximate. It was just barely longer than my calipers, which made its length difficult to estimate in two spans. pD2 was the anteriormost digit with phalanges, so this was treated as Antmost pedal digit in acropod_LM measurements.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Atlantogenata topology from Alvarez-Carretero et al. 2022 and Heritage et al. 2021 [Heritage S, Seiffert ER, Borths MR (2021). Recommended fossil calibrators for time-scaled molecular phylogenies of Afrotheria. Afrotherian Conservation 17. Accessed via URL=https://www.afrotheria.net/newsletter.php.2021.]
Ecotype_references: Rock Hyraxes occupy a range of terrestrial environments (Rifai et al. 2000 [https://doi.org/10.1080/09397140.2000.10637829]; Butynski et al. 2015 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2015- 2.RLTS.T41766A21285876.en]). We find no reference to aquatic habits for this species.
Limb_type_references: Skin specimen YPM_MAM_03617: hands and feet both shockingly have at least slight partial webbed, slighter on feet. Another hyrax species, Dendrohyrax arboreus crawshayi (ref: YPM_MAM_03047), is similarly webbed, suggesting that webbing is present in multiple hyrax species.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Radius measurement difficult to collect on mounted specimen. Only approximate.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype_references: Several felids in the genera Ictailurus, Leopardus, Panthera, and Prionailurus readily swim, in some cases engaging in extensive overwater travel or even mating in the water (Mattern and McLennan 2000; Kaiser et al. 2022). However, Felis spp. typically do not (Mattern and McLennan 2000). Notably, some breeds of F. catus challenge this pattern. For example, the Turkish Van Cat, which has a centuries-old reported history in the Lake Van region of Turkey, apparently enjoys swimming and readily swims when given the chance (Cak 2017). So too do a handful of other cat breeds (Moss 2015). However, given that the majority of domestic cats presumably do not, and given that we lack information on the breed of origin for this skeletonized specimen, we treat this cat as an example of the Terrestrial norm.
Limb_type_references: All Felis species have complete interdigital webbing (Pocock 1917 [https://doi.org/10.1080/00222931709487018]). No F. catus specimens were available, so we directly observed taxidermied bobcat (Lynx rufus) specimens YPM_MAM_013545 and YPM_MAM_13518, and fluid-preserved F. sylvestris specimen YPM_MAM_ 05816. The stiffness and fur cover of the skin specimens prevented us from interpreting them, but the F. sylvestris specimen had all of its proximal phalanges encased in soft-tissue “palm”, which counts as webbing under our operational definition. As a result, we scored both hands and feet as having complete webbing. This observation is consistent with previous descriptions of the species (e.g., Pocock 1917 [https://doi.org/10.1080/00222931709487018]).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: The phylogenetic placement of this taxon remains dubious (see Kammerer et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2011.627074]). We tentatively place it within Therocephalia.
Ecotype_references: The phylogenetic placement of this taxon remains dubious (see van den Heever 1987; Kammerer et al. 2011), and we find no comment on its level of terrestriality in the literature.
Limb_type_references: NA
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Marsupial interrelations derived from Mitchell et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1093/molbev/msu176].
Ecotype_references: The Water Opossum routinely inhabits and hunts in water, but dens in subterranean dens; because the species spends extensive time both on land and in water, we consider it Moderately Aquatic; this species appears to prefer higher-energy streams and river courses, since it is rarely seen in silt-laden water courses (Fish 1993 [https://doi.org/10.2307/1382382]; Pérez-Hernandez et al. 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-1.RLTS.T4671A22173467.en]). As a result, we tentatively score this species as occupying large or fast-moving water bodies.
Limb_type_references: Skin specimen YPM_03416: Complete, moderate-to-extensive pedal webbing spanning all digits. Hands are unwebbed.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (=d3) for both hand and foot.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype_references: Although it is primarily a fossorial ground-dweller, Tachyglossus is also good swimmer and will readily cross natural waterways when necessary (Rismiller and Grutzner 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1093/mspecies/sez012]). As a result, we consider the species Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Extensive palm girth overhang with at least partial (and potentially complete) webbing (tentative scoring based on taxidermied specimen YPM 10903).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (=mD3, pD2)
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Monotreme placement and interrelationships derived from Phillips et al. 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.0904649106].
Ecotype_references: Platypuses are highly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle (Pasitschniak-Arts and Marinelli 1998 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504433]). They excavate resting burrows on the banks of water bodies, with entrances below the water line, and feed almost entirely on aquatic (benthic) invertebrates (Pasitschniak-Arts and Marinelli 1998 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504433]). Platypuses spend a little under half of their time (avg 10 h/day) foraging in the water, and the rest of their time (an estimated 12–17 h/day) resting in their burrow dens (Serena 1994 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1469-7998.1994.tb01563.x]; Pasitschniak-Arts and Marinelli 1998 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504433]). However, they may occasionally spend more extended periods of time (3–7 days reported) in their dens out of the water (Serena 1994), and in some populations, individuals will travel significant distances (7–8 km reported) overland to migrate between neighboring river basins (Furlan et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10592-013-0478-7]). As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic. Platypuses inhabit a range of fast- and slow-moving water bodies (Pasitschniak-Arts and Marinelli 1998 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504433]).
Limb_type_references: Skin specimen YPM_10846: Completely webbed. The feet have complete moderate-to-extensive webbing with extensive snowshoeing along the posterior margin of the ankle. The hands have complete and very extensive webbing beyond the distal most tips of the unguals and extending beyond the contours of the hand.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (mD4, pD3).
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Platypuses spend a little under half of their time (avg 10 h/day) foraging in the water, and the rest of their time (an estimated 12–17 h/day) resting in their burrow dens (Serena 1994 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1469-7998.1994.tb01563.x]; Pasitschniak-Arts and Marinelli 1998 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504433]). However, they may occasionally spend more extended periods of time (3–7 days reported) in their dens out of the water (Serena 1994), and in some populations, individuals will travel significant distances (7–8 km reported) overland to migrate between neighboring river basins (Furlan et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10592-013-0478-7]). As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic. Platypuses inhabit a range of fast- and slow-moving water bodies (Pasitschniak-Arts and Marinelli 1998 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504433]).

Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: Raccoons are predominantly terrestrial animals that consume a wide variety of prey and other foodstuffs; their diet frequently includes waterfowl and freshwater turtles, which they often hunt on the edges of water courses; they may also make transient forays into shallow water courses to obtain prey (Lotze and Anderson 1979 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3503959]; Reid 2006, Northern Raccoon: pp. 455–456 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]). As a result, we consider this species Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Procyon lotor skin specimens YPM_01781 YPM_04500: hands and feet unwebbed but with extensive palm girth overhang
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Ecotype_references: We find no reference to aquatic habits in this predominantly terrestrial species (Salton and Sargis 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jmor.10697]; Stephenson et al. 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016- 1.RLTS.T40595A97204107.en]).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Genus-level interrelations of Glires were derived from Blanga-Kanfi et al. 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2148-9-71].
Ecotype_references: Domesticated guinea pigs are typically considered sedentary terrestrial animals (Dagg and Windsor 1971 [https://doi.org/10.1139/z72-019]; Barbera et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ar.24271]). Although wild cavies (C. aperea) readily take to the water and have been reported to swim several kilometers during intense floods (Lee 2010 [Lee Y (2010). Guinea Pigs. Chapter 8, in: Tynes VV (ed), Behavior of Exotic Pets. Blackwell Publishing: West Sussex. pp. 78–90.]), we find no reference to aquatic habits in the domesticated C. porcellus. As a result, while we would consider C. aperea Transiently Aquatic, we call C. porcellus Terrestrial.
Limb_type_references: C. porcellus hands and feet are completely unwebbed but with fleshy palm/sole pads (Barbera et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ar.24271]). Based on this report and a cursory examination of AMNH_M_skin specimens from other Cavia spp., we tentatively score this species as unwebbed with interdigital girth.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Species-level interrelations of Muroidea derived from Steppan and Schenk 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal. pone.0183070]
Ecotype_references: Much like their round-tailed cousins, these semi-aquatic rodents consume almost entirely aquatic food, build intricate lodges within the water column, and spend the vast majority of their time in, on, or very near the water (Chapman 1889 [http://hdl.handle.net/2246/1699]; Proulx and Gilbert 1983 [https://doi.org/10.5962/p.355039]; Reid 2006, Common Muskrat: pp. 326–327 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]). While they seem to be more active in the water than round-tailed muskrats (Chapman 1889 [http://hdl.handle.net/2246/1699]), and are sometimes broadly considered more aquatic (Reid 2006, Round-Tailed Muskrat, pp. 325–326 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]), they may still travel extended distances over land to find new suitable habitats. Yearling animals typically do so in the spring. As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic. Muskrats prefer slow-moving water bodies (Proulx and Gilbert 1983 [https://doi.org/10.5962/p.355039]).
Limb_type_references: Partial hand and foot webbing evident in the majority of observed skin specimens, including AMNH_16204, AMNH_122697, and AMNH_129223. However, there seems to be some intraspecific variation, because the foot of skin specimen AMNH_70591 is unwebbed and lacks stiff paradigital integument.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Genus-level interrelations of Xenarthra derived from Delsuc et al. 2002 [https://doi.org/10.1093/oxfordjournals.molbev.a003989].
Ecotype_references: The Southern Tamandua is primarily arboreal and ground-dwelling with terrestrial insect prey; although a small minority are found near streams, we find no reference to aquatic habits in this species (Hayssen 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1644/875.1]; Miranda et al. 2015 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2014-1.RLTS.T21350A47442916.en]).
Limb_type_references: Tamandua tetradactyla skin specimens YPM_MAM_347 and YPM_MAM_348 appeared completely unwebbed, consistent with diagrams given in Taylor 1978 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jmor.1051570307]).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Manual D3 length approximate (difficult to place distalmost point beneath ungual sheath)

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Genus-level interrelations of Xenarthra derived from Delsuc et al. 2002 [https://doi.org/10.1093/oxfordjournals.molbev.a003989].
Ecotype_references: Aardvarks are primarily terrestrial, occupying soft terrestrial terrains where they can dig easily (Knöthig 2005; Taylor and Lehmann 2015). They feed almost exclusively on terrestrial insects (Taylor et al. 2002; Knöthig 2005; Taylor and Lehmann 2015). While they are reportedly good swimmers, with one report of an ~20-meter river crossing, extensive field observations suggest that they very seldom do swim, and avoid areas with high ground-water levels (Knöthig 2005). Because they do not have any recurring or extended aquatic habits, we consider this species Terrestrial.
Limb_type_references: Taxidermied aardvark specimen YPM_05232: feet definitely unwebbed; hands probably unwebbed but with possible slight webbing between and D3 and D4. Because this webbing was so ambiguous and may have been palm girth, we call this species’ hand webbing phenotype “Unclear”.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Marsupial interrelations derived from Mitchell et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1093/molbev/msu176].
Ecotype_references: The Red Kangaroo inhabits arid or semi-arid environments where water is typically scarce (Russell 1974 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2907.1974.tb00347.x]; Ellis et al. 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-2.RLTS.T40567A21953534.en]). The closely related Eastern Grey Kangaroo (Macropus giganteus) has been known to enter deep water to escape predation by dingos (Purnell 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1071/AM10001]), but we find no similar observations reported for the Red Kangaroo.
Limb_type_references: Skin specimens YPM_MAM_31 (Macropus eugenii): palm girth but no webbing; YPM_MAM_04100 (Macropus eugenii): no webbing; YPM_MAM_32 (Macropus canguru/giganteus: clearly unwebbed. No Macropus Rufus skin specimens available, so scoring of M. rufus is tentative, based solely on webbing phenotype of congeners.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Genus-level interrelations of Xenarthra derived from Delsuc et al. 2002 [https://doi.org/10.1093/oxfordjournals.molbev.a003989].
Ecotype_references: Linné’s Two-Toed Sloth is predominantly arboreal, residing high in the upper canopies of rainforest; although it is apparently a good swimmer, we find no reference to aquatic habits in this species (Adam 1999 [https://doi.org/10.1644/812.1]). As a result, we consider it Terrestrial. To our knowledge, aquatic habits have only been described among sloths for Bradypus pygmaeus (Hayssen 2008 [https://doi.org/10.1644/812.1]).
IUCN_source: Chiarello et al. 2022 (Choloepus didactylus [Chiarello A, Plese T, De Thoisy B, Pool M, Aliaga-Rossel E, Santos P, Moraes-Barros N (2022). Choloepus didactylus. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2022: e.T4777A210443323. Accessed on 15 April 2023.])
Limb_type_references: Skin specimens of Choloepus didactylus (YPM_MAM_0352) and Bradypus tridactylus (YPM_MAM_0351) with attached acropodial skeletons demonstrate that complete, thick interdigital connective tissue encases all digits up to at least the base of the unguals in extant sloths. The digits appear to be fused within an extended palm-like structure, with just the ungual claws protruding from the extended palm.

Ecotype_references: The Lesser Grison is predominantly terrestrial; although wild individuals are commonly found near water, and captive individuals have been reported to bathe or play in water tubs, we find sparse if any reference to aquatic habits for this species (Yensen and Tarifa 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1644/728]; Helgen and Schiaffini 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-1.RLTS.T41639A45211832.en]). However, one clip online shows an individual raiding a bird's nest for eggs on the immediate shoreline of a large water body (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ETzOlcJQcXM), and transiently aquatic habits for the species remain plausible. In the absence of additional information about whether it ever enters the water, we consider its ecotype ambiguous between Terrestrial and Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Galictis cuja skin specimen YPM_01498 showed slight-to-moderate webbing up to base of ungual across all digits on hands and feet. This is consistent with the description of interdigital webbing provided by Yensen and Tarifa (2003).
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Skipped all but longest digit and D3 for sake of time.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Pholidota placed as sister to Carnivora following Delsuc et al. 2002 [https://doi.org/10.1093/oxfordjournals.molbev.a003989] and Upham et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pbio.3000494].
Ecotype_references: This predominantly arboreal species resides near water courses (Pietersen et al. 2019 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2019- 3.RLTS.T12767A123586469.en]) and is apparently a good swimmer (Jansen et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1016/B978-0-12-815507-3.00009-5]). However, while swimming is more common in some other pangolin species, like Phataginus tetradactyla (Gudehus et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1016/B978-0-12-815507-3.00008-3]) and Smutsia gigantea (de Heusch et al. 1993 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/2804441]), one non-biological report suggests that P. tricuspis seldom enters the water (de Heusch et al. 1993 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/2804441]). In the absence of additional natural history data to clarify this claim, we cannot resolve whether the species is Transiently Aquatic or Terrestrial.
Limb_type_references: Skin specimen of Phataginus tricuspis was missing legs, so we based the webbing phenotype of this species on the closely related skin specimen YPM_04415 (Phataginus tetradactyla), whose hands and feet were both unwebbed.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Pinniped interrelationships were derived from Park et al. 2024 [https://doi.org/10.1093/evolut/qpae061].
Ecotype_references: Pinnipeds are highly adapted to aquatic life, and spend most of their time in the water; they are active swimmers and dive to forage for their exclusively aquatic prey, but they also routinely haul onto land to rest, bask, and (in some species more than others) court and breed (Berta 2008 [Berta A (2008). Pinnipeds. In Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]; Bowen 2008 [Bowen WD (2008). Pinniped Ecology. In Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). As a result, we consider all pinnipeds Highly Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All pinnipeds were scored as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (following Cooper 2008 [Cooper LN. 2008. Forelimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). This scoring was supported by direct observation of Zalophus and Phoca specimens (YPM 03329: Phoca vitulina concolor, skin; AMNH_M_201648: Zalophus californianus, fluid-preserved). Otariid and odobenid foreflippers have digits that are outwardly indistinguishable from the flipper planform, and have limited independent mobility, whereas those of phocids are outwardly distinguishable and retain the ability to prominently pronate. Thus, phocid forelimbs were scored as Weakly Flippered, while otariid and odobenid forelimbs were considered Strongly Flippered. All pinniped hindlimbs have outwardly distinguishable digits, but those of otariids have more interdigital connective tissues, which stiffens them significantly (Adam 2008 [Adam PJ. 2008. Hindlimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). To reflect this pattern, otariid hindlimbs were considered Strongly Flippered, whereas odobenid and phocid hindlimbs were considered Weakly Flippered.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Merged from two separate radiographs of the same specimen (one with zeugopod cutoff and one with hand cutoff) in photoshop. Assumed calipers visible in radiograph were open to 10mm (John Ososky, personal communication).
