Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Heritage et al. 2021 [Heritage S, Seiffert ER, Borths MR (2021). Recommended fossil calibrators for time-scaled molecular phylogenies of Afrotheria. Afrotherian Conservation 17. Accessed via URL=https://www.afrotheria.net/newsletter.php.2021.]
Ecotype_references: Sirenians are fully aquatic; they are unable to travel on land, bearing their young and spending their entire lives in the water; Trichechus inunguis is predominantly freshwater, whereas all other extant Trichechus species readily travel between fresh and saltwater habitats, and Dugongs are exclusively marine (Domning 2008 [Domning DP (2008). Sirenian Evolution. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]).
Limb_type_references: All sirenians have strongly flippered forelimbs (Cooper 2008 [Cooper LN. 2008. Forelimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]) and lack hindlimbs altogether (Adam 2008 [Adam PJ. 2008. Hindlimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). This was supported by direct observation of Trichechus specimens (AMNH_M_S-03: Trichechus_inunguis, fluid-preserved; AMNH_202525 and AMNH_201322: Trichechus_sp, fluid-preserved) and high-resolution photographs of Trichechus manatus latirostris in the wild, provided by Meghan Cohorst: Save the Manatee Club, FL, USA).
Notes on occurrences: Extant. Max occurrence age for genus as a whole is older (7.246 Ma). Dugong dugon specifically has a max occurrence date of 0.0117 Ma reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Low-resolution. No scale, so all measurements in pixels.

Ecotype_references: Pronghorns are terrestrial cursors that primarily inhabit sagebrush deserts and open grasslands (Reid 2006, Pronghorn, pp. 500–501 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]). Although they are predominantly terrestrial, pronghorns will occasionally ford deep and fast-moving water bodies during long-distance travel when necessary (Skinner 1922 [https://doi.org/10.2307/1373299]). As a result, we call them Transiently Aquatic.

Ecotype_references: Bison are predominantly terrestrial animals that migrate across open grasslands (Reid 2006, American Bison, pp. 502–503 [Reid FA (2006). However, bison herds will regularly cross large rivers in the course of their migrations (Thomas et al. 2011). As a result, we consider them Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Some of the proximal phalanges of bison are presumably encased in a muscular soft-tissue “palm” like those of other sampled bovids. However, we could not access any zoological bison specimens to verify this claim. As a result, we scored its webbing phenotype as “Unclear”.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Humerus, femur, radius, and ulna measurements very approximate: rounded to nearest 10 mm (too large for my calipers, so measured using a large pliable metal 45 cm ruler).

Ecotype_references: The Common Hippopotamus is well-adapted to a semi-aquatic lifestyle (Van Der Geer et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1111/let.12095]). It spends the majority of the day standing or bottom-walking in the shallow water of rivers, coastal lakes, and marine estuaries (Fisher et al. 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ar.20531]; Lewison and Pluhácek 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2017- 2.RLTS.T10103A18567364.en]; Endo et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1111/ahe.12405]). They typically leave the water each night to forage on land for terrestrial grasses, which comprise a large part of their diet (Lewison and Pluhácek 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2017- 2.RLTS.T10103A18567364.en]). They have also been known to travel several kilometers overland at night (Clarke 1953 [https://doi.org/10.2307/1375838]). Given its extended daily activity both in water and on land, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic. Because it is typically bottom-walking, we consider it to inhabit relatively small or slow-moving water bodies that do not require surface swimming against strong currents in deep water.
Limb_type_references: Hippopotamids were scored using fluid-preserved Choeropsis specimens AMNH_214396 and AMNH_146608, which had very slight webbing between D3 and D4. However, Hippopotamus may have somewhat more pronounced webbing, and as we could not directly observe any zoological Hippopotamus specimens, this scoring remains tentative.
Photographable?: Forelimb is in ventral view, but dubiously so in dorsal view
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Webbing status ambiguous. Need verify with fixed specimens. D3 is longest manual digit and D4 is longest pedal digit. Excluded ungual from acropod measurements as with the others specimens.

Ecotype_references: Like other cryptobranchids (Browne et al. 2014 [Browne RK, Li H, Wang Z, Okada S, Hime P, McMillan A, Wu M, Diaz R, McGinnity D, Briggler JT (2013). The giant salamanders (Cryptobranchidae): Part B. Biogeography, ecology and reproduction. Amphibian and Reptile Conservation 5(4): 30-50.]; Vitt and Caldwell 2014 (Cryptobranchidae, pp. 459–460); IUCN SSC Amphibian Specialist Group 2022 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2022- 2.RLTS.T59077A82473431.en]), the Japanese Giant Salamander is considered a fully aquatic species, which reproduces, spawns, and metamorphoses within the water, and then spends most if not all of its time as an adult submerged. Though it does consume a combination of aquatic and terrestrial prey, the vast majority of its prey (when considered by number) is aquatic. Although it could presumably voyage onto land with its well-developed limbs, we could find no mention of overland travel in this species. As a result, we consider it Fully Aquatic. Because this species is primarily bottom-walking, we score it tentatively as effectively occupying small or slow-moving water bodies, insufficiently deep or fast-moving to prevent bottom-walking near land. A notable caveat is that it is known to sometimes occupy larger rivers, but presumably does so in lower-flow conditions at the river bottom (IUCN SSC Amphibian Specialist Group 2022 [IUCN SSC Amphibian Specialist Group (2022). Andrias japonicus. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2022: e.T1273A177177761. Accessed on 12 June 2024.]).
IUCN_source: IUCN SSC Amphibian Specialist Group 2022 (Andrias japonicus [IUCN SSC Amphibian Specialist Group (2022). Andrias japonicus. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2022: e.T1273A177177761. Accessed on 12 June 2024.])
Limb_type_references: Research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/26811-Andrias-japonicus; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/86843693) suggest no webbing on hands but slight partial webbing on feet.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Longest digit was D4 for both hand and foot. Measurement excluded unguals in line with amniote measurements. NEED RETAKE ACROPOD MEASUREMENTS TO INCLUDE UNGUALS, SINCE SALAMANDERS LACK CLAWS.

Ecotype_references: Cryptobranchids are fully aquatic salamanders that reproduce, spawn, metamorphose, and spend most if not all of their time as an adult submerged; they respire primarily through their skin in the water, and the major staple of their diet is freshwater crayfish (Browne et al. 2014 [Browne RK, Li H, Wang Z, Okada S, Hime P, McMillan A, Wu M, Diaz R, McGinnity D, Briggler JT (2013). The giant salamanders (Cryptobranchidae): Part B. Biogeography, ecology and reproduction. Amphibian and Reptile Conservation 5(4): 30-50.]; Vitt and Caldwell 2014 (Cryptobranchidae, pp. 459–460); IUCN SSC Amphibian Specialist Group 2022 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2022- 2.RLTS.T59077A82473431.en]). While Cryptobranchus individuals can presumably leave the water for vanishingly short periods, we find no reports of extensive overland travel. Because this species is primarily bottom-walking, we score it tentatively as effectively occupying small or slow-moving water bodies, insufficiently deep or fast-moving to prevent bottom-walking near land.

Limb_type_references: Research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/137789012; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/135435052; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/129333355; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/121678843) suggest that this species lacks webbing.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Family-level interrelations of Carnivora derived from Van Valkenburgh et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ar.23026].
Ecotype_references: This specimen is a St. Bernard. Although several domesticated dog breeds (so-called “water dogs”) were specifically bred to retrieve waterfowl, hunt semi-aquatic mammals, or facilitate human fish-netting boating activities (Tractive 2021), the St. Bernard is not one of these. We were unable to find a definitive source on how St. Bernards typically engage with water. While we doubt that it is averse to water, we assume it has no particularly elevated affinity for it, and we consider this breed Terrestrial.
Limb_type_references: This specimen is a St. Bernard skeleton. No zoological St. Bernard specimens were available, so we based its webbing phenotype on taxidermied Canis_lupus (grey wolf) specimen YPM_MAM_013619, which appeared to lack webbing (though it was very difficult to tell and this scoring remains dubious). We find no reference to interdigital webbing in St. Bernards online (eg, via https://blog.myollie.com/dogs-with-webbed-feet/), so we score this specimen as unwebbed.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Longest digit was D3 for both hand and foot. Dog breed: St. Bernard.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Family-level interrelations of artiodactyls derived from Gatesy et al. 2013 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2012.10.012].
Ecotype_references: Because we have no species designation for this specimen, we cannot reliably resolve its ecotype.
Limb_type_references: Camel feet provide a difficult case for our scoring system. Let us treat the condition of the Arabian Camel (Camelus dromadrius) as typical for group. With this assumption in place, the proximal phalanges of camels are prominently separated (immediately distal to the fused metacarpus) by a tissue-less space called the interdigital septum (Badawy 2011 [Badawy AM (2011). Computed Tomographic anatomy of the fore foot in one-humped camel (Camelus dromedrus). Global Veterinaria 6: 417-423.]). However, the distal epiphyses of the proximal phalanges, along with the entirety of the middle and ungual phalanges, are encased together within a single functional unit that serves as a makeshift false hoof (Badawy 2011 [Badawy AM (2011). Computed Tomographic anatomy of the fore foot in one-humped camel (Camelus dromedrus). Global Veterinaria 6: 417-423.]). As a result, camels alone among all the animals scored here have interdigital tissue that enmeshes their digits distally but not proximally. Given the unique morphology of camel autopodia, and our inability to directly observe zoological camelid specimens, we score camels as having an ambiguous limb phenotype.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Two digits of equal length on each limb. Measured medial (anterior) full digit.

The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]; Pol and Goloboff 2020 [http://hdl.handle.net/11336/170477]; Madzia et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.12362]; Fabbri et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04528-0].
Ecotype_references: Nothing in the skeleton of this ornithischian suggests an aquatic mode of life (see Gilmore 1909 [https://doi.org/10.5479/si.00963801.36-1666.197]). However, this taxon is known from the Morrison Formation, which represents a variety of riverine, lacustrine, and floodplain environments (Dodson et al. 1980), and we cannot rule out a Transiently Aquatic lifestyle for this predominantly terrestrial species. As a result, the ecotype of this species remains unclear.
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Humerus, femur, and tibia lengths were longer than calipers, so were taken using placeholders, making them more error-prone. Assumed (and confident that) all but the ungual phalanx of digit 3 was preserved on both the hand and foot. Radius and ulna lengths dubious, because they were disarticulated and I wasn’t sure of their orientations during articulation, so my estimation of the lateralmost points of their distal epiphyses was questionable.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Heritage et al. 2021 [Heritage S, Seiffert ER, Borths MR (2021). Recommended fossil calibrators for time-scaled molecular phylogenies of Afrotheria. Afrotherian Conservation 17. Accessed via URL=https://www.afrotheria.net/newsletter.php.2021.]
Ecotype_references: We find no reference to aquatic habits in this predominantly terrestrial species (Salton and Sargis 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jmor.10697]; Stephenson et al. 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016- 1.RLTS.T40595A97204107.en]).
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved Tenrec_ecaudatus specimen YPM_MAM_06028: completely unwebbed
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Treated D3 as longest digit for both hand and foot. Tibia difficult to measure given mounting position, so this measurement is more error-prone.

Ecotype_references: Anatomical and histological evidence support a terrestrial lifestyle for post-metamorphic individuals of this species, and it was thought to undergo metamorphosis rapidly (Berman et al. 1999 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2000)020[0253:ROSSSF]2.0.CO;2]; Bazzana et al. 2020). However, it still laid eggs and metamorphosed in water, so we tentatively treat Seymouria as a transiently aquatic (but predominantly terrestrial) species.
Limb_type_references: NA

Limb_region_measurement_notes: Longest digit was D4 on both hand and foot. Measurements included unguals as, in Seymouria, the unguals probably lacked keratinous claws (Maddin and Reisz 2007).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: The phylogenetic placement and monophyly of parareptiles have recently been disputed (see Ford and Benson 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41559-019-1047-3]; Simões et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.abq1898]: Science Advances). Our placement of Mesosaurs and other parareptiles within Pan-Reptilia varies in our different supertrees to reflect existing disagreements (see Methods and Supplementary Text). Piñeiro et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.4072/rbp.2021.3.04] clarify that Mesosaurus tenuidens is the only valid taxon of mesosaurid currently known, so we have renamed all the mesosaurid samples in our data set accordingly.
Ecotype_references: Although mesosaurs were viviparous (Piñeiro et al. 2012 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1080/08912963.2012.662230]) and clearly adapted for aquatic life in marine systems (Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Demarco et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.3389/fevo.2018.00109]), aspects of their vertebral morphometry have alluded to a more semi-aquatic lifestyle, with considerable time spent both in water and on land (Demarco et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.3389/fevo.2018.00109]; Demarco et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.3389/frym.2019.00039]). Adult mesosaurs may have occupied pelagic environments (Holmes 1953 [https://doi.org/10.1038/171669a0]; Verriere and Frobisch 2022 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.13866]), but we lack direct evidence concerning whether and to what extent they actually traversed on land, so it remains unclear whether mesosaurs were Moderately, Highly, or Fully Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All mesosaurids were scored as having extensive, complete pedal webbing, based on exceptional soft-tissues preserved on the hindlimb skeletons of the following mesosaurid specimens: SMNK_PAL_3806, SMF_R_4710, BSPG_1964_I_268, and URCR_064. Scorings were based on high-resolution photographs of the specimens, provided by Antoine Vérriere. Less soft-tissue preservation was discernible on forelimbs, but manual webbing phenotype was tentatively scored based on specimen NHMW_1987_0067_0001, which appears to have complete extensive webbing. Because the taxonomic breadth covered by these specimens remains unclear, the application of these scores to all sampled mesosaurids remains a tentative first approximation, as mesosaurids may have more variability in webbing phenotype than we can currently discern based on available specimens.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Assumed manual D3 has three phalanges (with the third, the ungual, not preserved in the specimen). Multiple casts have this specimen number. I measured the most complete skeleton on the large rectangular slab (the one that fills an entire drawer and contains multiple skeletons together in one slab). Piñeiro et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.4072/rbp.2021.3.04] clarify that Mesosaurus tenuidens is the only valid taxon of mesosaurid currently known, so we have renamed all the mesosaurid samples in our data set accordingly. This specimen is catalogued in the collections as “Mesosaurus brasiliensis”, and was thus renamed here.

Ecotype_references: Ringtails are arid-environment specialists that can survive with very little water; we find no reference to aquatic habits (Reid 2006, pp. 454–455, Bassariscus astutus [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]; Roadman 2014 [Roadman AA (2014). Ringtail distribution, dermatoglyphics, and diet in Zion National park, Utah. Order No. 1584442. Available from ProQuest Dissertations & Theses Global, ProQuest Dissertations & Theses Global Closed Collection, Publicly Available Content Database, 1659778695. Accessed via URL = https://www.proquest.com/dissertations-theses/ringtail-distribution-dermatoglyphics-diet-zion/docview/1659778695/se-2]).
Limb_type_references: Bassariscus astutus raptor skin specimens YPM_001437 and YPM_01438 appeared unwebbed, but stiff specimen hands and dense fur cover made it difficult to tell.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Treated D3 as longest manual digit and D4 as longest pedal digit.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Pinniped interrelationships were derived from Park et al. 2024 [https://doi.org/10.1093/evolut/qpae061].
Ecotype_references: Pinnipeds are highly adapted to aquatic life, and spend most of their time in the water; they are active swimmers and dive to forage for their exclusively aquatic prey, but they also routinely haul onto land to rest, bask, and (in some species more than others) court and breed (Berta 2008 [Berta A (2008). Pinnipeds. In Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]; Bowen 2008 [Bowen WD (2008). Pinniped Ecology. In Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). As a result, we consider all pinnipeds Highly Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All pinnipeds were scored as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (following Cooper 2008 [Cooper LN. 2008. Forelimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). This scoring was supported by direct observation of Zalophus and Phoca specimens (YPM 03329: Phoca vitulina concolor, skin; AMNH_M_201648: Zalophus californianus, fluid-preserved). Otariid and odobenid foreflippers have digits that are outwardly indistinguishable from the flipper planform, and have limited independent mobility, whereas those of phocids are outwardly distinguishable and retain the ability to prominently pronate. Thus, phocid forelimbs were scored as Weakly Flippered, while otariid and odobenid forelimbs were considered Strongly Flippered. All pinniped hindlimbs have outwardly distinguishable digits, but those of otariids have more interdigital connective tissues, which stiffens them significantly (Adam 2008 [Adam PJ. 2008. Hindlimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). To reflect this pattern, otariid hindlimbs were considered Strongly Flippered, whereas odobenid and phocid hindlimbs were considered Weakly Flippered.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: D1 was longest digit. The unguals were very long, so excluding the unguals led to an underestimate of macropod length. The length of the acropod including the D1 ungual was 76.49+27.3 = 103.79. Juvenile (small and distal epiphyses all present but not fused yet.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Our phylogenetic placement of geosaurs was derived from Young and De Andrade 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-3642.2009.00536.x], Young et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-3642.2011.00734.x], and Johnson et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.9808].
Ecotype_references: Metriorhynchids show several adaptations for a fully pelagic marine lifestyle (Romer 1956, p. 345 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Young et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-3642.2011.00734.x]; Foffa et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rsos.231951]; Johnson et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.9808]). Their flippered limb morphology casts doubt on their ability to walk on land for extended periods, and their lack of dorsal ornamentation with thermoregulatory osteoderms suggests that they did not engage in routine basking behavior and perhaps instead relied on muscle contraction associated with near-constant swimming for heat production (Spindler et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.26879/1099]; de Araujo Sena & Cubo 2023 [https://doi.org/10.26879/1099]). However, we do not know whether they came onto land to lay their eggs, so while we do know that they were either highly or fully aquatic, we cannot resolve which of these designations is more accurate.
Limb_type_references: Geosaurus has highly derived "paddle-like" limbs with reduced perichondral ossification (Caldwell 1997 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/4523834]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773]). Both its fore- and its hindlimbs are highly osteologically modified for swimming. However, the relative extremity, in the forelimbs, of joint surface reduction and integration and zeugo- and autopodial elements into a single unit—based on observation of Geosaurus specimen photographs (WDC CSG-203) and reconstructions in Caldwell (1997, 2002) lead us to tentatively predict a relatively greater independent mobility of digits in the hindlimbs, so we tentatively score the forelimbs as Strongly Flippered and the hindlimbs as ambiguous.
Photo credit: Neal Immega (Houston Gem and Mineral Society), Levi Shinkle (Wyoming Dinosaur Center)
Included_unguals?: Yes (given previously published descriptions of limbs as being flipper-like)
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Note that the measured photograph, provided by Neal Immega, was scaled using the scalebar in the photograph provided by Levi Shinkle. This was done by stitching the photographs together in photoshop. Forelimb element assignments were difficult, but achieved following Caldwell 2002. Unguals were included in measurements, given that this species was fully flippered, on the tentative assumption that fully flippered forms are less likely to have keratinized ungual claws.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Eulipotyphla placement and topology: Asher and Hofreiter 2006 [DO1:10.1080/10635150500433649] ; Douady and Douzery 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780199535033.003.0077]; Dubey et al. 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2006.12.002] [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2006.12.002]; He et al. 2014 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2013.10.002]; Upham et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pbio.3000494]; Alvarez-Carretero et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-021-04341-1]
Ecotype_references: This talpid is an eager and excellent swimmer, and often has a diet often consisting primarily of aquatic prey; it typically inhabits swamps, streams, or the wetter regions of woods or meadows, but it seems to spend extensive time on land as well, foraging commonly (and, in some cases, primarily) for terrestrial prey, and building nests far above the water line using terrestrial vegetation (Petersen and Yates 1980 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3503812]; Reid 2006, Star-Nosed Mole, pp. 389–390 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]; Cassola 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-3.RLTS.T41458A22322697.en]). As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Skin specimens YPM MAM 04525 and YPM MAM 02199: hands unwebbed but with stiff paradigital scales; feet partially webbed
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured D3 (D2 was 6.9 mm, slightly longer)

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Eulipotyphla placement and topology: Asher and Hofreiter 2006 [DO1:10.1080/10635150500433649] ; Douady and Douzery 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780199535033.003.0077]; Dubey et al. 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2006.12.002] [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2006.12.002]; He et al. 2014 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2013.10.002]; Upham et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pbio.3000494]; Alvarez-Carretero et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-021-04341-1]
Ecotype_references: These semi-aquatic soricids spend much of their active time swimming, diving, and hunting for prey in the water, in addition to walking at times on the water’s surface; however, they typically spend just a few minutes at a time in the water, returning frequently to land to rest or sleep; otherwise, it spends extensive time on land, where it remains as mobile as terrestrial shrew species (Beneski and Stinson 1987 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3503968]; Reid 2006, American Water Shrew, pp. 364–365 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]). Because it spends extensive time both on land and in water, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Skin specimens YPM MAM 12349 and AMNH M-3861: hands unwebbed but with paradigital hairs; feet partially webbed. This is consistent with Fig. 4 of Beneski and Stinson 1987 [Beneski and Stinson 1987 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3503968].
Limb_region_measurement_notes: D3=8.99+1.74; D4=11.02. D4 longer. Foot measurement underestimates actual length of hindlimb autopod, which is longer (16.27 mm) due to the contribution of the elongate ankle to its length. THIS IS A GOOD EXAMPLE TO GIVE FOR LIMITATIONS OF OUR AZR MEASUREMENT SCHEME.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Eulipotyphla placement and topology: Asher and Hofreiter 2006 [DO1:10.1080/10635150500433649] ; Douady and Douzery 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780199535033.003.0077]; Dubey et al. 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2006.12.002] [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2006.12.002]; He et al. 2014 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2013.10.002]; Upham et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pbio.3000494]; Alvarez-Carretero et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-021-04341-1]
Ecotype_references: Although these shrews occupy a range of habitats, including marshes, bogs, and riparian zones, it primarily traverses wet meadows, rather than entering the water, and we find no reference to even transiently aquatic habits for this species (Gillihan and Foresman 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1644/744]; Reid 2006, Vagrant Shrew, pp. 375–376 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]).
Limb_type_references: Skin specimens AMNH M-40222 and AMNH M-20552
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured D3 (longest digit) for both hand and foot

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Eulipotyphla placement and topology: Asher and Hofreiter 2006 [DO1:10.1080/10635150500433649] ; Douady and Douzery 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780199535033.003.0077]; Dubey et al. 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2006.12.002] [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2006.12.002]; He et al. 2014 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2013.10.002]; Upham et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pbio.3000494]; Alvarez-Carretero et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-021-04341-1]
Ecotype_references: The European Hedgehog (Erinaceus europaeus) eats terrestrial invertebrates and spends almost no time in the water (Riber 2006 [https://doi.org/10.1007/BF03195183]; Amori 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-3.RLTS.T29650A2791303.en]). As a result, we call this species Terrestrial.
Limb_type_references: Skin specimens YPM MAM 001721 (E. europaeus) and YPM MAM 003262 (E. europaeus roumanicus): very slight partial webbing on hands and feet. Note that pedal webbing was only clear on the former specimen, where it appeared more prominent, so there may be intraspecific variation in webbing phenotype.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured D3 (longest digit) for both hand and foot

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: The phylogenetic placement of Scandentia is uncertain (see Alvarez-Carreterro et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-021-04341-1]), so we place it in a polytomy at the base of Euarchontoglires, following Blanga-Kanfi et al. 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2148-9-71]. Treeshrew topology derived from Olson et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1023/B:JOMM.0000029145.28207.6d].
Ecotype_references: Tree shrews are either arboreal or ground-dwelling (Langham 1982 [https://doi.org/10.1111/jzo.1982.197.3.323]; Sargis 2001 [https://doi.org/10.1017/S0952836901000449]). We find no reference to aquatic habits in the Scandentia, so we score them as Terrestrial.
Limb_type_references: Dry zoological (ie: skin) specimens of Tupaia sp. (YPM MAM 000312) and Tupaia_belangeri (YPM_MAM_000310, YPM_MAM_000311)
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Penultimate phalanx of mD3 missing so I couldn’t get an fAZR measurement. pD3 was longest and this one was measured.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Rodent interrelationships were synthesized from the following sources: Weisbecker and Schmid 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mambio.2006.03.005]; Blanga-Kanfi et al. 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2148-9-71]; Pérez 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2010.522432] [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2010.522432]; Fabre et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1463-6409.2012.00572.x]; Steppan and Schenk 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal. pone.0183070].
Ecotype_references: Like muskrats, these semi-aquatic rodents eat almost exclusively aquatic plants and spend nearly all of their time in bank burrows, simple reed nests, or appropriated muskrat lodges <4 m from the water, (D’Adamo et al. 2000; Guichón et al. 2003; Reid 2006, Coypu, pp. 339–340 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]). While their relatively unmodified tails and crude vegetation nests make them seem less aquatic than their muskrat and beaver counterparts, we find almost no reference to extensive overland travel in this species—although some road kills on major highways (Martino et al. 2008) suggest at least occasional journeys beyond their reported 10 m envelopes. A survey of coypu activity found that they spend the majority of their time (>80%) foraging near or within very shallow water, about 11% swimming between foraging sites, and about 9% resting or grooming (D’Adamo et al. 2000). Because they extensive time foraging un-submerged (i.e., either not fully swimming or beyond the water’s edge), we consider coypus Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Skin specimens AMNH M-42742 and YPM MAM 004400 had moderate-to-extensive partial pedal webbing, consistent with the description of coypu pedal webbing given by Reid (2006: Coypu, pp. 339–340 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Wasn't sure where to place proximalmost point of humerus for caliper measurement, so didn't measure humerus. Measured longest digit (D3) of both hand and foot.

Honors Thesis, Department of Biological Sciences, Florida State University. 44 pp.]; Panyutina et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1111/joa.13211] [https://doi.org/10.1111/joa.13211]). As a result, we consider this species Terrestrial.
Limb_type_references: Rather to our surprise, AMNH-M skin specimens of Anomalurus derbianus showed no obvious signs of webbing, though the stiffness and fur cover of the specimens made it very difficult to tell. Some other anomalure species do seem to have slight webbing between the proximal phalanges of at least some manual digits (Panyutina et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1111/joa.13211]). Because it was particularly difficult to determine webbing phenotype from the observed skin specimens, we score the webbing phenotype of this species “Unclear”.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database. The max reported occurrence age for the genus as a whole is 15.97 Ma.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured D3 (longest digit) for both hand and foot

As a result, we consider Atherurus macrurus Terrestrial.

Ecotype_references: All IUCN–assessed Hystrix spp. are described as occupying terrestrial environments (Amori and De Smet 2006 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-2.RLTS.T10746A22232484.en]; Amori and Aplin 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-2.RLTS.T10754A22231673.en]; Cassola 2016 [H. africaeaustralis: http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-3.RLTS.T10748A22232321.en]; Cassola 2016 [H. crassispinis: http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-2.RLTS.T10750A22232051.en]; Lunde et al. 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016- 3.RLTS.T10749A22232129.en]; Clayton 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018-1.RLTS.T10753A22231557.en]). However, at least one Hystrix cristata individuals have been strongly suggested to occassionally cross small bodies of water (e.g., an 8m stream) as needed (Pigozzi and Patterson 1990 [Pigozzi G, Patterson IJ (1990). Movements and diet of crested porcupines in the Maremma Natural Park, central Italy. Acta Theriologica. 35: 173-80.]). Assuming this behavior is rare but part of the typical repertoire for the species, we tentatively score  Hystrix cristata as "Transiently Aquatic" and consider all other species' ecotypes ambiguous.
Limb_type_references: Hystrix africae australis possesses complete slight manual webbing and complete slight-to-moderate pedal webbing (Pocock 1922 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-3642.1922.tb02149.x]). The scorings for other Hystrix spp. are based on this one, and thus remain tentative and require additional verification.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured D3, also longest

Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: Cane rats (Thryonomys spp.) often reside within vegetated wetland or fluvial habitats (Van Der Merwe 2007 [https://doi.org/10.3377/1562- 7020(2007)42[165:DBTSAT]2.0.CO;2]; Child 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-3.RLTS.T21847A22278009.en]; Cassola 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2017-2.RLTS.T21846A22277877.en]). They seem to consume primarily terrestrial vegetation (Aluko et al. 2015 [Aluko FA, Salako AE, Ngere LO, Eniolorunda OO (2015). Grasscutter: A review of the habitat, feeds and feeding, behaviour and economic importance. American Journal of Research Communicatio 3: 96–107.]). T. gregorianus is the less aquatic of the two, often inhabiting drier terrain with less permanent water courses (Van Der Merwe 2007 [https://doi.org/10.3377/1562- 7020(2007)42[165:DBTSAT]2.0.CO;2]). However, both species are reputedly good swimmers and use water as a regular means of escape when threatened (Aluko et al. 2015 [Aluko FA, Salako AE, Ngere LO, Eniolorunda OO (2015). Grasscutter: A review of the habitat, feeds and feeding, behaviour and economic importance. American Journal of Research Communicatio 3: 96–107.]). For this reason, we consider cane rats Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Thryonomys spp. lack interdigital webbing (Weisbecker and Schmid 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mambio.2006.03.005]).
Notes on occurrences: Extant. Recorded max age for T. swinderianus, of wich T. variegatus is a subspecies.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured D3, also longest

Ecotype_references: White and Alberico 1992; Roach 2017 (Dinomys branickii): We find no reference to aquatic habits in this species.
Limb_type_references: Dinomys spp. have complete, moderate pedal webbing and an ambiguous manual webbing phenotype (Grand and Eisenberg 1982 [Grand TI, Eisenberg JF (1982). On the affinities of the Dinomyidae. Saugetierkundliche Mitteilungen 40: 151–157.]). We scored the latter as “Unclear”. The more certain aspects of this phenotype were supported by direct observation of AMNH_M skin specimens of Dinomys_branickii.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured D3, also longest

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: Capybaras are a semi-aquatic species: They mate in water, and spend extensive time in water each day wallowing and foraging for aquatic plants, which make up a large portion of their diet (Herrera and MacDonald 1989 [https://doi.org/10.2307/4855]). However, they also spend extensive time each day resting and foraging out of water, and their diet and habitat use varies seasonally (Mones and Ojasti 1986 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3503784]; Herrera and MacDonald 1989 [https://doi.org/10.2307/4855]). Because they spend extensive time active in both land and water, we consider capybaras Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Capybaras have moderate complete webbing on both hands and feet (Mones and Ojasti 1986 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3503784]). This was supported by direct observation Hydrochoerus hydrochaeris skin specimens AMNH_M_14226 and YPM_MAM_017039, which also had webbed paws.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. Other Hydrochoerus specimens are older.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit for both hand and foot. For foot, this was D3. For hand, this was D4. mD3 length: 78.82.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Rodent interrelationships were synthesized from the following sources: Weisbecker and Schmid 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mambio.2006.03.005]; Blanga-Kanfi et al. 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2148-9-71]; Pérez 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2010.522432] [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2010.522432]; Fabre et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1463-6409.2012.00572.x]; Steppan and Schenk 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal. pone.0183070].
Ecotype_references: Domesticated guinea pigs are typically considered sedentary terrestrial animals (Dagg and Windsor 1971 [https://doi.org/10.1139/z72-019]; Barbera et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ar.24271]). Although wild cavies (C. aperea) readily take to the water and have been reported to swim several kilometers during intense floods (Lee 2010 [Lee Y (2010). Guinea Pigs. Chapter 8, in: Tynes VV (ed), Behavior of Exotic Pets. Blackwell Publishing: West Sussex. pp. 78–90.]), we find no reference to aquatic habits in the domesticated C. porcellus. As a result, while we would consider C. aperea Transiently Aquatic, we call C. porcellus Terrestrial.
Limb_type_references: C. porcellus hands and feet are completely unwebbed but with fleshy palm/sole pads (Barbera et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ar.24271]). Based on this report and a cursory examination of AMNH_M skin specimens from other Cavia spp., we tentatively score this species as unwebbed with interdigital girth.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured D3, also longest

Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]; Cassola 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-3.RLTS.T42650A22371112.en]). In the absence of additional information, it remains unclear to us whether this species is Terrestrial or Transiently Aquatic.

Ecotype_references: Much like their round-tailed cousins, these semi-aquatic rodents consume almost entirely aquatic food, build intricate lodges within the water column, and spend the vast majority of their time in, on, or very near the water (Chapman 1889 [http://hdl.handle.net/2246/1699]; Proulx and Gilbert 1983 [https://doi.org/10.5962/p.355039]; Reid 2006, Common Muskrat: pp. 326–327 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]). While they seem to be more active in the water than round-tailed muskrats (Chapman 1889 [http://hdl.handle.net/2246/1699]), and are sometimes broadly considered more aquatic (Reid 2006, Round-Tailed Muskrat, pp. 325–326 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]), they may still travel extended distances over land to find new suitable habitats. Yearling animals typically do so in the spring. As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic. Muskrats prefer slow-moving water bodies (Proulx and Gilbert 1983 [https://doi.org/10.5962/p.355039]).
Limb_type_references: Partial hand and foot webbing evident in the majority of observed skin specimens, including AMNH_16204, AMNH_122697, and AMNH_129223. However, there seems to be some intraspecific variation, because the foot of skin specimen AMNH_70591 is unwebbed and lacks stiff paradigital integument.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (D2). D3 length was 46.41.

Ecotype_references: These semi-aquatic rodents seem to spend the majority of their time in the shallow densely vegetated water of marshes, where they consume almost exclusively aquatic plants and build intricate dome-shaped houses and feeding platforms within the water column (Birkenholz 1972 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3503816]; Cassolla 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-2.RLTS.T14520A22356567.en]). They seem to actively swim less than Ondatra zibethica (Chapman 1889 [http://hdl.handle.net/2246/1699]; Reid 2006, Round-Tailed Muskrat, pp. 325–326 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]), but nevertheless spend much of their lives in or on the water upon their vegetated constructions.  We cannot find a clear source on how frequently this species ventures onto land, but the frequency of roadkill reports, some hundreds of yards or more from the nearest pond (Birkenholz 1963 [https://doi.org/10.2307/1942628]), suggest that individuals will travel great distances over land to find suitable aquatic habitat. As a result, we tentatively score this species as Moderately Aquatic. If these terrestrial forays are actually rare, we would consider the species Highly Aquatic instead.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: Although this species occupies terrestrial environments (Reid 2006, American Badger, pp. 470–471 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]), the American Badger is a good swimmer (Long 1973 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504047]), and at least two observations have clarified that it will readily travel great distances over water (Wood 1921 [https://doi.org/10.1093/jmammal/2.3.170]; Duebbert 1967 [https://doi.org/10.2307/1378046]), for reasons that remain unclear. Assuming this activity is infrequent but within their normal range of behaviors, we consider the American Badger to be Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Taxidea spp. were scored based on “Taxidea americana” skin specimen AMNH_02842, which appeared unwebbed. However, Long 1973 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504047] reports that badgers have a partially webbed manus (and presumably unwebbed feet). This description of the manus conflicts with our observation on skin specimens, so we score the hand’s webbing phenotype as “Unclear”.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (=D3). Listed in the collection as “Taxidea americana”, a synonym for Taxidea taxus.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: Although this species occupies terrestrial environments (Reid 2006, American Badger, pp. 470–471 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]), the American Badger is a good swimmer (Long 1973 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504047]), and at least two observations have clarified that it will readily travel great distances over water (Wood 1921 [https://doi.org/10.1093/jmammal/2.3.170]; Duebbert 1967 [https://doi.org/10.2307/1378046]), for reasons that remain unclear. Assuming this activity is infrequent but within their normal range of behaviors, we consider the American Badger to be Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Taxidea spp. were scored based on “Taxidea americana” skin specimen AMNH_02842, which appeared unwebbed. However, Long (1973) reports that badgers have a partially webbed manus (and presumably unwebbed feet). This description of the manus conflicts with our observation on skin specimens, so we score the hand’s webbing phenotype as “Unclear”.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (=D3) for both hand and foot.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: Although this species occupies terrestrial environments (Virgós et al. 2012 [https://doi.org/10.7591/9780801466076-014]; Proulx and Aubry 2020 [Proulx G, Aubry KB (2020). The Martes Complex: opportunities for developing multi-species management and conservation strategies. Canadian Wildlife Biology & Management 9: 49–67.]), it has (much like the American badger) been reported to readily cross large water bodies (Clark et al. 1987 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3503918]). Assuming this behavior is infrequent but typical, we consider Martes spp. Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Martens have complete interdigital webbing, which varies in extent but may reach the proximal end of the toe pads (Kitchener et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780198759805.003.0003]). We directly examined Martes skin specimens AMNH_70236 (Martes foina) and AMNH_120687 (Martes origenes), but were unable to determine their webbing phenotype due to their stiffness and the extent of fur cover.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (=D3) for both hand and foot.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Mustelinae was placed following Law 2019 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2019.0155]. Due to ongoing taxonomic controversy regarding the genera that make up Mustelinae (see Patterson et al. 2021 [http://dx.doi.org/10.29252/JAD.2021.3.2.1]), we place all Mustela/Neovison/Neogale specimens in an unresolved polytomy.
Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: The aquatic affinity of the European Polecat is a little unclear (Blandford 1987 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2907.1987.tb00282.x]). Some sources report that it avoids and refrains from entering water, while others highlight its great swimming ability and high affinity for water (reviewed in Blandford 1987 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2907.1987.tb00282.x]), or its tendency to reside by the water's edge (Skumatov et al. 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-1.RLTS.T41658A45214384.en]). These reports suggest intraspecific and perhaps geographic variation in polecat habitat preferences (Blandford 1987 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2907.1987.tb00282.x]). Because there are nevertheless reports of these animals readily traversing water and consuming some aquatic prey (Blandford 1987 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2907.1987.tb00282.x]), we consider the species Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Mustela specimens AMNH_243106, AMNH_36632, and YPM_01479: possible slight webbing between pD3 and pD4, but very difficult to tell. However, given that nearly all  mustelids have complete interdigital webbing, which varies in extent but may reach the proximal end of the toe pads (Kitchener et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780198759805.003.0003]), we tentatively scored this species as having complete, slight webbing.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (=D3) for both hand and foot. Habitat score (terrestrial) dubious––maybe more terrestrial-to-slightly-aquatic.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Mustelinae was placed following Law 2019 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2019.0155]. Due to ongoing taxonomic controversy regarding the genera that make up Mustelinae (see Patterson et al. 2021 [http://dx.doi.org/10.29252/JAD.2021.3.2.1]), we place all Mustela/Neovison/Neogale specimens in an unresolved polytomy.
Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: The American Mink regularly hunts for aquatic prey, including waterfowl, aquatic arthropods and fish, along with terrestrial Pan-Mammalian prey, and spends extensive time both on land and in water (Larivière 1999 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504420]; Reid et al. 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016- 1.RLTS.T41661A45214988.en]). As a result, we consider the species Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Taxidermied specimen YPM_01690 clearly has slight-to-extensive webbing on both hands and feet. This is consistent with Larivière 1999 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504420], who reports slight webbing in this species. Given that nearly all  mustelids have complete interdigital webbing, which varies in extent but may reach the proximal end of the toe pads (Kitchener et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780198759805.003.0003]), we tentatively scored this species as having complete, slight webbing.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (=D3) for both hand and foot. Habitat score (slightly aquatic) assigned given that it doesn’t spend all or most of its day in the water and it can't dive. Though it's more like slightly-to-moderately aquatic.

Ecotype_references: Although primarily aquatic, Lutra spp. spend extensive time out of the water and occasionally make extended overland journeys between water courses (Carss 1995 [https://doi.org/10.4404/hystrix-7.1-2-4069]; Sasaki et al. 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-3.RLTS.T12421A164579488.en]; Loy et al. 2022 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2022-2.RLTS.T12419A218069689.en]). As a result, we consider them Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Lutra spp. scorings based on fluid-preserved specimen AMNH_202460
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (=D3) for both hand and foot.

Ecotype_references: Although primarily aquatic, Lutra spp. spend extensive time out of the water and occasionally make extended overland journeys between water courses (Carss 1995 [https://doi.org/10.4404/hystrix-7.1-2-4069]; Sasaki et al. 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-3.RLTS.T12421A164579488.en]; Loy et al. 2022 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2022-2.RLTS.T12419A218069689.en]). As a result, we consider them Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Lutra spp. scorings based on fluid-preserved specimen AMNH_202460
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit: D3 for hand, D2 for foot. Specimen was juvenile (long bone epiphyses unfused to shaft.

Ecotype_references: Although primarily aquatic, Aonyx cinereus regularly spends extensive time on land, and will occasionally travel far from water sources, so we consider it Moderately Aquatic (see Larivière 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1644/720]; Wright et al. 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-3.RLTS.T44166A164580923.en]).
Limb_type_references: AMNH_M_skin specimens of Amblyonyx spp.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (=D3) for both hand and foot. Listed in collections as “Amblyonyx cinerea”, a synonym of Aonyx cinereus.

Ecotype_references: This highly aquatic species seldom leaves the water, only venturing onto land for short periods during intense storms; although primarily near-shore marine, this species may inhabit high-energy coastal systems, often around 30m deep, and max diving depths have exceeded 90m, so we consider this species to occupy large/fast water bodies (Reid 2006, pp. 472–473, Sea Otter [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]; Doroff et al. 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-3.RLTS.T7750A219377647.en]).
Limb_type_references: Hands have moderate complete webbing. Feet have complete, extensive webbing. The feet are so highly webbed as to be near-flippered. Indeed, Reid (2006, p. 472) calls their hindlimbs flippers [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]. To address this midground, we score them as non-flippered in limb type (given that the webbing does not functionally integrate the auto- and zeugopodia into a continuous unit) and weakly flippered in limb subtype. Scored based on high-resolution photographs provided by Michael Durham (Oregon Zoo, Oregon, USA)
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit: D3 for hand, D5 for foot. pD3 length 154.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: The Pygmy Hippopotamus (Choeropsis liberiensis) might be considered less aquatic than its larger counterpart (Hippopotamus amphibius), as it gives birth on land—although less is known about its natural history (Ransom et al. 2015 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2015- 2.RLTS.T10032A18567171.en]). However, its range is limited to marshes and water courses, and it still spends extensive time in an aquatic medium, foraging for semi-aquatic plants, suckling pups, and (in some cases at least) copulating in the water. As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Hippopotamids were scored using fluid-preserved Choeropsis specimens AMNH_214396 and AMNH_146608, which had very slight webbing between D3 and D4.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (D4 for hand; mD3 length = 132.32; and D3 for foot). Osteologically not fully matured (acropodial epiphyses not fully fused). Not sure about webbing score—need clarify. Habitat score tough to say—between slightly aquatic and moderately aquatic.

Ecotype_references: The Common Hippopotamus is well-adapted to a semi-aquatic lifestyle (Van Der Geer et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1111/let.12095]). It spends the majority of the day standing or bottom-walking in the shallow water of rivers, coastal lakes, and marine estuaries (Fisher et al. 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ar.20531]; Lewison and Pluhácek 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2017- 2.RLTS.T10103A18567364.en]; Endo et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1111/ahe.12405]). They typically leave the water each night to forage on land for terrestrial grasses, which comprise a large part of their diet (Lewison and Pluhácek 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2017- 2.RLTS.T10103A18567364.en]). They have also been known to travel several kilometers overland at night (Clarke 1953 [https://doi.org/10.2307/1375838]). Given its extended daily activity both in water and on land, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic. Because it is typically bottom-walking, we consider it to inhabit relatively small or slow-moving water bodies that do not require surface swimming against strong currents in deep water.
Limb_type_references: Hippopotamids were scored using fluid-preserved Choeropsis specimens AMNH_214396 and AMNH_146608, which had very slight webbing between D3 and D4. However, Hippopotamus may have somewhat more pronounced webbing, and as we could not directly observe any zoological Hippopotamus specimens, this scoring remains tentative.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (D3 for hand, D_ for foot). Juvenile (not fully osteologically mature: long bone epiphyses ossified but not yet fused).

Ecotype_references: Pinnipeds are highly adapted to aquatic life, and spend most of their time in the water; they are active swimmers and dive to forage for their exclusively aquatic prey, but they also routinely haul onto land to rest, bask, and (in some species more than others) court and breed (Berta 2008 [Berta A (2008). Pinnipeds. In Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]; Bowen 2008 [Bowen WD (2008). Pinniped Ecology. In Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). As a result, we consider all pinnipeds Highly Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All pinnipeds were scored as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (following Cooper 2008 [Cooper LN. 2008. Forelimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). This scoring was supported by direct observation of Zalophus and Phoca specimens (YPM 03329: Phoca vitulina concolor, skin; AMNH_M_201648: Zalophus californianus, fluid-preserved). Otariid and odobenid foreflippers have digits that are outwardly indistinguishable from the flipper planform, and have limited independent mobility, whereas those of phocids are outwardly distinguishable and retain the ability to prominently pronate. Thus, phocid forelimbs were scored as Weakly Flippered, while otariid and odobenid forelimbs were considered Strongly Flippered. All pinniped hindlimbs have outwardly distinguishable digits, but those of otariids have more interdigital connective tissues, which stiffens them significantly (Adam 2008 [Adam PJ. 2008. Hindlimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). To reflect this pattern, otariid hindlimbs were considered Strongly Flippered, whereas odobenid and phocid hindlimbs were considered Weakly Flippered.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens of this subspecies are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (D1). D3 length was 72.16. mD1 Acropod measurement underestimates true length because it excludes ungual phalanx, which is very long. With ungual, it was 133.91.

Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digits (mD1 and pD5). Ecotype_references: Though they spend the majority (a ball-parked “two-thirds”) of their time in the water (Fay 1982 [https://doi.org/10.3996/nafa.74.0001]), walruses also frequently haul out onto land to court mates, rest, bask, and give birth (Reid 2006, pp. 475–476, Walrus [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]). They have limited terrestrial mobility, hunting and mating almost exclusively in the water.
Limb_type_references: All pinnipeds were scored as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (following Cooper 2008 [Cooper LN. 2008. Forelimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). This scoring was supported by direct observation of Zalophus and Phoca specimens (YPM 03329: Phoca vitulina concolor, skin; AMNH_M_201648: Zalophus californianus, fluid-preserved). Otariid and odobenid foreflippers have digits that are outwardly indistinguishable from the flipper planform, and have limited independent mobility, whereas those of phocids are outwardly distinguishable and retain the ability to prominently pronate. Thus, phocid forelimbs were scored as Weakly Flippered, while otariid and odobenid forelimbs were considered Strongly Flippered. All pinniped hindlimbs have outwardly distinguishable digits, but those of otariids have more interdigital connective tissues, which stiffens them significantly (Adam 2008 [Adam PJ. 2008. Hindlimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). To reflect this pattern, otariid hindlimbs were considered Strongly Flippered, whereas odobenid and phocid hindlimbs were considered Weakly Flippered.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Included unguals in all acropod measurements because foreflippers are effectively unclawed. Humerus and ulna measurements more approximate because they were longer than the length of my calipers so I had to measure them in two sections. Acropod measurement taken from longest digit (D1) on ImageJ (because the collections were closing and I didn’t have time to caliper-measure it), using a MetroCard as my scale and assuming a standard NYC MetroCard length of 85.7mm.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Gatesy et al. 2013 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2012.10.012].; Hassanin et al. 2012
Ecotype_references: Little is written about the ecology and natural history of Tragulus javanicus (see Matsubayashi et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1644/1545-1542(2003)084<0234:AAHUOL>2.0.CO;2]; Duckworth et al. 2015 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2015- 2.RLTS.T41780A61978138.en]). Other tragulids (Hyemoschus and Moschiola spp., as well as Tragulus napu) are known to reside near water and readily take to the water as a means of escape when threatened, though they are predominantly terrestrial (Thewissen et al. 2009; Meijaard et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mambio.2009.05.007]). While similar behavior is expected in all tragulids, it has yet to be verified in T. javanicus and T. kanchil. As a result, the aquatic habits of this species remain unclear, and we cannot resolve whether it counts as Terrestrial or Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: No Tragulus zoological specimens were available for study, and no observations were sufficiently clear on iNaturalist to score webbing phenotype, so we scored it as “Unclear”.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (=D4) for both hand and foot. mD3 length was 36.79+7.73+1.99

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: This taxon is considered a mesonychid (YPM Collections Database: https://collections.peabody.yale.edu/search/Record/YPM-VP-010935). The phylogenetic placement and monophyly of Mesonychia remains disputed, but following a recent analysis (Yu et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1080/14772019.2023.2189436]), we treat Mesonychia as a lineage of basal pan-ungulates, branching off before the Perissodactyl-Artiodactyl split.
Ecotype_references: While the morphology of the specimen provides no suggestion of aquatic habits, too little is known about this taxon for us to resolve its ecotype.
Limb_type_references: NA
Citation for occurrences: YPM Collections Database (https://collections.peabody.yale.edu/search/Record/YPM-VP-010935); Murphey and Evanoff 2011 [Murphey PC, Evanoff EM (2011). Paleontology and stratigraphy of the middle Eocene Bridger Formation, southern Green River basin, Wyoming. In: Proceedings of the 9th Conference on Fossil Resources. pp. 83-109.]
Notes on occurrences: This specimen is from the Bridger Frm. (https://collections.peabody.yale.edu/search/Record/YPM-VP-010935), which dates from 49–45.5 Ma (Murphey and Evanoff 2011 [Murphey PC, Evanoff EM (2011). Paleontology and stratigraphy of the middle Eocene Bridger Formation, southern Green River basin, Wyoming. In: Proceedings of the 9th Conference on Fossil Resources. pp. 83-109.]).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (=D3) for both hand and foot. pD3 length was difficult to obtain, due to the position of the mounted skeleton, which made the pD3 metacarpal difficult to measure with calipers, so this portion of the pD3 measurement has a greater error margin than usual. This specimen belongs to Mesonychia. Taxonomy source: https://collections.peabody.yale.edu/search/Record/YPM-VP-010935

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Rio and Mannion 2021 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.12094]. Placed in a polytomy at the base of Alligatoridae with the other species-unresolved alligatorid specimens.
Ecotype_references: Wilberg et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-36795-1] report no major changes in habitat preference over the evolutionary history of crown Alligatoroidea. Given that we call all modern alligatorids “Moderately Aquatic” and inhabiting a range of low- and high-energy aquatic habitats, we do the same for this extinct Alligator species. However, in the absence of additional data, this scoring remains speculative.
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Tibia in position that made it difficult to measure, so this length measurement is dubious. Measured D3 for both hand and foot.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Leptomeryx is a basal ruminant that was recently recovered within Tragulina (Ducrocq et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2019.1722682]), so we tentatively place it within Tragulina.
Ecotype_references: While the morphology of the specimen provides no suggestion of aquatic habits, too little is known about this taxon for us to resolve its ecotype.
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Distal ulna broken, so estimated ulna length by assuming same distolateralmost point as that of radius. Measured longest digit (mD4).

Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurs) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1949, p. 73; Thewissen and Taylor 2007; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurs as “Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: All ichthyosaurs are commonly described as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (eg, Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773]; Motani 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2005)025[0462:DTMIAD]2.0.CO;2]; Maxwell 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jez.b.22459]; Gutarra and Rahman 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1111/brv.12790]); Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]. These flippers were highly integrated structures [Fernández et al. 2020 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2020.0281], and presumably encased in dense connective tissue like those of modern-day cetaceans (Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]), so that their digits were not independently mobile.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured D3 as third full digit (including metacarpal) from the anterior margin of the hand (ignoring polyphalangeal partial digits). AZR probably underestimated, as the distal most phalanges of D3 may be missing.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Ketchum and Benson 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1469-185X.2009.00107.x] used for topology and interrelations of Plesiosauria. . Placed Muraenosaurus, Trinacromerum, and Dolichorhynchops in a polytomy following Ketchum and Benson 2010, Fig. 5A.
Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurs) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1949, p. 73; Thewissen and Taylor 2007; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurs as “Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: Plesiosaurs had fully fledged fore- and hindflippers, as supported by both osteological evidence and exceptional soft-tissue preservation (Huxley 1871 [Huxley TH (1871) A Manual of the Anatomy of Vertebrated Animals. J. & A. Churchill: London. 510 pp. Accessed online via URL = https://archive.org/details/b21933236]; Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Caldwell 1997 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/4523834]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773];  Frey et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.18268/BSGM2017v69n1a5]; Vincent et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-017-1472-6]). Musculoskeletal reconstructions suggest that plesiosaurs could not move their digits independently (Krahl and Witzel 2021 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.12537]), so we score them as "Strongly Flippered".
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured D3 as third full digit (including metacarpal) from the anterior margin of the hand, as with all others.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Heritage et al. 2021 [Heritage S, Seiffert ER, Borths MR (2021). Recommended fossil calibrators for time-scaled molecular phylogenies of Afrotheria. Afrotherian Conservation 17. Accessed via URL=https://www.afrotheria.net/newsletter.php.2021.]
Ecotype_references: Sirenians are fully aquatic; they are unable to travel on land, bearing their young and spending their entire lives in the water; Trichechus inunguis is predominantly freshwater, whereas all other extant Trichechus species readily travel between fresh and saltwater habitats, and Dugongs are exclusively marine (Domning 2008 [Domning DP (2008). Sirenian Evolution. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]).
IUCN_source: Deutsch et al. 2008 (Trichechus manatus: http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2008.RLTS.T22106A9359881.en). Note that the species as a whole is VU, but each subspecies is EN.
Limb_type_references: All sirenians have strongly flippered forelimbs (Cooper 2008 [Cooper LN. 2008. Forelimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]) and lack hindlimbs altogether (Adam 2008 [Adam PJ. 2008. Hindlimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). This was supported by direct observation of Trichechus specimens (AMNH_M_S-03: Trichechus_inunguis, fluid-preserved; AMNH_202525 and AMNH_201322: Trichechus_sp, fluid-preserved) and high-resolution photographs of Trichechus manatus latirostris in the wild, provided by Meghan Cohorst: Save the Manatee Club, FL, USA).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Calipers opened to 1 cm (10 mm) (John Ososky, pers. comm.). Measured longest digit (D4) excluding ungual, since D2–D4 have associated fingernails in this species (Cooper 2008). D5 measurement (postmost) included the ungual since this digit has no associated fingernail (Cooper 2008). D5 with the ungual was longer than D4 without it, but D4 was longer altogether, so it was chosen as the longest digit.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: The phylogenetic placement and monophyly of parareptiles have recently been disputed (see Ford and Benson 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41559-019-1047-3]; Simões et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.abq1898]: Science Advances). Our placement of Mesosaurs and other parareptiles within Pan-Reptilia varies in our different supertrees to reflect existing disagreements (see Methods and Supplementary Text). Piñeiro et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.4072/rbp.2021.3.04] clarify that Mesosaurus tenuidens is the only valid taxon of mesosaurid currently known, so we have renamed all the mesosaurid samples in our data set accordingly.
Ecotype_references: Although mesosaurs were viviparous (Piñeiro et al. 2012 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1080/08912963.2012.662230]) and clearly adapted for aquatic life in marine systems (Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Demarco et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.3389/fevo.2018.00109]), aspects of their vertebral morphometry have alluded to a more semi-aquatic lifestyle, with considerable time spent both in water and on land (Demarco et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.3389/fevo.2018.00109]; Demarco et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.3389/frym.2019.00039]). Adult mesosaurs may have occupied pelagic environments (Holmes 1953 [https://doi.org/10.1038/171669a0]; Verriere and Frobisch 2022 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.13866]), but we lack direct evidence concerning whether and to what extent they actually traversed on land, so it remains unclear whether mesosaurs were Moderately, Highly, or Fully Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All mesosaurids were scored as having extensive, complete pedal webbing, based on exceptional soft-tissues preserved on the hindlimb skeletons of the following mesosaurid specimens: SMNK_PAL_3806, SMF_R_4710, BSPG_1964_I_268, and URCR_064. Scorings were based on high-resolution photographs of the specimens, provided by Antoine Vérriere. Less soft-tissue preservation was discernible on forelimbs, but manual webbing phenotype was tentatively scored based on specimen NHMW_1987_0067_0001, which appears to have complete extensive webbing. Because the taxonomic breadth covered by these specimens remains unclear, the application of these scores to all sampled mesosaurids remains a tentative first approximation, as mesosaurids may have more variability in webbing phenotype than we can currently discern based on available specimens.
Photo credit: Antoine Verrière, Museum für Naturkunde, Leibniz-Institut für Evolutions- und Biodiversitätsforschung, Berlin, Germany

The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]; Pol and Goloboff 2020 [http://hdl.handle.net/11336/170477]; Madzia et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.12362]; Fabbri et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04528-0].
Ecotype_references: The aquatic affinities of Spinosaurus aegyptiacus are notoriously fraught. The species likely consumed fish (Brusatte 2021 and references therein [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cub.2021.09.002]), and was thus at least slightly aquatic. Isotopic data supports a somewhat aquatic lifestyle for many spinosaurids, which have ∂18 oxygen phosphate signatures more similar to semi-aquatic crocodilians and turtles than to other (presumably terrestrial) theropods (Amiot et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1130/G30402.1]). However, it remains unclear precisely how aquatic spinosaurids really were. Several morphological and histological features have supported more extreme aquatic habits in Spinosaurus, but it remains disputed whether Spinosaurus was merely a wading shoreline piscivore (Sereno et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.80092]) or a highly aquatic pursuit predator (Ibrahim et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-020-2190-3]). On the one hand, tall neural spines on the caudal vertebrae supported a paddle-like tail that may acted as an efficient propulsive organ in water (Ibrahim et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-020-2190-3]), and the bone compactness of its femur falls within the range of modern diving or highly aquatic reptiles (Fabbri et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04528-0]). On the other hand, most 3D computer models of Spinosaurus have found it to be a competent walker on land, and a highly buoyant, slow, and inefficient swimmer (Henderson 2018 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.5409]; Sereno et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.80092]). Moreover, at least two Spinosaurus specimens have been identified in inland fluvial deposits, suggesting a tolerance for less permanently aquatic and non-marine habitats (Sereno et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.80092]). From these conflicting lines of evidence, we cannot resolve whether the species is Transiently, Moderately, or Highly Aquatic. As a result, the ecotype of this species remains ambiguous. Given its many apparent morphological adaptations to life in the water, we tentatively score the limb type of the species as "Unclear, aquat.", although a transiently aquatic lifestyle would merit rescoring as "Unclear, terr." Either way, all researchers agree on some significant aquatic affinity for the species, and it has been associated with freshwater fish faunas (Ibrahim et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-020-2190-3]; Fabbri et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04528-0]).
