John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]; Pol and Goloboff 2020 [http://hdl.handle.net/11336/170477]; Madzia et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.12362]; Fabbri et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04528-0].
Ecotype_references: The highly modified cranial anatomy of Suchomimus suggests that it was adapted for consuming fish (Arden et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cretres.2018.06.013]), but the absence of obvious postcranial adaptations for aquatic life (Arden et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cretres.2018.06.013]) and the level of bone compactness of its femur (Fabbri et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04528-0]) suggest that it was not an avid swimmer or diver. As a result, the species is usually considered a non-subaqueous forager with a Transiently Aquatic lifestyle. However, in the absence of additional evidence, we cannot rule out a purely Terrestrial lifestyle, so the precise ecotype of the taxon remains unclear.

Ecotype_references: Like other cryptobranchids, the Chinese Giant Salamander is considered a fully aquatic species, which reproduces, spawns, and metamorphoses within the water, and then spends most if not all of its time as an adult submerged; though it does consume a combination of aquatic and terrestrial prey, the vast majority of its prey (when considered by number) is aquatic (Liang et al. 2004 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2004.RLTS.T1272A3375181.en]; Browne et al. 2014 [Browne RK, Li H, Wang Z, Okada S, Hime P, McMillan A, Wu M, Diaz R, McGinnity D, Briggler JT (2013). The giant salamanders (Cryptobranchidae): Part B. Biogeography, ecology and reproduction. Amphibian and Reptile Conservation 5(4): 30-50.]; Vitt and Caldwell 2014, Cryptobranchidae, pp. 459–460 [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). Although it could presumably voyage onto land with its well-developed limbs, we could find no mention of overland travel in this species. As a result, we consider it Fully Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Few observations present on iNaturalist (https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/26813-Andrias-davidianus) suggest at least partial slight webbing on hand but extent and spread of webbing is unclear; pedal webbing phenotype unclear from existing iNaturalist observations.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.

Ungual_measurement_notes: Unguals insufficiently ossified or mesh too low-resolution to take measurements on them.

As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic.

Ecotype_references: Black Spiny-Tailed Iguanas (Ctenosaura similis) spend most of their time on land, but occupy a range of habitats near and far from water (Pasachnik 2015 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2015-1.RLTS.T174480A73611567.en]). They will readily dive into the water (or into burrows or bushes) when threatened (Briggs-Gonzalez et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1656/058.022.0303]), and have even been reported to engage in intertidal foraging (Furness 2021 [https://doi.org/10.17161/randa.v28i3.15892]). However, the presumed rarity of this behavior leads us to consider Black Spiny-Tailed Iguanas Transiently Aquatic.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: Bonobos are primarily terrestrial. They are apparently bad swimmers, which avoid crossing the Congo River (Takemoto et al. 2015 [https://doi.org/10.1002/evan.21456]). However, they also occassionally forage up to their abdomen in ponds and streams, where they may catch aquatic prey (Badrian and Malenky 1984 [chapter 11 in Susman RL (ed), The Pygmy Chimpanzee: Evolutionary Biology and Behavior. Plenum Press: New York. 435 pp.]) ; Fruth et al. 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-2.RLTS.T15932A17964305.en]). Moreover, ancestral bonobo populations are thought to have crossed the Congo River during times of lower river discharge in the ancient past (Takemoto et al. 2015 [https://doi.org/10.1002/evan.21456]). For these reasons, we consider the species Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Bonobo manus completely unwebbed (van Leeuwen 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1111/joa.12841]; van Leeuwen 2021 [van Leeuwen T (2021) Anatomical adaptations to varying functional demands in the primate thumb: An integrated anatomical and biomechanical study of bonobos, gibbons, and rhesus macacques. Doctor in Biomedical Sciences Dissertation, Department of Development and Regeneration, KU Leuven. 281 pp.]). Pedal webbing phenotype unclear—no sufficiently clear iNaturalist observations or published images to verify soft-tissue phenotype.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil Pan specimens are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measurements in millimeters. Measured longest digit (mD3, pD3), excluding unguals as in all other clawed mammals (assuming Bonobos have keratinized finger nails/claws).
Ungual_measurement_notes: Phalanges to low resolution to measure. Unguals surprisingly flat in many primates. Due to reduction of claws to fingernails?
proxp_measurement_notes: Phalanges to low resolution to measure.

Ecotype_references: Chameleons are collectively described as occupying terrestrial habitats, with no reference to semi-aquatic habits (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, pp. 583–584: Chamaeleonidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). To our knowledge, there are no known extant chameleon species with even transiently semi-aquatic lifestyles. Oceanic dispersal from Madagascar to mainland Africa has been suggested for some species (Raxworthy et al. 2002 [https://doi.org/10.1038/415784a]), but this was presumably accomplished by floating on vegetation rafts, rather than swimming. This species in particular is primarily terrestrial, occupying forest bushes and leaf litter (Tolley and Menegon 2014 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2014- 3.RLTS.T172520A1344134.en]).
Limb_type_references: Chameleons have stiff syndactylous finger pairs / triads (see Molnar et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jmor.20708]). Ecotype_references: Platypuses are highly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle (Pasitschniak-Arts and Marinelli 1998 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504433]). They excavate resting burrows on the banks of water bodies, with entrances below the water line, and feed almost entirely on aquatic (benthic) invertebrates (Pasitschniak-Arts and Marinelli 1998 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504433]). Platypuses spend a little under half of their time (avg 10 h/day) foraging in the water, and the rest of their time (an estimated 12–17 h/day) resting in their burrow dens (Serena 1994 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1469-7998.1994.tb01563.x]; Pasitschniak-Arts and Marinelli 1998 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504433]). However, they may occasionally spend more extended periods of time (3–7 days reported) in their dens out of the water (Serena 1994), and in some populations, individuals will travel significant distances (7–8 km reported) overland to migrate between neighboring river basins (Furlan et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10592-013-0478-7]). As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic. Platypuses inhabit a range of fast- and slow-moving water bodies (Pasitschniak-Arts and Marinelli 1998 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3504433]).
Limb_type_references: Skin specimen YPM_10846: Completely webbed. The feet have complete moderate-to-extensive webbing with extensive snowshoeing along the posterior margin of the ankle. The hands have complete and very extensive webbing beyond the distal most tips of the unguals and extending beyond the contours of the hand.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measurements in millimeters. Measured longest digit (mD4, pD5). mD3/mD4 and pD4/pD5 were very close in length.
Ungual_measurement_notes: Unguals have flattened proximolateral processes and distolateral ridges not captured by measurement scheme. No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype_references: Chameleons are collectively described as occupying terrestrial habitats, with no reference to semi-aquatic habits (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, pp. 583–584: Chamaeleonidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). To our knowledge, there are no known extant chameleon species with even transiently semi-aquatic lifestyles. Oceanic dispersal from Madagascar to mainland Africa has been suggested for some species (Raxworthy et al. 2002 [https://doi.org/10.1038/415784a]), but this was presumably accomplished by floating on vegetation rafts, rather than swimming.
Limb_type_references: Chameleons have stiff syndactylous finger pairs / triads (see Molnar et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jmor.20708]). We verified the presence of these "pincer-palms" with several fluid-preserved chameleon specimens (Bradypodion fischeri: YPM_HERR_009976, Brookesia perarmata: YPM_HERR_011451, Calumma brevicornis: YPM_HERR_009788, Chamaeleo deremensis: YPM R 10314).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: The phylogenetic relationships of litopterns and notoungulates remain disputed (see Chimento and Agnolin 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-020-70287-5]; Perini et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10914-021-09583-4]). We tentatively follow the molecular tree topology (Welker et al 2015 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature14249]), which  places the Notoungulata and Litopterna together as a monophyletic sister group to crown Perissodactyla. Following Perini et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10914-021-09583-4], we place notoungulates and litopterns crownward of Phenacodontidae on the Perissodactyl stem.

Ecotype_references: Theosodon has been found in the terrestrial Galera and Santa Cruz Formations (Flynn et al. 2002 [https://doi.org/10.1016/S0895-9811(02)00043-3]; McGrath et al. 2020). The Santa Cruz Formation is thought to represent terrestrial semi-deciduous forest and savannah habitat (Kay et al. 2021). T. garrettorum was probably a fast runner on land (Blanco et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.geobios.2021.04.006]), and implicitly assumed by most authors to be predominantly terrestrial. However, a Transiently Aquatic lifestyle cannot be ruled out, so the precise ecotype of this species remains ambiguous.

We tentatively follow the molecular tree topology (Welker et al 2015 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature14249]), which  places the Notoungulata and Litopterna together as a monophyletic sister group to crown Perissodactyla. Following Perini et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10914-021-09583-4], we place notoungulates and litopterns crownward of Phenacodontidae on the Perissodactyl stem.

Bell & Howell Information and Learning Company: Ann Arbor. 68 pp.]  as more minimal than that seen in Hydrosaurus spp., but not identified by Ringma and Salisbury (2014) as an aquatic adaptation. We treat it here as a very minor form of interdigital integument. We find no such interdigital fringing on the hands.

Ecotype_references: The majority of gekkonid species typically dwell on rocks, in trees, or on/under terrestrial substrates (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 562: Gekkonidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). Researchers have reported a transiently aquatic lifestyle in just one gekkonid species: Hemidactylus platyurus, which has been observed to commonly employ a unique swimming gait to travel between trees (Nirody et al. 2018). To our knowledge, no comparable forays into water have been described for Gekko siamensis in the wild. describe Gekko gecko has been described as a species of tree/rock climbers within varied terrestrial habitats (Zaaf and Van Damme 2001 [https://doi.org/10.1007/S004350100044]).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: The placement of "phenacodontids" has long been disputed (see Cooper et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0109232]; Halliday et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1111/brv.12242]; Chimento and Agnolin 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-020-70287-5]). Following two recent analyses (Cooper et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0109232]; Halliday et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1111/brv.12242]), we tentatively treat the "phenacodontids" in our sample as a clade of stem Perissodactyls. Following Perini et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10914-021-09583-4], we tentatively place Phenacodontidae stemward of notoungulates and litopterns within Pan-Perissodactyla.
Ecotype_references: Phenacodontids are thought to have been closed-canopy forest dwellers that fed on fruit and foliage (Schankler 1981 [https://doi.org/10.1038/293135a0]). However, a Transiently Aquatic lifestyle cannot be ruled out, so the precise ecotype of this species remains unclear.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: The placement of "phenacodontids" has long been disputed (see Cooper et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0109232]; Halliday et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1111/brv.12242]; Chimento and Agnolin 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-020-70287-5]). Following two recent analyses (Cooper et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0109232]; Halliday et al. 2017), we tentatively treat the "phenacodontids" in our sample as a clade of stem Perissodactyls. Following Perini et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10914-021-09583-4], we tentatively place Phenacodontidae stemward of notoungulates and litopterns within Pan-Perissodactyla.
Ecotype_references: Phenacodontids are thought to have been closed-canopy forest dwellers that fed on fruit and foliage (Schankler 1981 [https://doi.org/10.1038/293135a0]). Like other Phenacodontids, Tetraclaenodon is thought to have been primarily terrestrial (Kondrashov and Lucas 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1666/11-009.1]). However, a Transiently Aquatic lifestyle cannot be ruled out, so the precise ecotype of this species remains unclear.
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Only pes present. Measured longest digit (=mD3).
Ungual_measurement_notes: Heights not measurable from this cast.

Ecotype_references: Mesohippus was a low-level browser that fed on terrestrial plants (see Damuth and Janis 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1469-185X.2011.00176.x]). However, a Transiently Aquatic lifestyle cannot be ruled out, so the precise ecotype of this species remains ambiguous.
Limb_type_references: NA
Notes on occurrences: The genus Mesohippus is known from a broader range of ages: 40.4–30.8 Ma (https://paleobiodb.org/classic/checkTaxonInfo?taxon_no=43010&is_real_user=1).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Only manus and pes present. Measured longest digit (=D3) for both hand and foot.
Ungual_measurement_notes: Heights not measurable from this cast.

Ecotype_references: The skeleton of Stenomylus shows no obvious aquatic adaptations (see Frick and Taylor 1981 [http://hdl.handle.net/2246/2563]), and its specialized dentition was presumably suited for selective feeding on terrestrial plants (see Damuth and Janis 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1469-185X.2011.00176.x]), so we assume this species is predominantly terrestrial, but cannot rule out a Transiently Aquatic lifestyle. As a result, the precise ecotype of this species remains ambiguous.
Limb_type_references: NA
Notes on occurrences: The genus Stenomylus is known from a wider range of ages: 30.8–13.6 Ma (https://paleobiodb.org/classic/checkTaxonInfo?taxon_no=42553&is_real_user=1).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Only manus present. Measured longest digit (=mD4).
Ungual_measurement_notes: Unguals in this specimen appear to be a reconstruction, so they were not measured.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Family-level interrelations of artiodactyls derived from Gatesy et al. 2013 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2012.10.012].
Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: Although this species is primarily terrestrial (Reid 2006, Wild Boar, pp. 487–488 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]), and most individuals presumably spend little time in the water, wild boars are great swimmers (Kavicic et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1080/03949370.2020.1733669]), and multiple reports describe extended voyages at sea spanning several kilometers (Yong et al. 2010 [https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusID:150368393] and references therein; IUCN Wild Pig Specialist Group 2014 [https://www.researchgate.net/publication/264898388_Suiform_Soundings_131__Suiform_Soundings_Newsletter_of_the_IUCN_SSC_Wild_Pig_Peccary_and_Hippo_Specialist_Groups]). Because these journeys are common for the species as a whole, but presumably rare events for individual animals, we consider the wild boar Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Google image searches suggest that the proximal phalanges of pigs, like most other artiodactyls, are embedded in a soft-tissue “palm”, without soft interdigital tissue distal to them. However, no zoological specimens of Sus scrofa were available for direct observation, so this scoring remains tentative.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Only manus and pes present. Measured longest digit (=D3) for both hand and foot.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Family-level interrelations of Carnivora derived from Van Valkenburgh et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ar.23026].
Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: Although wolves are primarily terrestrial (Boitani et al. 2018 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018-2.RLTS.T3746A163508960.en], they are also good swimmers who will readily wade across large water bodies during travel, even occasionally following prey into the water (Mech 1974 [https://doi.org/10.2307/3503924]).
Limb_type_references: The taxidermied Canis_lupus (grey wolf) specimen YPM_MAM_013619 appeared to lack webbing, though the stiff nature and fur cover of the specimen made it difficult to tell.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Only manus and pes present. Measured longest digit (=mD4, pD3). D2 was the anteriormost phalanx-bearing digit.
Ungual_measurement_notes: Presence of bony ungual sheath precluded accurate measurements of ungual.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Marsupial interrelations derived from Mitchell et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1093/molbev/msu176].
Ecotype_references: This species is predominantly arboreal, requiring little water beyond what they get from consuming vegetation (Jones 1921 [https://doi.org/10.2307/1373550]; Morris et al. 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-2.RLTS.T40585A21952080.en]). We find no reference to aquatic habits.
Limb_type_references: We did not have access to sufficiently preserved zoological specimens, and there were no sufficiently clear photos on iNaturalist. As a result, the webbing phenotype of this species was scored "unclear".
Notes on occurrences: Extant. The genus has an older first occurrence age.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (=mD3, pD5).
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.
proxp_measurement_notes: Not measurable given position of specimen in slab.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Non-avian dinosaur interrelationships synthesized from the following sources: Carpenter et al. 2005 [Carpenter K, Miles C, Cloward K (2005). New Small Theropod from the Upper Jurassic Morrison Formation of Wyoming. Chapter 2, in: Carpenter K (ed), The Carnivorous Dinosaurs. Indiana University Press: Bloomington. pp. 23–48.]; Brusatte et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2010.04.001]; Xu et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature10288];  Rauhut et al. 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1203238109]; Brusatte et al. 2014 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cub.2014.08.034]; Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]; Pol and Goloboff 2020 [http://hdl.handle.net/11336/170477]; Madzia et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.12362]; Fabbri et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04528-0].
Ecotype_references: This specimen is labeled as "Archaeopteryx recurva". Archaeopteryx recurva was synonymized with Archaeopteryx lithographica, so the The Paleobiology Database page and ecotype info for lithographica was used. This species is not typically considered aquatic (Ostrom 1974 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/2821658]; Voeten et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-018-03296-8]), histological data suggest that it was not a wing-propelled diver (Voeten et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-018-03296-8]), and morphological considerations suggest that it could not run or was at least not adapted to run on water (Ma et al. 2002 [Ma MS, Ma WY, Nieuwland I, Easley RR (2002). Why Archaeopteryx did not run over water. Archaeopteryx 20: 51–56.]). However, we cannot fully rule out a Transiently Aquatic lifestyle for this species, so its ecotype remains ambiguous.

Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA
Ungual_measurement_notes: Insufficient relief in this slab to measure unguals in 3D
proxp_measurement_notes: Not measurable given position of specimen in slab.

Ecotype_references: The majority of gekkonid species typically dwell on rocks, in trees, or on/under terrestrial substrates (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 562, Gekkonidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). Researchers have reported a transiently aquatic lifestyle in just one gekkonid species: Hemidactylus platyurus, which has been observed to commonly employ a unique swimming gait to travel between trees (Nirody et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cub.2018.10.064]). To our knowledge, no comparable forays into water have been described for Paroedura masobe in the wild. Paroedura oviceps has been described as living near forest creeks, but other other Paroedura species typically occupy the forest floor or climb about in arid habitats (Zaaf and Van Damme 2001 [https://doi.org/10.1007/S004350100044]).
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved specimen P. masobe specimen YPM_HERR_010209 and research-grade iNaturalist observations of other Paroedura species  (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/151446751; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/150945392; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/144443118; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/142670429; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/139841873) show that this genus has ungual toe pads, but lacks true lobate or other interdigital webbing.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No species-level fossils present, but Paroedura indet. specimens  are max 0.0117 Ma on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype_references: The majority of gekkonid species typically dwell on rocks, in trees, or on/under terrestrial substrates (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 562, Gekkonidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). Researchers have reported a transiently aquatic lifestyle in just one gekkonid species: Hemidactylus platyurus, which has been observed to commonly employ a unique swimming gait to travel between trees (Nirody et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cub.2018.10.064]). However, to our knowledge, no comparable forays into water have been described for Ptychozoon spp. Though Ptychozoon lionotum inhabits moist tropical forest lowlands, it is considered terrestrial by the IUCN (Sumontha et al. 2008 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018-2.RLTS.T177831A103308608.en]).
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_14447
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No taxon page on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: For antmost, ungual was included because it is unclawed. Longest pedal digit (pD4) measurement difficult because penultimate phalanx was slightly faded at present mesh ISO value.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Panjiangsaurus is a likely junior synonym of Guizhouichthyosaurus (see Pan et al. 2006 [Pan X, Jiang D, Sun Z, Cai T, Zhang D (2006). Discussion on Guizhouichthyosaurus tangae Cao and Luo in Yin et al., 2000 (Reptilia, Ichthyosauria) from the Late Triassic of Guanling County, Guizhou. Acta Scientiarum Naturalium Universitatis Pekinensis 42: 697–703.]; Long et al. 2006 [https://doi.org/10.1666/0022-3360(2006)80[583:GITTIP]2.0.CO;2]; Moon 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1080/14772019.2017.1394922]), a non-parvipelvian ichthyosaur that we place as branching off after Shastasaurus, following Moon 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1080/14772019.2017.1394922].
Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered and demonstrably viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1986, p. 73 [Romer AS, Parsons TS (1986). The Vertebrate Body, Sixth Edition (First Edition 1949). CBS College Publishing: New York. 679 pp.]; Thewissen and Taylor 2007 [Thewiseen JGM, Taylor MA (2007). Aquatic Adaptations in the Limbs of Amniotes. Chapter 18, in: Hall BK (ed.), Fins into Limbs: Evolution, Development, and Transformation. The University of Chicago Press: Chicago. pp. 310–322]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines as “Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: All ichthyosaurs are commonly described as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (eg, Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773]; Motani 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2005)025[0462:DTMIAD]2.0.CO;2]; Maxwell 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jez.b.22459]; Gutarra and Rahman 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1111/brv.12790]); Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]. These flippers were highly integrated structures [Fernández et al. 2020 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2020.0281], and presumably encased in dense connective tissue like those of modern-day cetaceans (Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]), so that their digits were not independently mobile.
Photo credit: Cheng Long (Wuhan Center of China Geological Survey, Hubei Key Laboratory of Paleontology and Geological Environment Evolution) and Oliver Wings (Naturkundemuseum Bamberg)
Limb_region_measurement_notes: This specimen was originally labeled and accessioned as "Yichang Institute of Geology TR00001" (see Long et al. 2006), but the Yichang Institute has since moved to Wuhan and become part of the Wuhan Geological Survey Center (WGSC) collection, with the new specimen ID number "WGSC TR00001" (Motani, personal communication). Note that the polyphalangeal nature of this specimen (which made it difficult to assign some phalanges to particular digits) and imperfect stitching of this photograph made measurements more difficult than most of the other photographs in our data set were to measure, so the acropodial measurements may be more approximate.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Pinniped interrelationships were derived from Park et al. 2024 [https://doi.org/10.1093/evolut/qpae061].
Ecotype_references: Pinnipeds are highly adapted to aquatic life, and spend most of their time in the water; they are active swimmers and dive to forage for their exclusively aquatic prey, but they also routinely haul onto land to rest, bask, and (in some species more than others) court and breed (Berta 2008 [Berta A (2008). Pinnipeds. In Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]; Bowen 2008 [Bowen WD (2008). Pinniped Ecology. In Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). As a result, we consider all pinnipeds Highly Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All pinnipeds were scored as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (following Cooper 2008 [Cooper LN. 2008. Forelimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). This scoring was supported by direct observation of Zalophus and Phoca specimens (YPM 03329: Phoca vitulina concolor, skin; AMNH_M_201648: Zalophus californianus, fluid-preserved). Otariid and odobenid foreflippers have digits that are outwardly indistinguishable from the flipper planform, and have limited independent mobility, whereas those of phocids are outwardly distinguishable and retain the ability to prominently pronate. Thus, phocid forelimbs were scored as Weakly Flippered, while otariid and odobenid forelimbs were considered Strongly Flippered. All pinniped hindlimbs have outwardly distinguishable digits, but those of otariids have more interdigital connective tissues, which stiffens them significantly (Adam 2008 [Adam PJ. 2008. Hindlimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). To reflect this pattern, otariid hindlimbs were considered Strongly Flippered, whereas odobenid and phocid hindlimbs were considered Weakly Flippered.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Excluded unguals because phocid unguals are clawed. Used NYC Metrocard (length 85.7 mm) as scale.

Ecotype_references: Wood ducks spend the majority of their time on or near the water's surface, but also walk on land and fly over great distances during migrations (Hartke and Hepp 2010 [https://doi.org/10.2193/0022-541X(2004)068[0084:HUAPOB]2.0.CO;2]; BirdLife International 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-3.RLTS.T22680104A136999461.en]). As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Direct observation of specimen (wood duck donated to YPM_ORN_by hunter), supported by research-grade iNaturalist observation (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152577523).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: For all birds, Flipper_status concerns hindlimbs only.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: Gray herons primarily consume aquatic prey, and spend the majority of their lives in or (more often) on the edge of shallow water, searching for food; however, it also breeds and roosts in trees, spends extensive time walking on land (even if right next to the water), and flies great distances (BirdLife International 2019 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2019-3.RLTS.T22696993A154525233.en]). As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Ardea spp. Scored as having partial slight webbing (between pD3 and pD4) on proximal-most phalanges, based on direct observation of taxidermied Ardea herodias specimen YPM_ORN_138298, uncatalogued Ardea cinerea specimen (heron donated to YPM_ORN_by hunter), and research-grade iNaturalist observation (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152599194).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: For all birds, Flipper_status concerns hindlimbs only.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Rio and Mannion 2021 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.12094] recover the molecular topology (Longirostres = Gavialoidea + Crocodyloidea, where Gavialoidea includes Gavialis and Tomistoma) with morphological data.
Ecotype_references: A detailed explanation for the aquatic affinities of crocodylians is given in our Supplementary Text.

Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype_references: These turtles consume xerophytic terrestrial plants and do not seem to enter large water bodies; we find no reference to aquatic habits (Leuteritz et al. 2008 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2008.RLTS.T9014A12950491.en]).
Limb_type_references: Testudinoids scored based on taxidermied adult Geochelone gigantea specimen YPM_HERR_016084, fluid-preserved testudinid specimens (Geochelone carbonaria: YPM_HERR_005961; Gopherus polyphemus: YPM_HERR_007115) and CT scan of fluid-preserved Psammobates tentorius specimen MVZ Herp_69192. Testudinoids have stiff connective tissue encasing each set of manual and pedal digits in an immobile extended stub-like palm, with only the ungual claws protruding.
Photo credit: Matt Gage (Museum of Comparative Zoology, Harvard University)
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported for this species on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Excluded unguals because tortoises have clawed unguals. Digit pD4 was considered postmost pedal digit because pD5 lacks phalanges.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths were present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype_references: These tortoises live in arid habitats and feed mainly on terrestrial plants; we find no reference to aquatic habits (Hofmeyr et al. 2018 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018- 2.RLTS.T170524A115656793.en])
Limb_type_references: Testudinoids scored based on taxidermied adult Geochelone gigantea specimen YPM_HERR_016084, fluid-preserved testudinid specimens (Geochelone carbonaria: YPM_HERR_005961; Gopherus polyphemus: YPM_HERR_007115) and CT scan of fluid-preserved Psammobates tentorius specimen MVZ Herp_69192. Testudinoids have stiff connective tissue encasing each set of manual and pedal digits in an immobile extended stub-like palm, with only the ungual claws protruding.
Photo credit: Shion Otsuka (Museum of Vertebrate Zoology, UC Berkeley)
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported for this species on The Paleobiology Database, though there is one recorded for another Psammobates species.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Excluded unguals because tortoises have clawed unguals. Digit pD4 was considered postmost pedal digit because pD5 lacks phalanges.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype_references: Trachemys taylori inhabits marshy lacustrine freshwater environments, where it is almost always found in rivers, deep ponds, or other aquatic microhabitats in the Cuatro Cienegas basin (Seidel 2002 [Seidel ME (2002). Trachemys taylori. Catalogue of American Amphibians and Reptiles 745: 1–2.]; vanDijk and Flores-Villela 2007 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2007.RLTS.T63662A12693739.en]). However, short overland travel is common for Trachemys males seeking mates and females seeking nesting sites (Gibbons 1970 [https://doi.org/10.2307/2423953]; Thomas and Parker 2000 [https://doi.org/10.2307/1565374]), and such routine jaunts may (it seems) span up to several hundred meters (Thomas and Parker 2000 [https://doi.org/10.2307/1565374]). In addition, Trachemys hatchlings often overwinter, hibernating in their terrestrial nests for their first post-hatching winter before entering the water (Gibbons 2013 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/43287332]). Given these routine extended activities on land, we consider Trachemys species Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: CT scan of fluid-preserved Trachemys taylori specimen MVZ Herp_137443: This species has webbing reaching above base of ungual and spanning all manual and pedal digits with extensive snowshoeing on both hands and feet.
Photo credit: Shion Otsuka (Museum of Vertebrate Zoology, UC Berkeley)
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens are reported for this species on The Paleobiology Database, though there are many recorded for other Trachemys species.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: All unguals in this species are clawed except for pD5 (which lacks a keratinous claw). As a result, the ungual was included in the pD_postmost measurement.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype_references: Sternotherus odoratus is primarily aquatic, but they do overwinter in nests and some males have been reported to make extended overland excursions (Gibbons 1970 [https://doi.org/10.2307/2423953]; Rowe et al. 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1674/0003-0031-162.2.266]; Gibbons 2013 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/43287332]). As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Direct observation of Bhullar Lab musk turtle embryos consistent with Smith-Paredes et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1002/dvdy.210]: Musk turtles have extensive webbing spanning all digits.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: All unguals in this species are clawed except for pD5 (which lacks a keratinous claw). As a result, the ungual was included in the pD_postmost measurement.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype_references: Carettochelys spend the overwhelming majority of their time submerged, but leave the water at least once during the breeding cycle to lay eggs, like chelonioids, so we consider them "Highly", rather than "Fully" aquatic (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, pp. 528–529, Carettochelyidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Eisemberg et al. 2015 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018-2.RLTS.T3898A2884984.en]). They are known to traverse fast-moving rivers (Joyce and Gauthier 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2003.2523]). While they may venture into brackish or marine settings, they are typically associated with freshwater systems (Eisemberg et al. 2015 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018-2.RLTS.T3898A2884984.en]).
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved Carettochelys insculpta specimen YPM_HERR_014673: limbs are highly derived to form flippers, and this species is the only extant non-chelonioid turtle that is typically described as having flippers (see Delfino et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s13127-010-0019-x]).
Photo credit: Matt Gage (Museum of Comparative Zoology, Harvard University)
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens for this genus are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: The first two digits (mD1–mD2 and pD1–pD2) have clawed unguals; the other unguals are unclawed (scored based on YPM_HERR_014673).
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype_references: All chelonioids are highly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle, and spend the vast majority of their lives in the water, but nevertheless spend an extended period of time on land at least once each breeding season to nest on shorelines, over which they may travel great distances (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 531, Cheloniidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). As a result, we consider them "Highly" rather than "Fully" aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All crown chelonioids (“sea turtles”) were scored as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (following Wyneken 2001 [Wyneken J (2001). The anatomy of sea turtles. Seattle, WA: U.S. Department of Commerce NOAA Technical Memorandum NMFS-SEFSC-470.]; Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 531  [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). This was supported by direct observation of fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_02957 (Caretta caretta).
Photo credit: Gregory Pandelis and Ana Motta (KUBI Herpetology)
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens for this genus are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: The first two digits (mD1–mD2 and pD1–pD2) have clawed unguals; the other unguals are unclawed.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths present on the unguals measured.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Heritage et al. 2021 [Heritage S, Seiffert ER, Borths MR (2021). Recommended fossil calibrators for time-scaled molecular phylogenies of Afrotheria. Afrotherian Conservation 17. Accessed via URL=https://www.afrotheria.net/newsletter.php.2021.]
Ecotype_references: Sirenians are fully aquatic; they are unable to travel on land, bearing their young and spending their entire lives in the water; Trichechus inunguis is predominantly freshwater, whereas all other extant Trichechus species readily travel between fresh and saltwater habitats, and Dugongs are exclusively marine (Domning 2008 [Domning DP (2008). Sirenian Evolution. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]).
IUCN_source: Deutsch et al. 2008 (Trichechus manatus latirostris: http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2008.RLTS.T22106A9359881.en). Note that the species as a whole is VU, but each subspecies is EN.
Limb_type_references: All sirenians have strongly flippered forelimbs (Cooper 2008 [Cooper LN. 2008. Forelimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]) and lack hindlimbs altogether (Adam 2008 [Adam PJ. 2008. Hindlimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). This was supported by direct observation of Trichechus specimens (AMNH_M_S-03: Trichechus_inunguis, fluid-preserved; AMNH_202525 and AMNH_201322: Trichechus_sp, fluid-preserved) and high-resolution photographs of Trichechus manatus latirostris in the wild, provided by Meghan Cohorst: Save the Manatee Club, FL, USA).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: No scale, so all measurements in pixels. D2–D4 clawed, D1 and D5 unclawed (see Cooper 2008). Unguals included or excluded accordingly. Measured longest digit (mD5), including ungual.

Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered and demonstrably viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1986, p. 73 [Romer AS, Parsons TS (1986). The Vertebrate Body, Sixth Edition (First Edition 1949). CBS College Publishing: New York. 679 pp.]; Thewissen and Taylor 2007 [Thewiseen JGM, Taylor MA (2007). Aquatic Adaptations in the Limbs of Amniotes. Chapter 18, in: Hall BK (ed.), Fins into Limbs: Evolution, Development, and Transformation. The University of Chicago Press: Chicago. pp. 310–322]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines as “Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: Mosasaurines had fully fledged flippers, as supported by both osteological evidence and exceptional soft-tissue preservation (eg, Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773]; Lindgren et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms3423]), but the independent mobility of the individual digits within each flipper (and thus whether they should be "strongly flippered" or "weakly flippered") remains uncertain and may have varied among species.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Radius epiphyses difficult to distinguish from shaft, so radius measurement is likely approximate. Phalanges not visible.

Limb_region_measurement_notes: Distal phalanges not visible

Ecotype_references: Cetaceans are fully aquatic mammals, bearing their young and spending their entirely lives in the water (Reid 2006, Cetacea, p. 510 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]; Chivers 2008 [Chivers SJ (2008). In Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]).
Limb_type_references: Crown cetaceans have strongly flippered forelimbs (Cooper 2008 [Cooper LN. 2008. Forelimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]) and typically lack complete hindlimbs (Adam 2008 [Adam PJ. 2008. Hindlimb Anatomy. In: Perrin WF, Würsig B, Thewissen JGM (eds). 2008. Encyclopedia of Marine Mammals. Elsevier Science & Technology. 1316 pp. Accessed online via ProQuest Ebook Central: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/yale-ebooks/detail.action?docID=405935.]). This was supported by direct observation of fluid-preserved specimens AMNH_202413 (Megaptera_novaeangliae), AMNH_239112 (Stenella_frontalis), AMNH_200667 (Monodon_monoceros), and the assessment of soft-tissue outlines from all sampled USNM_radiographs.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (=mD2). Boundaries between zeugopodia and humerus were hard to identify.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Taxon labeled as "Ceresiosaurus calcagnii", but Rieppel 1998 [https://doi.org/10.5962/bhl.title.3413] synonymized this genus with Lariosaurus.Placement and interrelationships of ‘nothosauroids’ and ‘pachypleurosaurs’ varied among our different supertrees (see Methods and Supplementary Text). These differing placements and interrelationships were synthesized from the following sources: Sander 1989 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1989.0103]; Neenan et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms2633]; Hinz et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2019.1585364]; Lin et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2021.1901730]; Xu et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-11309-2]; Xu et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-24930-y].
Ecotype_references: Although viviparity (which permits a Fully Aquatic lifestyle in many modern amniotes) has been demonstrated in at least one species of pachypleurosaurs (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), and suggested in some species of nothosauroids (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]), these eosauropterygians retain jointed limbs that may have been able to amble onto land for short periods—it remains unclear to what degree (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]). As a result, while they were clearly adapted to spend most of their time in the water, we cannot resolve whether they were Highly Aquatic or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Non-pistosaurian sauropterygians are typically interpreted as strictly near-shore species (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]; Houssaye 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1749-4877.2009.00146.x]), though the size of some nothosaurs makes pelagic forays seem plausible. Even so, we follow previous suggestions and treat this species as exclusively near-shore, occupying primarily small or slow-moving water bodies, though we stress that this  scoring remains tentative.
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Acropodia too disarticulated and incomplete to measure

Ecotype_references: This species inhabits dry deciduous forests; we find no reference to aquatic habits (Blanco et al. 2018 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2020-2.RLTS.T163023599A115588562.en]).
Limb_type_references: Research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/109064237; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/89905285; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/83046216; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/69851909) lead us to tentatively suggest that this species has interdigital girth but no true webbing. However, this needs verification with museum specimens.
Photo credit: Duke Lemur Center Museum of Natural History
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Longest digit was pD4
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype_references: Though it is predominantly aquatic, Lontra canadensis is known to make long-distance overland journeys. It occupies a wide range of fast and slow-moving aquatic habitats, mostly freshwater but also occassionally brackish or marine (Reid 2006, pp. 471–472 [Reid FA (2006). Peterson Field Guide to Mammals of North America, Fourth Edition. Houghton Mifflin Company: Boston. 579 pp.]; Serfass 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-3.RLTS.T12302A164577078.en]).
Limb_type_references: Lutra and Lontra spp. scorings based on fluid-preserved specimens AMNH_MAM_202460 and AMNH_MAM_231106.
Photo credit: Heather Prestige (Texas A&M University Biodiversity Research and Teaching Collection)
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (=D3) for both hand and foot.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths present on the unguals measured.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Mustelinae was placed following Law 2019 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2019.0155]. Due to ongoing taxonomic controversy regarding the genera that make up Mustelinae (see Patterson et al. 2021 [http://dx.doi.org/10.29252/JAD.2021.3.2.1]), we place all Mustela/Neovison/Neogale specimens in an unresolved polytomy.
Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: This species is predominantly terrestrial, but lives near water, readily swims, and uses water ways for long-distance dispersal (Helgen and Reid 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-1.RLTS.T41654A45213820.en])
Limb_type_references: Mustela specimens AMNH_243106, AMNH_36632, and YPM_01479: possible slight webbing between pD3 and pD4, but very difficult to tell. However, given that nearly all  mustelids have complete interdigital webbing, which varies in extent but may reach the proximal end of the toe pads (Kitchener et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780198759805.003.0003]), we tentatively scored this species as having complete, slight webbing.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured longest digit (=mD4, pD3).
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths present on the unguals measured.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Phylogenetic analysis of the unpublished sphenosuchian, nicknamed "Popobuddy", is pending (Ruebenstahl et al., in prep). We tentatively place it here crownward of Poposaurus.
Ecotype_references: The morphology of this specimen suggests it was adapted for a cursorial lifestyle, but transient aquatic habits cannot be ruled out (Ruebenstahl, personal communication).
Limb_type_references: NA
Citation for occurrences: See note for clarification on age range and sources.
Notes on occurrences: This unpublished specimen, nicknamed "Popobuddy", is Norian in age, from the Adamanian Biozone (Ruebenstahl, personal communication). The Adamanian Biozone data from 224.5–215 Ma (Martz and Parker 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/B978-0-12-803243-5.00002-9]; Kligman et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.18476/pale.v13.a3].).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Popobuddy is Sphenosuchia_indet. It remains unresolved.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Marsupial interrelations derived from Mitchell et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1093/molbev/msu176].
Ecotype_references: Although thylacines went extinct within the last century, their ecology is poorly understood (Warburton et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.26879/947]). It is sometimes likened in habits to the predominantly terrestrial canid and felid generalist carnivores (Barnett and Lorenzen 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41559-017-0384-3]), and its skeletal anatomy is consistent with a generalized terrestrial lifestyle. However, Aboriginal art and oral narratives of the "djanggerrk" (which researchers have identified with the thylacine) from the Western Arnhem Land of mainland Australia describe this animal as a ready and powerful swimmer (Glaskin 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1080/00664677.2021.1937513]), suggesting transiently aquatic habits. In the absence of decisive evidence for or against transiently aquatic habits, we consider the aquatic affinity of this predominantly terrestrial taxon ambiguous.
Limb_type_references: One online source clarifies that thylacines lacked interdigital webbing (http://www.naturalworlds.org/thylacine/biology/anatomy/external/external_anatomy_8.htm#:~:text=thylacine's%20toes%20have%20no%20webbing,%2C%20tri%2Dlobed%20plantar%20pad.). In the absence of physical specimens, we tentatively score this species as unwebbed.
Photo credit: Douglass Rovinsky, Justin Adams (Tasmanian Museum and Art Gallery)
Notes on occurrences: Recent. The thylacine went extinct in the early-to-mid 20th century (Burbidge and Woinarski 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016- 2.RLTS.T21866A21949291.en]).
Limb_region_measurement_notes: This mesh is too low-resolution for any acropodial or phalanx measurements (mesh simplification led to blending of acropodia?).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Specimen labeled as "Aquiloeurycea galeanae", but we call it "Pseudoeurycea galeanae" following Pyron et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2148-13-93], and place it accordingly.
Ecotype_references: This species lives, breeds, and engages in direct development on land ((IUCN SSC Amphibian Specialist Group 2016 [Aquiloeurycea: http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016- 3.RLTS.T59379A53982166.en]).
IUCN_source: IUCN SSC Amphibian Specialist Group 2016 (Aquiloeurycea: http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016- 3.RLTS.T59379A53982166.en)
Limb_type_references: From research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/56294747; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/34922560; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/2899819; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/1191893), this species appears to have soft-tissue webbing at least on the proximal phalanges spanning all digits on the hands and feet. But this scoring is tentative.
Citation for occurrences: No fossils are known of tropical plethodontid salamanders (Rovito et al. 2015 [https://doi.org/10.1111/zoj.12271]).

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references:
The hoatzin lives in riparian forests in Venezuela, where it nests on branches over water courses (Domínguez-Bello et al. 1994 [https://doi.org/10.1093/auk/111.3.643]). Nestlings of this species readily dive into the underlying water, where they can reach depths of 10m, and swim to the shoreline, where they climb back onto land to start a new nest (Woodbridge and Grimmer 1962 [Woodbridge WM, Grimmer JL (1962). Strange Little World of the Hoatzin. National Geographic
Magazine 122(3): 391–400. National Geographic Virtual Library,
link.gale.com/apps/doc/PHXHYH798729145/NGMA?u=29002&sid=bookmark-NGMA&xid=7d6e4e28. Accessed 11 Apr. 2023.]; Müllner and Linsenmair 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1557-9263.2007.00123.x]). This common, stereotyped escape behavior has only been described in adults. Breeding and nesting behaviors for this species seem to depend upon seasonal changes in water level (Müllner and Linsenmair 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1557-9263.2007.00123.x]).
Limb_type_references: Research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/150617005; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/151403574) show that this species lacks interdigital webbing.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: The phylogenetic placement of Scandentia is uncertain (see Alvarez-Carreterro et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-021-04341-1]), so we place it in a polytomy at the base of Euarchontoglires, following Blanga-Kanfi et al. 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2148-9-71]. Treeshrew topology derived from Olson et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1023/B:JOMM.0000029145.28207.6d].
Ecotype_references: Tree shrews are either arboreal or ground-dwelling (Langham 1982 [https://doi.org/10.1111/jzo.1982.197.3.323]; Sargis 2001 [https://doi.org/10.1017/S0952836901000449]). We find no reference to aquatic habits in the Scandentia, so we score them as Terrestrial.

Ecotype_references: Although primarily ground-dwelling, tapirs are excellent swimmers; they readily cross wide streams and enter the water both to flee or hide from predators and to feed on aquatic vegetation (Meijaard and van Strien 2003 [Meijaard E, Strien NV (2003). The Asian Tapir (Tapirus indicus). In: Briefing Book, Malay Tapir Conservation Workshop. Krau Wildlife Reserve, Malaysia. 22 pp. Accessed online via URL = https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Erik-Meijaard/publication/236898616_Der_Schabrackentapir_Tapirus_indicus/links/58338f4008aef19cb81cb43e/Der-Schabrackentapir-Tapirus-indicus.pdf.]; Garcìa et al. 2016 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-1.RLTS.T21471A45173340.en]; Naranjo 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-28868-6]). As a result, we tentatively score all tapirs in this study as being Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Scored based on skin specimen of Tapirus_bairdii (YPM_14313): some degree of webbing to mid-digit on both hand and foot, difficult to distinguish from interdigital girth and hard to score given close appresion of digits. Webbing "subtype" too difficult to score.

New Small Theropod from the Upper Jurassic Morrison Formation of Wyoming. Chapter 2, in: Carpenter K (ed), The Carnivorous Dinosaurs. However, since transiently aquatic habits cannot be ruled out, the ecotype of this taxon remains ambiguous.

antirrhopus, an unusual theropod from the Lower Cretaceous of Montana. Bulletin of the Peabody Museum of Natural History 30: 162 pp.]), but we cannot rule out a transiently aquatic lifestyle, so its ecotype remains ambiguous.

Ecotype_references: The hippo-like proportions of this animal (its relatively short limbs and massively girthy ribcage) led Osborn 1898 [Osborn HF (1898) A COMPLETE SKELETON OF TELEOCERAS FOSSIGER. NOTES UPON THE GROWTH AND SEXUAL CHARACTERS OF THIS SPECIES. Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History 10: 51–59.] to consider it semi-aquatic. However, isotopic evidence from tooth enamel has suggested a terrestrial lifestyle (Clementz et al. 2008 [https://doi.org/10.2110/palo.2007.p07-054r]; Wang & Secord 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.palaeo.2019.109411]). In the absence of conclusive evidence, the precise ecotype of this group remains ambiguous.

proxp_measurement_notes: Not sure where proximal phalanx epiphyses are, so I refrained from measuring them.

Ecotype_references: Hyracodon is a genus of Oligocene rhinocerotoids whose slender limbs, long neck, and small skull make them historically analogized to modern cursorial pecorans (Troxell 1921 [Troxell EL (1921). Art. III. New Species of Hyracodon. Contributions  from the Othniel Charles Marsh Publication Fund, Peabody. Museum, Yale University. pp. 34–40. Accessed online via URL = <https://zenodo.org/record/1519362/files/article.pdf>.]). Based on their dentition, hyracodontids are thought to have browsed primarily for nuts, twigs, and other tough vegetation on the periphery of bushland floodplains (Mead & Wall 1998a [Mead AJ, Wall WP (1998a).  PALEOECOLOGICAL IMPLICATIONS OF THE CRANIODENTAL AND PREMAXILLA MORPHOLOGIES OF TWO RHINOCEROROIDS (PERISSODACTYLA) FROM BADLANDS NATIONAL PARK, SOUTH DAKOTA. In: Badlands National Park chapter, Santucci VL and McClelland L (eds.), National Park Service Paleontological Research, Technical Report NPS/NRGRD/GRDTR-98/01: Kemmerer. pp. 18–22]; Mead & Wall 1998b [Mead AJ, Wall WP (1998b). DIETARY IMPLICATIONS OF JAW BIOMECHANICS IN THE RHINOCEROTOIDS HYRACODON AND SUBHYRACODON FROM BADLANDS NATIONAL PARK, SOUTH DAKOTA. In: Badlands National Park chapter, Santucci VL and McClelland L (eds.), National Park Service Paleontological Research, Technical Report NPS/NRGRD/GRDTR-98/01. pp. 23–28.). Given its cursorial habits and tendency to graze plants that grow distant from water, this group was probably predominantly terrestrial, but we cannot rule out transiently aquatic habits and its ecotype thus remains ambiguous.

Ecotype_references: Carettochelys spend the overwhelming majority of their time submerged, but leave the water at least once during the breeding cycle to lay eggs, like chelonioids, so we consider them "Highly", rather than "Fully" aquatic (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, pp. 528–529, Carettochelyidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Eisemberg et al. 2015 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018-2.RLTS.T3898A2884984.en]). They are known to traverse fast-moving rivers (Joyce and Gauthier 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2003.2523]). While they may venture into brackish or marine settings, they are typically associated with freshwater systems (Eisemberg et al. 2015 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018-2.RLTS.T3898A2884984.en]).
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved Carettochelys insculpta specimen YPM_HERR_014673: limbs are highly derived to form flippers, and this species is the only extant non-chelonioid turtle that is typically described as having flippers (see Delfino et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s13127-010-0019-x]).

Ecotype_references: Although the green tree monitor is primarily terrestrial (Shea et al 2018 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018- 2.RLTS.T42485751A101752115.en]), it is an excellent swimmer (Baldwin 2006 [Baldwin B (2006). Successful care and reproduction of green tree monitors (Varanus prasinus) at the San Diego Zoo. Iguana 13: 283–287.]), which is thought to occasionally swim or wade in the water in the wild (Shuter 2014 [Shuter AD (2014). A novel underwater foraging behavior observed in Varanus prasinus at the Wildlife Conservation Society’s Bronx Zoo. Biawak 8: 61–63.]), and one individual has even once been observed foraging underwater in captivity (Shuter 2014 [Shuter AD (2014). A novel underwater foraging behavior observed in Varanus prasinus at the Wildlife Conservation Society’s Bronx Zoo. Biawak 8: 61–63.]). Assuming this is a very rare occurrence, but that very occasional swimming is typical for the species, we consider this taxon Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Varanus spp. were scored from fluid-preserved YPM VZ specimens (V. beccarii: YPM_HERR_010047; V. exanthematicus: YPM_HERR_010408; V. griseus: YPM_HERR_003865; Varanus dumerilii: YPM_HERR_010122; V. salvator: YPM_HERR_009466). All observed Varanus specimens appear to lack interdigital webbing.

Ecotype_references: Coastal Goannas (Varanus panoptes) are inhabit a range of Australian habitats, including coastlands, floodplains, and the outskirts of mangroves (Shea et al. 2018 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018-1.RLTS.T42485740A42485745.en]). They spend extensive time foraging on coasts (Blamires 2004). At least three individuals have been seen to go on extended subaqueous hunts for aquatic prey (Martin 1990 [Martin KC (1990). A Note on Diving Behaviour in the Northern Sand Goanna Varanus panoptes. Northern Territory Naturalist 12: 28–29.]; Shannon and Mendyk 2009 [Shannon R, Mendyk RW (2009). Aquatic Foraging Behavior and Freshwater Mussel (Velesunio sp.) Predation by Varanus panoptes panoptes in Central-Western Queensland. Biawak 3: 85–87.]), but field studies do not report common voluntary submergences (Blamires 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1643/CH-03-016R1]). This underwater foraging behavior has been interpreted as an occasional seasonal phenomenon during dry seasons, when terrestrial food is particularly scarce (Shannon and Mendyk 2009 [Shannon R, Mendyk RW (2009). Aquatic Foraging Behavior and Freshwater Mussel (Velesunio sp.) Predation by Varanus panoptes panoptes in Central-Western Queensland. Biawak 3: 85–87.]). The rare and transient (on the order of minutes) nature of these aquatic forays lead us to tentatively score this behavior as Transiently Aquatic. However, if additional field studies indicate that these aquatic behaviors are more prolongued or frequent, we would revise the designation of this taxon to "Moderately Aquatic" instead.
Limb_type_references: Varanus spp. were scored from fluid-preserved YPM VZ specimens (V. beccarii: YPM_HERR_010047; V. exanthematicus: YPM_HERR_010408; V. griseus: YPM_HERR_003865; Varanus dumerilii: YPM_HERR_010122; V. salvator: YPM_HERR_009466). All observed Varanus specimens appear to lack interdigital webbing.

Ecotype_references: Roughneck monitors (Varanus rudicollis) are primarily arboreal species that consume primarily terrestrial arthropods , though ome populations may also reside in mangroves and will occasionally forage for frogs, crabs, and fish (Phimmachack et al. 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-2.RLTS.T83778529A83778660.en]). Little is known about the natural history of this elusive species, but given the apparent rarity of aquatic foraging and the absence of reported submergences in the literature, we tentatively consider this species Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Varanus spp. were scored from fluid-preserved YPM VZ specimens (V. beccarii: YPM_HERR_010047; V. exanthematicus: YPM_HERR_010408; V. griseus: YPM_HERR_003865; Varanus dumerilii: YPM_HERR_010122; V. salvator: YPM_HERR_009466). All observed Varanus specimens appear to lack interdigital webbing.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No taxon page on The Paleobiology Database. There are, to our knowledge, no fossils of V. rudicollis, although see Welton et al. 2014 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ympev.2014.01.016] for an estimate of the divergence between V. rudicollis and the V. salvator complex.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Pes insufficiently articulated for acropod planform measurements.
Ungual_measurement_notes: Keratinous sheaths present on all unguals, preventing measurement of bone proportions.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: The Common Water Monitor (Varanus salvator) is a semi-aquatic species (Bauer and Jackman 2008 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10750-007-9115-0]), which thrives opportunistically in a variety of habitats, including mangroves, beaches, subtropical riparian forests, and lakesides (Quah et al. 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-2.RLTS.T178214A113138439.en]). It typically lives near or along bodies of water, though individuals may occasionally venture farther inland (Quah et al. 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-2.RLTS.T178214A113138439.en]). Their diet includes crabs and many species of aquatic vertebrates, and they regularly submerge in water to cool down during hotter parts of the day (Quah et al. 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-2.RLTS.T178214A113138439.en]). They are frequently observed swimming (De Lisle 2007 [De Lisle HF (2007). Observations on Varanus s. salvator in North Sulawesi. Biawak 1: 59–66.]), and have even been observed swimming long distances (several hundred meters) across saltwater to travel between islands (Borden 2007 [Borden R (2007). Varanus salvator (Asian Water Monitor) migration. Biawak 1: 84.]). However, they also spend extensive time on land every day, and juveniles have been reported to voyage inland (Quah et al. 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-2.RLTS.T178214A113138439.en]). As a result, we consider this species Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Varanus spp. were scored from fluid-preserved YPM VZ specimens (V. beccarii: YPM_HERR_010047; V. exanthematicus: YPM_HERR_010408; V. griseus: YPM_HERR_003865; Varanus dumerilii: YPM_HERR_010122; V. salvator: YPM_HERR_009466). All observed Varanus specimens appear to lack interdigital webbing.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: This specimen is pretty small and looks like a juvenile (though not a neonate), which is worth noting given the less obvious aquatic habits of V. salvator juveniles (De Lisle 2007; Quah et al. 2021), but it still has defined long bone epiphyses. The pes is disarticulated and the manus is not sufficiently complete for acropod planform measurements.
Ungual_measurement_notes: Keratinous sheaths present on all unguals, preventing measurement of bone proportions.

Limb_region_measurement_notes: This specimen is quite large (total length recorded with specimen at 70.5 inches, weight 21 lbs), and yet it seems to have unfused epiphyses stylo- and zeugopodial epiphyses (though its acropodial epiphyses are all fully defined). I would assume it's mature, given the size, though I can't be sure. I manually rearticulated this specimen, which was completely disarticulated to begin with. This makes the D3 measurements dubious. I think I put the hand together correctly, and I checked my reconstruction with Dalton Meyer, but given the similarity and sheer number of phalanxes present in this specimen, I cannot be certain that my manus reconstruction was accurate. This is particularly notable because the digit was shorter than I was expecting, which might be an artefact of faulty reconstruction.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths present, but specimen was fully disarticulated and I couldn't determine which unguals belonged to D3.
proxp_measurement_notes: Didn't trust manus reconstruction sufficiently to measure mD3p1 dimensions.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: Although komodo dragons are predominantly terrestrial (Ciofi et al. 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1643/0045-8511(2007)7[462:PAOHRS]2.0.CO;2]; Jessop et al. 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-2.RLTS.T22884A123633058.en]), they have been observed to wade through and cross larger water bodies (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=y33tYaoV5sk; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/44176726). As a result, we consider this species Transiently Aquatic. It is worth noting, however, that these online-posted observations have not (to our knowledge) been vetted by herpetologists, so the taxon's Transiently Aquatic designation remains dubious and requires verification.
Limb_type_references: Varanus spp. were scored from fluid-preserved YPM VZ specimens (V. beccarii: YPM_HERR_010047; V. exanthematicus: YPM_HERR_010408; V. griseus: YPM_HERR_003865; Varanus dumerilii: YPM_HERR_010122; V. salvator: YPM_HERR_009466). All observed Varanus specimens appear to lack interdigital webbing.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Very large specimen. Almost fully disarticulated except for portions of acropodium. I was able to identify one fully articulated digit as mD3, and its phalangeal formula and morphology support this designation, but its identification remains tentative.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths present on the unguals measured.

Ecotype_references: Varanus jobiensis primarily eats terrestrial insects, spends the vast majority of its time on land, and flees predators by scaling trees; however, it has also been found to patrol brooks and catch fish and shrimps, though it is unclear whether it ever submerges to do this (Phillip et al. 2004 [Phillip KM, Böhme W, Ziegler T (2004). Varanus jobiensis, in: Pianka ER, King DR, King RA (eds.), Varanoid Lizards of the World. Indiana University Press: Bloomington. 593 pp.]). As a result, we consider this species just barely transiently aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Varanus spp. were scored from fluid-preserved YPM VZ specimens (V. beccarii: YPM_HERR_010047; V. exanthematicus: YPM_HERR_010408; V. griseus: YPM_HERR_003865; Varanus dumerilii: YPM_HERR_010122; V. salvator: YPM_HERR_009466). All observed Varanus specimens appear to lack interdigital webbing.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No taxon page on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Small captive indiivdual (17.5 cm SVL), but had defined long bone epiphyses. Manus was partly disarticulated, so the mD3 measurement should be treated with caution.
Ungual_measurement_notes: Keratinous sheaths present on all unguals, preventing measurement of bone proportions.
proxp_measurement_notes: The proximal phalanges were too small to measure reliably with calipers.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: The natural history of Varanus dumerilii is poorly known; though it is known to venture far inland, it also inhabits mangroves, and seeks refuge in both trees and water when threatened; some individuals spend considerable time submerged in water and forage for crabs there, but others have a more terrestrial diet consisting primarily of terrestrial arthropods and birds; some researchers have suggested that this species is specialized for crab-hunting (Bennett 2004 [Bennett D (2004). Varanus dumerili, in: Pianka ER, King DR, King RA (eds.), Varanoid Lizards of the World. Indiana University Press: Bloomington. 593 pp.]). This species is thus either Transiently or Moderately Aquatic. We tentatively score it as Moderately Aquatic on the basis of its most aquatic mangrove-dwelling subpopulations.
Limb_type_references: Varanus spp. were scored from fluid-preserved YPM VZ specimens (V. beccarii: YPM_HERR_010047; V. exanthematicus: YPM_HERR_010408; V. griseus: YPM_HERR_003865; Varanus dumerilii: YPM_HERR_010122; V. salvator: YPM_HERR_009466). All observed Varanus specimens appear to lack interdigital webbing.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens for this species are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Small captive indiivdual, but had defined long bone epiphyses.
Ungual_measurement_notes: Keratinous sheaths present on all unguals, preventing measurement of bone proportions.
proxp_measurement_notes: The proximal phalanges were too small to measure reliably with calipers.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, seldom in water
Ecotype_references: Although this species is predominantly terrestrial and feeds primarily on terrestrial arthropod prey (Bennett et al. 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-2.RLTS.T178346A16967669.en]), it has been observed to occasionally cross large rivers during travel between habitats (Bennett 2000 [Bennett D (2000). Observations on Bosc's Monitor Lizard (Varanus exanthematicus) in the Wild. Bull. Chicago Herp. Soc. 35: 177–180.]). As a result, we consider this species Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Varanus spp. were scored from fluid-preserved YPM VZ specimens (V. beccarii: YPM_HERR_010047; V. exanthematicus: YPM_HERR_010408; V. griseus: YPM_HERR_003865; Varanus dumerilii: YPM_HERR_010122; V. salvator: YPM_HERR_009466). Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Pyron et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2148-13-93] (place it as sister to Anniella + Anguidae); Augusta et al. 2022 [Augusta BG, Zaher H, Polcyn MJ, Fiorillo AR, Jacobs LL (2022), in: https://doi.org/10.1017/9781108938891] (place it within Varanoidea)
Ecotype_references: The Gila Monster is primarily terrestrial and often found in arid environments; we find no reference to aquatic habits in this species (Hammerson et al. 2007 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2007.RLTS.T9865A13022716.en]).
Limb_type_references: Beautifully preserved dry specimen of H. horridum (YPM HERR 011611) and research-grade iNaturalist observations of H. suspectum (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/144330931; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/146062367; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/147784200; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/150798912) show that this genus completely lacks interdigital webbing.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: SVL when alive: 352 mm
Ungual_measurement_notes: Keratinous sheaths were present on all unguals, preventing measurement of bone proportions.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Pyron et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2148-13-93] [https://doi.org/10.1186/1471-2148-13-93]; Augusta et al. 2022 [Augusta BG, Zaher H, Polcyn MJ, Fiorillo AR, Jacobs LL (2022), in: https://doi.org/10.1017/9781108938891] (both sources place it as sister to Varanidae)
Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: Lanthanotus dig burrows along water courses and flee into the water when threatened; captive individuals spend most of their time lying in the water; wild individuals are thought to forage on land and in the water at night (Das and Auliya 2021 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021- 2.RLTS.T102342572A102342580.en]). As a result, it is either Transiently Aquatic or Moderately Aquatic. We tentatively consider the species Moderately Aquatic, given the frequency of its aquatic foraging behavior.
Limb_type_references: Scan of fluid-preserved L. borneensis specimen FMNH_Amphib_and_Rep_134698 (viewed at low isosurface cut-off) shows that this individual seems to lack manual and pedal webbing. However, scan of fluid-preserved L. borneensis specimen UF_Herp_16268 shows very slight webbing between pD3 and pD4. Research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/77418936; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/8942885; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/146812646) verify that this species typically lacks manual interdigital webbing but the pedal webbing phenotype is difficult to resolve. As a result, we tentatively assume intraspecific variation in webbing phenotype within the species, and score all Lanthanotus specimens on an individual basis.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: Each day/night cycle, Shinisaurus crocodilurus spends considerable time both on land and in water (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 581, Shinisauridae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Nguyen et al. 2022 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2022-1.RLTS.T57287221A217811129.en]), so we consider it Moderately Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Existing iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/39531-Shinisaurus-crocodilurus; https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/39531-Shinisaurus-crocodilurus/browse_photos) show that this species lacks interdigital webbing.
