Ecotype_references: Carettochelys spend the overwhelming majority of their time submerged, but leave the water at least once during the breeding cycle to lay eggs, like chelonioids, so we consider them "Highly", rather than "Fully" aquatic (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, pp. 528–529, Carettochelyidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]; Eisemberg et al. 2015 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018-2.RLTS.T3898A2884984.en]). They are known to traverse fast-moving rivers (Joyce and Gauthier 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2003.2523]). While they may venture into brackish or marine settings, they are typically associated with freshwater systems (Eisemberg et al. 2015 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018-2.RLTS.T3898A2884984.en]).
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved Carettochelys insculpta specimen YPM_HERR_014673: limbs are highly derived to form flippers, and this species is the only extant non-chelonioid turtle that is typically described as having flippers (see Delfino et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s13127-010-0019-x]).
Photo credit: Mesh exported from scan created by the University of Michigan Museum of Zoology, Research Museums Center, which was itself funded by NSF DBI-1701713. The files were downloaded from www.Morphosource.org, Duke University.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens for this genus are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: The first two digits (mD1–mD2 and pD1–pD2) have clawed unguals; the other unguals are unclawed (scored based on YPM_HERR_014673). Stylopodia were skipped because stylopodial epiphyses were completely unossified.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths present on the unguals measured.

Limb_type_references: Research-grade iNaturalist observations (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/157114783; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/153412400; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/152427662; https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/149298747) show that Cuvier's Dwarf Caiman has slight manual webbing across two or three manual digits and extensive pedal webbing across all pedal digits.
Photo credit: Mesh segmented by Caleb Gordon from scan created by Emma Schachner. This was a full body scan of a live 5kg male Cuvier's dwarf caiman (Paleosuchus palpebrosusu) during a natural apnea with no sedation. The scan occurred after the animal, named Mithridates VI of Pontus, ingested a large rodent. Mithridates lives in captivity and was scanned in the prone position. The scan was downloaded from MorphoSource (Duke University),  with the following citation: Schachner ER, Diaz Jr RE, Coke R, Echols MS, Osborn ML, Hedrick BP. 2022. Architecture of the bronchial tree in Cuvier's dwarf caiman (Paleosuchus palpebrosus). The Anatomical Record 305: 3037–3054.
Notes on occurrences: Extant. No fossil specimens for this taxon are reported on The Paleobiology Database.
Included_unguals?: Digit-dependent (posterior-most pedal digit [pD4] and two posterior-most manual digits [mD4–mD5] are unclawed, based on observation of present specimen scan and research-grade iNaturalist observations https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/143454787, https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/146344750, https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/149298747, https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/173842068)
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Low contrast made it difficult to capture all digits without the osteoderm scales during quick segmentation; some of the distal phalanges are missing from some of the digits in the mesh from which we took measurements. This prevented us from collecting acropod proportion measurements. However, a more careful and complete segmentation would be feasible from this scan if needed.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths present on unguals measured; pD3 ungual cut off in either scan or segmentation.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Pritchard and Nesbitt 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsos.170499], Pritchard and Sues 2019 (J. Systematic Palaeontology), and Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.] place Claudiosaurus within Neodiapsida crownward of Weigeltisauridae.
Ecotype_references: Morphological features suggest that Claudiosaurus had an aquatic lifestyle (Carroll 1981 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1981.0079]; Sues 2019, p. 41 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]). However, it retains well-developed jointed appendages, and it remains unclear whether and to what extent it traversed on land. As a result, we cannot resolve whether this taxon was Moderately or Highly Aquatic. Even so, it is thought to have resided in near-shore environments without adaptations to a pelagic lifestyle (Carroll 1981 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1981.0079]; Houssaye 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1749-4877.2009.00146.x]).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Placement and interrelationships of ‘nothosauroids’ and ‘pachypleurosaurs’ varied among our different supertrees (see Methods and Supplementary Text). These differing placements and interrelationships were synthesized from the following sources: Sander 1989 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1989.0103]; Neenan et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms2633]; Hinz et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2019.1585364]; Lin et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2021.1901730]; Xu et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-11309-2]; Xu et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-24930-y].
Ecotype_references: Although viviparity (which permits a Fully Aquatic lifestyle in many modern amniotes) has been demonstrated in at least one species of pachypleurosaurs (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), and suggested in some species of nothosauroids (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]), these eosauropterygians retain jointed limbs that may have been able to amble onto land for short periods—it remains unclear to what degree (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]). As a result, while they were clearly adapted to spend much of their time in the water, we cannot resolve whether they were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Pachypleurosaurs and nothosaurs are thought to have lived in shallow near-shore environments (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]).
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Zeugopodia and acropodia impressions very faint in this cast, so radius and ulna measurements were averaged from left and right limbs, and mD3 measurement should be considered approximate.

Ecotype_references: Although clearly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle (Romer 1956, pp. 344, 524 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Rieppel et al. 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1139/E05-076]), most thalattosaurs seem to retain well-developed jointed limbs, suggesting that they may have retained the ability to make limited trips on land. The degree to which they traveled onto land remains unclear, so we cannot resolve whether thalattosaurs were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Because they lack obvious adaptations for a pelagic existence, most thalattosaurs are considered to have occupied shallower water basins (Müller 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1139/E05-030]), but some thalattosaur genera seem more highly adapted to aquatic habits than others, so we consider their Ecotype2 score ambiguous. Thalattosaurs were marine, rather than freshwater (Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]).

Ecotype_references: Eoraptor was recovered from a nonmarine deposit among other presumably terrestrial fauna, and nothing in its skeleton obviously indicates aquatic habits (Sereno et al. 1993 [https://doi.org/10.1038/361064a0]). However, a Transiently Aquatic lifestyle cannot be fully ruled out, so the precise ecotype of this taxon remains ambiguous.

Ecotype_references: Cau et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature24679] used histological and morphological features to argue for an amphibious (or, under our binning procedure, "Moderately Aquatic") ecology for this taxon. Brownstein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-52867-2] challenged many of the inferences in Cau et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature24679], but many of these counterpoints were rebutted by Cau 2020 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.8672]. This discourse suggests that the ecology of Halszkaraptor remains contentious, and we score it accordingly.

However, it remains unclear whether they were moderately, highly, or fully aquatic, so their ecotype remains ambiguous.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Göhlich and Chiappe 2006 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature04579]; Butler and Upchurch 2007 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2007)27[253:HITATP]2.0.CO;2]; Bell and Hendrickx 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12517]; Pol and Goloboff 2020 [http://hdl.handle.net/11336/170477]
Ecotype_references:
Nothing in the skeleton of Juravenator strongly suggests aquatic habits. However, Juravenator was recovered from a lagoonal/shallow subtidal palaeoenvironment (Bell and Hendrickx 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12517]), in association with fish remains (Arratia and Schultze 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1002/mmng.201200001]), has a tooth morphology reminiscent of some other opportunistically piscivorous coelurosaurs (Bell and Hendrickx 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12517]), and has unique ornamental scales on its tail that are reminiscent of integumentary sense organs useful for detecting prey in the water (Bell and Hendrickx 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12517]). As a result, Bell and Hendrickx (2020 [https://doi. org/10.1016/j.cub.2020.08.066.], 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12517]) proposed a "semi-aquatic opportunist" lifestyle in which Juravenator hunted by "submerging
its tail (and other body regions with integumentary sense organs) to acquire sensory information" about "aquatic prey on the lagoon margins and other water bodies on
the Solnhofen reef archipelago" (Bell and Hendrickx 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12517]). Given this ambiguity, transiently and moderately aquatic habits remain plausible but unverified, and the ecotype of this species remains unclear.

Limb_region_measurement_notes: pD3 partially obscured in different places on each foot by overlying digits in slab, so pD3 measurement was averaged from both feet.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Placement and interrelationships of ‘nothosauroids’ and ‘pachypleurosaurs’ varied among our different supertrees (see Methods and Supplementary Text). These differing placements and interrelationships were synthesized from the following sources: Sander 1989 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1989.0103]; Neenan et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms2633]; Hinz et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2019.1585364]; Lin et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2021.1901730]; Xu et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-11309-2]; Xu et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-24930-y].
Ecotype_references: Although viviparity (which permits a Fully Aquatic lifestyle in many modern amniotes) has been demonstrated in at least one species of pachypleurosaurs (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), and suggested in some species of nothosauroids (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]), these eosauropterygians retain jointed limbs that may have been able to amble onto land for short periods—it remains unclear to what degree (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]). As a result, while they were clearly adapted to spend much of their time in the water, we cannot resolve whether they were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Pachypleurosaurs and nothosaurs are thought to have lived in shallow near-shore environments (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]).
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: The phylogenetic placement and monophyly of parareptiles have recently been disputed (see Ford and Benson 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41559-019-1047-3]; Simões et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.abq1898]: Science Advances). Our placement of Mesosaurs and other parareptiles within Pan-Reptilia varies in our different supertrees to reflect existing disagreements (see Methods and Supplementary Text). Piñeiro et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.4072/rbp.2021.3.04] clarify that Mesosaurus tenuidens is the only valid taxon of mesosaurid currently known, so we have renamed all the mesosaurid samples in our data set accordingly.
Ecotype_references: Although mesosaurs were viviparous (Piñeiro et al. 2012 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1080/08912963.2012.662230]) and clearly adapted for aquatic life in marine systems (Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Demarco et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.3389/fevo.2018.00109]), aspects of their vertebral morphometry have alluded to a more semi-aquatic lifestyle, with considerable time spent both in water and on land (Demarco et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.3389/fevo.2018.00109]; Demarco et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.3389/frym.2019.00039]). Adult mesosaurs may have occupied pelagic environments (Holmes 1953 [https://doi.org/10.1038/171669a0]; Verriere and Frobisch 2022 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.13866]), but we lack direct evidence concerning whether and to what extent they actually traversed on land, so it remains unclear whether mesosaurs were Moderately, Highly, or Fully Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All mesosaurids were scored as having extensive, complete pedal webbing, based on exceptional soft-tissues preserved on the hindlimb skeletons of the following mesosaurid specimens: SMNK_PAL_3806, SMF_R_4710, BSPG_1964_I_268, and URCR_064. Scorings were based on high-resolution photographs of the specimens, provided by Antoine Vérriere. Less soft-tissue preservation was discernible on forelimbs, but manual webbing phenotype was tentatively scored based on specimen NHMW_1987_0067_0001, which appears to have complete extensive webbing. Because the taxonomic breadth covered by these specimens remains unclear, the application of these scores to all sampled mesosaurids remains a tentative first approximation, as mesosaurids may have more variability in webbing phenotype than we can currently discern based on available specimens.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: NA

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: This specimen was labeled "Nothosaurus raabi", but that taxon was synonymized with "Nothosaurus marchicus" in 1996 (Albers 2011 and references therein). Placement and interrelationships of ‘nothosauroids’ and ‘pachypleurosaurs’ varied among our different supertrees (see Methods and Supplementary Text). These differing placements and interrelationships were synthesized from the following sources: Sander 1989 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1989.0103]; Neenan et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms2633]; Hinz et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2019.1585364]; Lin et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2021.1901730]; Xu et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-11309-2]; Xu et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-24930-y].  "Nothosaurus" is polyphyletic, with N. marchicus branching off before N. giganteus (Hinz et al. 2019; Lin et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2021.1901730]).
Ecotype_references: Although viviparity (which permits a Fully Aquatic lifestyle in many modern amniotes) has been demonstrated in at least one species of pachypleurosaurs (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), and suggested in some species of nothosauroids (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]), these eosauropterygians retain jointed limbs that may have been able to amble onto land for short periods—it remains unclear to what degree (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]). As a result, while they were clearly adapted to spend most of their time in the water, we cannot resolve whether they were Highly Aquatic or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Non-pistosaurian sauropterygians are typically interpreted as strictly near-shore species (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]; Houssaye 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1749-4877.2009.00146.x]), though the size of some nothosaurs makes pelagic forays seem plausible. Even so, we follow previous suggestions and treat this species as exclusively near-shore, occupying primarily small or slow-moving water bodies, though we stress that this  scoring remains tentative.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Non-avian dinosaur interrelationships synthesized from the following sources: Carpenter et al. 2005 [Carpenter K, Miles C, Cloward K (2005). New Small Theropod from the Upper Jurassic Morrison Formation of Wyoming. Chapter 2, in: Carpenter K (ed), The Carnivorous Dinosaurs. Indiana University Press: Bloomington. pp. 23–48.]; Brusatte et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2010.04.001]; Xu et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature10288];  Rauhut et al. 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1203238109]; Brusatte et al. 2014 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cub.2014.08.034]; Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]; Pol and Goloboff 2020 [http://hdl.handle.net/11336/170477]; Madzia et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.12362]; Fabbri et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04528-0].
Ecotype_references: The edentulous beak of Ornithomimus and Gallimimus specimens, along with its associated soft-tissue remains in an exceptionally preserved Gallimimus specimen, led Norell et al. 2001 [https://doi.org/10.1038/35091139] to suggest that these ornithomimids were near-shore suspension feeders, who used a keratinous rhamphotheca to strain sediments for food in acqueous environments, in a manner similar to many modern anseriforms (Barrett 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1475-4983.2005.00448.x]). This claim is consistent with the recovery of most ornithomimids from marshy, riverine, or lacustrine deposites (Barrett 2005). However, this dietary niche was disputed by Barrett 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1475-4983.2005.00448.x], who argued that it would be difficult for such large animals to achieve their daily caloric  intake this way. Barrett also noted that the retention of stomach stones in ornithomimids implied the presence of a gastric mill, which would facilitate herbivory more than it would strain-feeding. However, strain-feeding mallards are also known to swallow stones to aid in digestion (Hassan and Moussa 2012 [https://doi.org/10.21608/JVA.2012.44877]), so Barrett's gastric mill counterargument seems moot. Even so, the aquatic affinities of ornithomimids remain unverified. We cannot confidently determine whether they were terrestrial, transiently aquatic, or moderately aquatic, and their ecotype remains unclear.
Limb_type_references: NA

The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]). However, it remains unclear whether they were moderately, highly, or fully aquatic, so their ecotype remains ambiguous.

Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered and demonstrably viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1986, p. 73 [Romer AS, Parsons TS (1986). The Vertebrate Body, Sixth Edition (First Edition 1949). CBS College Publishing: New York. 679 pp.]; Thewissen and Taylor 2007 [Thewiseen JGM, Taylor MA (2007). Aquatic Adaptations in the Limbs of Amniotes. Chapter 18, in: Hall BK (ed.), Fins into Limbs: Evolution, Development, and Transformation. The University of Chicago Press: Chicago. pp. 310–322]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines as “Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: Mosasaurines had fully fledged flippers, as supported by both osteological evidence and exceptional soft-tissue preservation (eg, Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773]; Lindgren et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms3423]), but the independent mobility of the individual digits within each flipper (and thus whether they should be "strongly flippered" or "weakly flippered") remains uncertain and may have varied among species.
Notes on occurrences: Only counted occurrences for P. tympaniticus
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Assumed mD3 was complete; if distal phalanges missing, then computed f_AZR is an underestimate.

Limb_type_references: Plesiosaurs had fully fledged fore- and hindflippers, as supported by both osteological evidence and exceptional soft-tissue preservation (Huxley 1871 [Huxley TH (1871) A Manual of the Anatomy of Vertebrated Animals. J. & A. Churchill: London. 510 pp. Accessed online via URL = https://archive.org/details/b21933236]; Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Caldwell 1997 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/4523834]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773];  Frey et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.18268/BSGM2017v69n1a5]; Vincent et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-017-1472-6]). Musculoskeletal reconstructions suggest that plesiosaurs could not move their digits independently (Krahl and Witzel 2021 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.12537]), so we score them as "Strongly Flippered".

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Simões et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1186/s12915-020-00901-5] and Bever and Norell 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsos.170570] were used for the interrelations of Rhynchocephalia.
Ecotype_references: Pleurosaurs are highly adapted for an aquatic lifestyle (Dupret 2004 [Dupret V (2004). The pleurosaurs: Anatomy and phylogeny. Revue de Paleobiologie vol. spéc. 9:61–80.]; Jones 2008; Bever and Norell 2017). The extreme reduction of the limbs and elongation of the body cast doubt on their ability to travel on land for extended periods. However, we have no data to resolve whether pleurosaurs were Highly or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic, so their ecotype remains ambiguous. Pleurosaurs are thought to have occupied shallower waters (see Bever and Norell 2017), so we tentatively score them as preferring small or slow-moving water bodies.

Ecotype_references:
Pontosaurs have highly modified body proportions, highly elongated dorsoventrally tall and laterally compressed tails, and reduced, acropodially elongated limbs (Caldwell 2006 [Caldwell MW (2006). A new species of Pontosaurus (Squamata, Pythonomorpha) from the Upper Cretaceous of Lebanon
and a phylogenetic analysis of Pythonomorpha.  Memorie della Società Italiana di Scienze Naturali e del Museo Civico di Storia Naturale di Milano XXXIV, Fascicolo III:  3–43.]). They were probably predominantly aquatic animals, but whether they were moderately, highly, or fully aquatic remains unclear. Caldwell (2006) suggests that they probably retained the ability to locomote with limited efficiency on land.
Limb_type_references:
The widened, acropodially elongate limbs of Pontosaurus led Caldwell 2006 [Caldwell MW (2006). A new species of Pontosaurus (Squamata, Pythonomorpha) from the Upper Cretaceous of Lebanon
and a phylogenetic analysis of Pythonomorpha.  Memorie della Società Italiana di Scienze Naturali e del Museo Civico di Storia Naturale di Milano XXXIV, Fascicolo III:  3–43.] to suggest that they may have functioned as hydrofoils, but it is not clear from the bones alone whether the limbs of Pontosaurus were fully fledged flippers.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Longest manual digit was mD4.

However, whether this taxon was moderately, highly, or fully aquatic remains unclear.

John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]; Pol and Goloboff 2020 [http://hdl.handle.net/11336/170477]; Madzia et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.12362]; Fabbri et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04528-0].
Ecotype_references: The aquatic affinities of Spinosaurus aegyptiacus are notoriously fraught. The species likely consumed fish (Brusatte 2021 and references therein [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cub.2021.09.002]), and was thus at least slightly aquatic. Isotopic data supports a somewhat aquatic lifestyle for many spinosaurids, which have ∂18 oxygen phosphate signatures more similar to semi-aquatic crocodilians and turtles than to other (presumably terrestrial) theropods (Amiot et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1130/G30402.1]). However, it remains unclear precisely how aquatic spinosaurids really were. Several morphological and histological features have supported more extreme aquatic habits in Spinosaurus, but it remains disputed whether Spinosaurus was merely a wading shoreline piscivore (Sereno et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.80092]) or a highly aquatic pursuit predator (Ibrahim et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-020-2190-3]). On the one hand, tall neural spines on the caudal vertebrae supported a paddle-like tail that may acted as an efficient propulsive organ in water (Ibrahim et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-020-2190-3]), and the bone compactness of its femur falls within the range of modern diving or highly aquatic reptiles (Fabbri et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04528-0]). On the other hand, most 3D computer models of Spinosaurus have found it to be a competent walker on land, and a highly buoyant, slow, and inefficient swimmer (Henderson 2018 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.5409]; Sereno et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.80092]). Moreover, at least two Spinosaurus specimens have been identified in inland fluvial deposits, suggesting a tolerance for less permanently aquatic and non-marine habitats (Sereno et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.80092]). From these conflicting lines of evidence, we cannot resolve whether the species is Transiently, Moderately, or Highly Aquatic. As a result, the ecotype of this species remains ambiguous. Given its many apparent morphological adaptations to life in the water, we tentatively score the limb type of the species as "Unclear, aquat.", although a transiently aquatic lifestyle would merit rescoring as "Unclear, terr." Either way, all researchers agree on some significant aquatic affinity for the species, and it has been associated with freshwater fish faunas (Ibrahim et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-020-2190-3]; Fabbri et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04528-0]).
Limb_type_references: NA

The manus (particularly D3) is much longer, relative to the zeugopodia, in Sereno et al.'s reconstruction than in Ibrahim et al.'s.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: The phylogenetic placement and monophyly of parareptiles have recently been disputed (see Ford and Benson 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41559-019-1047-3]; Simões et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.abq1898]: Science Advances). Our placement of Mesosaurs and other parareptiles within Pan-Reptilia varies in our different supertrees to reflect existing disagreements (see Methods and Supplementary Text). Piñeiro et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.4072/rbp.2021.3.04] clarify that Mesosaurus tenuidens is the only valid taxon of mesosaurid currently known, so we have renamed all the mesosaurid samples in our data set accordingly.
Ecotype_references: Although mesosaurs were viviparous (Piñeiro et al. 2012 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1080/08912963.2012.662230]) and clearly adapted for aquatic life in marine systems (Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Demarco et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.3389/fevo.2018.00109]), aspects of their vertebral morphometry have alluded to a more semi-aquatic lifestyle, with considerable time spent both in water and on land (Demarco et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.3389/fevo.2018.00109]; Demarco et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.3389/frym.2019.00039]). Adult mesosaurs may have occupied pelagic environments (Holmes 1953 [https://doi.org/10.1038/171669a0]; Verriere and Frobisch 2022 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.13866]), but we lack direct evidence concerning whether and to what extent they actually traversed on land, so it remains unclear whether mesosaurs were Moderately, Highly, or Fully Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All mesosaurids were scored as having extensive, complete pedal webbing, based on exceptional soft-tissues preserved on the hindlimb skeletons of the following mesosaurid specimens: SMNK_PAL_3806, SMF_R_4710, BSPG_1964_I_268, and URCR_064. Scorings were based on high-resolution photographs of the specimens, provided by Antoine Vérriere. Less soft-tissue preservation was discernible on forelimbs, but manual webbing phenotype was tentatively scored based on specimen NHMW_1987_0067_0001, which appears to have complete extensive webbing. Because the taxonomic breadth covered by these specimens remains unclear, the application of these scores to all sampled mesosaurids remains a tentative first approximation, as mesosaurids may have more variability in webbing phenotype than we can currently discern based on available specimens.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: The longest manual digit was mD3. The pes was too incomplete to gage whether pD4 or pD5 was longer.

Ecotype_references: The highly modified cranial anatomy of Suchomimus suggests that it was adapted for consuming fish (Arden et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cretres.2018.06.013]), but the absence of obvious postcranial adaptations for aquatic life (Arden et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cretres.2018.06.013]) and the level of bone compactness of its femur (Fabbri et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04528-0]) suggest that it was not an avid swimmer or diver. As a result, the species is usually considered a non-subaqueous forager with a Transiently Aquatic lifestyle. However, in the absence of additional evidence, we cannot rule out a purely Terrestrial lifestyle, so the precise ecotype of the taxon remains unclear.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Salisbury et al. 2006 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2006.3613]; Turner and Pritchard 2015 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.759]; Lijima et al. 2018 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsos.171774]
Ecotype_references: Susisuchus has been reconstructed as living "in and around one of the small streams that feed into the Crato Lagoon", which represents a euryhaline suite of freshwater and saline paleoenvironments (Salisbury et al. 2003 [Salisbury SW, Eberhard F, Martill DM, Buchy M-C (2003). A new crocodilian from the Lower Cretaceous Crato Formation of north-eastern Brazil. Palaeontographica Beiträge zur Naturgeschichte der Vorzeit. Abteilung A, Band 270: 3–47.). This suggests a Moderately Aquatic lifestyle as a nearshore ambush predator, akin to that of most modern crocodilian species (see Salisbury et al. 2006 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2006.3613]). We tentatively adopt this prediction here, though we cannot confidently rule out highly aquatic habits.
Limb_type_references: The holotype (SMNK PAL 3804) preserves extensive forelimb soft-tissue (Salisbury et al. 2003 [Salisbury SW, Eberhard F, Martill DM, Buchy M-C (2003). A new crocodilian from the Lower Cretaceous Crato Formation of north-eastern Brazil. Palaeontographica Beiträge zur Naturgeschichte der Vorzeit. Abteilung A, Band 270: 3–47.), which (from observation of the photographs) clearly preserves slight webbing between mD3–mD5 and possibly extensive webbing between mD1–mD3.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: The longest manual digit was mD2.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Currie 1982 [https://biostor.org/reference/111508]; Reisz et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2011.0439]; Pritchard and Nesbitt 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsos.170499]: Placed Tangasaurus, Thadeosaurus, and Hovasaurus in a polytomy within Tangasauridae, which I placed in a polytomy branching off after Claudiosauridae (following Pritchard and Nesbitt 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsos.170499]).
Ecotype_references: Morphological features suggest that Tangasaurus had an aquatic lifestyle (Harris and Carroll 1977 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/1303471]; Carroll 1981 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1981.0079]; Currie 1982 [https://biostor.org/reference/111508]). This taxon retains well-developed jointed appendages, making a moderately aquatic lifestyle plausible (Houssaye 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1749-4877.2009.00146.x]. However, it remains unclear whether and to what extent it traversed on land. As a result, we cannot resolve whether it had a moderately or highly aquatic lifestyle.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Currie 1982 [https://biostor.org/reference/111508]; Reisz et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2011.0439]; Pritchard and Nesbitt 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsos.170499]: Placed Tangasaurus, Thadeosaurus, and Hovasaurus in a polytomy within Tangasauridae, which I placed in a polytomy branching off after Claudiosauridae (following Pritchard and Nesbitt 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsos.170499]).
Ecotype_references: Aside from having slightly more symmetrical hand planforms than its ancestors, and being both from the same assemblage as and perhaps closely related to Claudiosaurus, there is no direct morphological evidence for aquatic habits in Thadeosaurus (Carroll 1981 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1981.0079]; Currie and Carroll 1984 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.1984.10011987]). Currie and Carroll 1984 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.1984.10011987] suggest that  "Thadeosaurus might have spent a great deal of time in the water, even though there is no morphological evidence in the skeleton to suggest swimming habits." The centrum length profile along its trunk is similar to that of Iguana iguana (Demarco et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.3389/fevo.2018.00109]), which also spends extensive time in the water (see above). However, in the absence of further evidence, we cannot resolve whether this taxon was terrestial, transiently aquatic, or moderately aquatic, and its ecotype remains unclear.

Ecotype_references: Wilberg et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-36795-1] report no major changes in habitat preference over the evolutionary history of crown Alligatoroidea. Given that we call all modern alligatorids “Moderately Aquatic” and inhabiting a range of low- and high-energy aquatic habitats, we do the same for this extinct caiman relative. This is consistent with the discussion of freshwater preferences by Walter et al. (2021). However, in the absence of additional data, this scoring remains highly speculative. In particular, it remains rather unclear whether this taxon had moderately or highly aquatic habits.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Evans and Wang 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cretres.2011.10.004] recover a close relationship between Yabeinosaurus and Liushusaurus; Dong et al. 2017 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cretres.2016.12.017] consider "Yabeinosaurus tenuis" a nomen dubium.
Ecotype_references: Some adult specimens of Yabeinosaurus preserve stomach contents containing fish, which indicate that they hunted in the water (Evans and Wang 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cretres.2011.10.004]), which implies at least moderately aquatic habits according to our scoring system. Remains of gravid females indicate that Yabeinosaurus mothers gave birth to live young (Wang and Evans 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-011-0820-1]), which would have enabled a fully aquatic lifestyle. However, Yabeinosaurus has no obvious morphological adaptations to life in the water (Evans and Wang 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cretres.2011.10.004]), retaining jointed appendages that could have presumably permitted walking on land. Thus, whether and to what extent Yabeinosaurus walked/lived/hunted on land remains unclear. No juvenile Yabeinosaurus specimens preserve gut contents containing fish, which may hint at a dietary shift towards fish-eating in adults, though this remains speculative (Evans and Wang 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cretres.2011.10.004]).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Long et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-014-1148-4] recover this taxon in a trichotomy with Placodontia and Eosauropterygia at the base of Sauropterygia. A new whole-body specimen did not resolve its phylogenetic position further (Chun et al. 2016 [https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/ sciadv.1501659]). Wang et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/ j.isci.2022.105635] recovered the specimen at the base of the line leading to Placodontia but note that its position remains unstable. We thus keep it in a trichotomy at the base of Sauropterygia, following Long et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-014-1148-4].
Ecotype_references: Chun et al. 2016 [https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.1501659] describe the taxon as marine and it appears well-adapted for an aquatic lifestyle, but it remains unclear how aquatic this taxon was.
Limb_type_references: NA
Photo credit: Cheng Long (Wuhan Center of China Geological Survey, Hubei Key Laboratory of Paleontology and Geological Environment Evolution)
Included_unguals?: No (pedal ungual morphology casts doubt on the presence of ungual claws but we can't be certain they aren't there)
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measured radius from proxmost point to medial-most (rather than lateral-most) point of distal epiphysis because lateral margin of distal epiphysis was missing from the slab. Longest digits were mD4 and pD4.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Our phylogenetic placement of geosaurs was derived from Young and De Andrade 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-3642.2009.00536.x], Young et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-3642.2011.00734.x], and Johnson et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.9808].
Ecotype_references: Metriorhynchids show several adaptations for a fully pelagic marine lifestyle (Romer 1956, p. 345 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Young et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1096-3642.2011.00734.x]; Foffa et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rsos.231951]; Johnson et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.9808]). Their flippered limb morphology casts doubt on their ability to walk on land for extended periods, and their lack of dorsal ornamentation with thermoregulatory osteoderms suggests that they did not engage in routine basking behavior and perhaps instead relied on muscle contraction associated with near-constant swimming for heat production (Spindler et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.26879/1099]; de Araujo Sena & Cubo 2023 [https://doi.org/10.26879/1099]). However, we do not know whether they came onto land to lay their eggs, so while we do know that they were either highly or fully aquatic, we cannot resolve which of these designations is more accurate.
Limb_type_references: Geosaurs have highly derived "paddle-like" limbs with reduced perichondral ossification (Fraas 1902 [Fraas E (1902). Die Meer-Crocodilier (Thalattosuchia) des oberen Jura unter specieller Berücksichtigung von Dacosaurus und Geosaurus. Palaeontographica (1846-1933) Band 49 Lieferung 1 (1902), pp. 1–72.]; Caldwell 1997 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/4523834]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi.org/10.1002/ajmg.10773]). Both its fore- and its hindlimbs are highly modified for swimming. However, the relative extremity, in the forelimbs, of joint surface reduction and integration and zeugo- and autopodial elements into a single unit—based on observation of Dakosaurus maximus (Rygol specimen) and Geosaurus specimen photographs (WDC CSG-203), along with reconstructions in Caldwell (1997, 2002, both cited above) lead us to suspect a relatively greater independent mobility of digits in the hindlimbs, so we tentatively score the forelimbs as Strongly Flippered and the hindlimbs as ambiguous.
Included_unguals?: Yes (given previously published descriptions of limbs as being flipper-like)
Limb_region_measurement_notes: mD3 length is an underestimate, taken to faint impression of penultimate phalanx, so missing mD3 ungual. Manual unguals were missing and no impressions of them were clear enough to measure. As a result, the actual f_AZR for this specimen (which, in this specimen's case, should include the ungual) would be higher. Longest manual digit unclear because distal-most phalanges missing. Longest pedal digit was pD4.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Sato et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1017/S0016756813000356] recover Diandongosaurus either within a massive polytomy at the base of Eosauropterygia or as branching off on the line leading up to the clade containing Pachypleurosauroidea + Nothosauroidea. Liu et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-021-01309-z] and Xu et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-24930-y] recover Diandongosaurus as a basally branching member of Pachypleurosauroidea. We tentatively follow the most recent analysis (Xu et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-24930-y]) in placing a Diandongosaurus+Majiashanosaurus clade at the base of Pachypleurosauroidea.
Ecotype_references: Although viviparity (which permits a Fully Aquatic lifestyle in many modern amniotes) has been demonstrated in at least one species of pachypleurosaurs (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), and suggested in some species of nothosauroids (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]), these eosauropterygians retain jointed limbs that may have been able to amble onto land for short periods—it remains unclear to what degree (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]). As a result, while they were clearly adapted to spend much of their time in the water, we cannot resolve whether they were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Pachypleurosaurs and nothosaurs are thought to have lived in shallow near-shore environments (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), but this one is fairly large (nearly 1m in length: Liu et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-021-01309-z], and may have been better able to travel through large or fast bodies of water. Since we don't know, we leave its Ecotype2 score ambiguous.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Wang et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/ j.isci.2022.105635] confidently recover Hanosaurus as the most basally branching member of Sauropterygiformes. However, Xu et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-24930-y] recover it in a tritomy at the base of Sauropterygia, and Neenan et al. 2015 [http://doi.org/10.1111/zoj.12277] [https://doi.org/ 10.1111/zoj.12277] recover it at the base of Pachypleurosauroidea. We incorporate the two more recent placements (from Wang et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/ j.isci.2022.105635] and Xu et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-24930-y]) in our analysis.
Ecotype_references: This taxon was found in marine sediments, has pachyostitic vertebrae, a reduced ilium with bar-shaped sacral ribs that would have hindered effective terrestrial locomotion, and an elongate trunk with short limbs (Wang et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.isci.2022.105635]). All these features suggest that Hanosaurus was predominantly aquatic, but whether it was Moderately, Highly, or Fully so remains unclear.

Limb_region_measurement_notes: Longest digits were mD3 and pD4. Distal-most phalanges of mD3 were missing and inferred from impressions.
proxp_measurement_notes: Proximal phalanx measurements taken in ventral view. They were fairly low resolution but still feasible to measure.

Ecotype_references: Although viviparity (which permits a Fully Aquatic lifestyle in many modern amniotes) has been demonstrated in at least one species of pachypleurosaurs (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), and suggested in some species of nothosauroids (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]), these eosauropterygians retain jointed limbs that may have been able to amble onto land for short periods—it remains unclear to what degree (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]). As a result, while they were clearly adapted to spend much of their time in the water, we cannot resolve whether they were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Pachypleurosaurs are thought to have lived in shallow near-shore environments (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Though Jiang et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/ 10.1080/02724634.2018.1480113.] do not recover a monophyletic Pachypleurosauroidea while Xu et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-24930-y] do, both analyses recover Panzhousaurus as branching off shortly before the Keichuosauridae among other traditional Pachypleurosaurs. Wang et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/ j.isci.2022.105635] supports this position.
Ecotype_references: Although viviparity (which permits a Fully Aquatic lifestyle in many modern amniotes) has been demonstrated in at least one species of pachypleurosaurs (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), and suggested in some species of nothosauroids (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]), these eosauropterygians retain jointed limbs that may have been able to amble onto land for short periods—it remains unclear to what degree (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]). As a result, while they were clearly adapted to spend much of their time in the water, we cannot resolve whether they were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Pachypleurosaurs are thought to have lived in shallow near-shore environments (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]).

Given that Ketchum and Benson's (2010) analysis is more comprehensive, we follow their placement in this analysis.
Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered and demonstrably viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1986, p. 73 [Romer AS, Parsons TS (1986). The Vertebrate Body, Sixth Edition (First Edition 1949). CBS College Publishing: New York. 679 pp.]; Thewissen and Taylor 2007 [Thewiseen JGM, Taylor MA (2007). Aquatic Adaptations in the Limbs of Amniotes. Chapter 18, in: Hall BK (ed.), Fins into Limbs: Evolution, Development, and Transformation. The University of Chicago Press: Chicago. pp. 310–322]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines as “Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: Plesiosaurs had fully fledged fore- and hindflippers, as supported by both osteological evidence and exceptional soft-tissue preservation (Huxley 1871 [Huxley TH (1871) A Manual of the Anatomy of Vertebrated Animals. J. & A. Churchill: London. 510 pp. Accessed online via URL = https://archive.org/details/b21933236]; Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Caldwell 1997 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/4523834]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773];  Frey et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.18268/BSGM2017v69n1a5]; Vincent et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-017-1472-6]). Musculoskeletal reconstructions suggest that plesiosaurs could not move their digits independently (Krahl and Witzel 2021 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.12537]), so we score them as "Strongly Flippered".
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Longest digits were mD4 and pD4. Forelimb and hindlimb measured from separate photographs. Scalebar in hindlimb photograph was blurry, so those absolute measurements should be treated cautiously (but the ratios are still reliable).

Ecotype_references: Yunguisaurus is a pistosaur, outside of Plesiosauria proper, and has a blend of plesiosaurian and non-plesiosaurian features in its appendicular skeleton (Sato et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/ 10.1111/pala.12048]). It appears flippered, and was probably fully aquatic, but we cannot resolve a priori whether it was fully flippered and whether it was fully aquatic or merely highly aquatic.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Neenan et al. 2013 [http://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms2633], Neenan et al. 2015 [http://doi.org/10.1111/zoj.12277] [https://doi.org/ 10.1111/zoj.12277] (strict consensus tree, Fig. 7), and Wang et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-24930-y] all recover Paraplacodus as branching off before Placodus within Placodontia.
Ecotype_references: Placodonts have a highly derived anatomy (ill-suited for walking on land), are present in aquatic sediments, and have extensive bone mass increase (via osteosclerosis), which all suggest an aquatic lifestyle (Klein et al. 2015 [https://doi.org/10.1163/18759866-08401005]; Sues 2019 [Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]). However, it remains unclear whether they were moderately, highly, or fully aquatic, so their ecotype remains ambiguous.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Wang et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/ j.isci.2022.105635] recover Sinosaurosphargis within a monophyletic Saurosphargidae that includes Eusaurosphargis. However, Scheyer et al. 2017 [http://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-017-04514-x] recover Sinosaurosphargis as branching off after Placodontia. We tentatively follow the more recent Wang et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/ j.isci.2022.105635] topology as it includes a larger sample of taxa and morphological characters, though our topology of basal sauropterygiforms varies across our eight supertrees (see Methods and Supplementary Text).
Ecotype_references: Sinosaurosphargis was recovered from a Triassic marine deposit and has several morphological features—including dorsally retracted nares, a huge, flat, osteoderm-covered trunk, and very short arms—that together seem to suggest a predominantly aquatic lifestyle (see Li et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2011.550368]). However, it remains unclear whether this taxon was moderately, highly, or fully aquatic, so its precise ecotype remains unclear.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Pol and Goloboff 2020 [http://hdl.handle.net/11336/170477] used for interrelations of Coelurosauria
Ecotype_references: Oviraptors are typically considered terrestrial cursors; though aquatic habits have been suggested for them in the past, these seem unlikely (Persons et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.4202/app.2012.0093]). This passage from Persons et al. 2013 summarizes this debate here: "the large size of the M. caudofemoralis in most oviraptorosaurs is consistent with their previously in- ferred cursorial potential. However, the M. longissimus and M. ilio-ischiocaudalis control tail motion, rather than femoral retraction, and the large anterior size of both these muscles still merits functional explanation. Barsbold (1977, 1983) pre- viously recognized the highly muscular nature of ovirapto- rosaur tails, and speculated that they may have been adapted for aquatic sculling. A swimming explanation is consistent with both a robust M. longissimus and M. ilio-ischiocaudalis, which are responsible for laterally swinging the tail and are large in many modern aquatic reptiles, and with the highly flexible nature of the oviraptorosaur caudal series. However, as later pointed out by Barsbold et al. (1990), the cursorial hind limbs of oviraptorosaurs are inconsistent with an aquatic life- style. Moreover, the reduced tail length, un-heightened neural spines, and lack of lateral compression strongly contradict the notion that oviraptorosaur tails were adapted for swimming. Additionally, the majority of oviraptorosaurs are associated with arid to semi-arid environments (Longrich et al. 2010)." While oviraptors were probably predominantly terrestrial, we cannot rule out transient forays into the water, so their precise ecotype remains ambiguous.

Ecotype_references: All chelonioids are highly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle, and spend the vast majority of their lives in the water, but nevertheless spend an extended period of time on land at least once each breeding season to nest on shorelines, over which they may travel great distances (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 531, Cheloniidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). As a result, we consider them "Highly" rather than "Fully" aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All crown chelonioids (“sea turtles”) were scored as having strongly flippered fore- and hindlimbs, following Wyneken 2001 [Wyneken J (2001). The anatomy of sea turtles. Seattle, WA: U.S. Department of Commerce NOAA Technical Memorandum NMFS-SEFSC-470.]. This was supported by direct observation of fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_02957 (Caretta caretta).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Phylogenetic placement of Archelon derived from Evers and Benson 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12384], strict consensus tree, Fig. 4.
Ecotype_references: Given its massive size, fully fledged flippers, and recovery from marine deposits, Archelon is typically considered highly adapted to life in the water (see Wieland 1902 [Wieland GR (1902). ART. XIV. Notes on the Cretaceous turtles, Toxochelys and Archelon, with a classification of the marine Testudinata. American Journal of Science 14: 95. Retrieved from https://www.proquest.com/scholarly-journals/art-xiv-notes-on-cretaceous-turtles-toxochelys/docview/89596439/se-2.];  Hirayama 1997 [Hirayama R (1997). Distribution and diversity of Cretaceous chelonioids. Chapter 8, in: Callaway JM, Nicholls EL. Ancient Marine Reptiles. Academic Press: San Diego. pp. 225–241. Accessed online via URL = https://www.researchgate.net/publication/256198355.). Academic Press.; Evers and Benson 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12384]). No turtles are viviparous, and Archelon has most recently been recovered within crown-group Testudines (Evers and Benson 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12384]), so it quite likely returned to land as modern sea turtles do to lay its eggs. As a result, we can confidently score this taxon as predominantly marine and highly aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Archelon is generally reconstructed as bearing fully fledged flippers (see Wieland 1902 [Wieland GR (1902). ART. XIV. Notes on the Cretaceous turtles, Toxochelys and Archelon, with a classification of the marine Testudinata. American Journal of Science 14: 95. Retrieved from https://www.proquest.com/scholarly-journals/art-xiv-notes-on-cretaceous-turtles-toxochelys/docview/89596439/se-2.]; Evers and Benson 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12384]). Moreover, Evers and Benson (2018) recover this taxon within crown Chelonioidea, and all crown chelonioids (“sea turtles”) were scored as having strongly flippered fore- and hindlimbs, following Wyneken 2001 [Wyneken J (2001). The anatomy of sea turtles. Seattle, WA: U.S. Department of Commerce NOAA Technical Memorandum NMFS-SEFSC-470.]. This was supported by direct observation of fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_02957 (Caretta caretta).
Notes on occurrences: The Paleobiology Database (https://paleobiodb.org/classic/checkTaxonInfo?taxon_no=128038&is_real_user=1) gives a wider age range for this taxon (83.6–66 Mya), but we opt here for the more conservative estimate noted in Evers and Benson 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12384].
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Measurements taken using larger >600mm length Fowler digital calipers. Humerus length taken in two intervals because it maxed out the largest available calipers, so this measurement is roughly 16 mm greater than 629 mm.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Given the historical uncertainty in the interrelationships of basal synapsids (Benson 2012; Ford & Benson 2020), Edaphosauridae (incl. Edaphosaurus) and Varanopidae (incl. Cabarzia) are placed in a polytomy at the base of Synapsida.
Ecotype_references: Edaphosaurus shows no obvious morphological adaptations to an aquatic lifestyle, and it seems intuitive to assume that it was fully terrestrial. However, Edaphosaurus specimens are commonly found in depositional environments representing swamps or swampy lakes (Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Bakker 1982 [https://doi.org/10.1126/science.217.4554.53]), and at least one fully articulated Edaphosaurus skeleton has been found in marine deposits (Reisz et al. 1982 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/1304403]). As a result, we cannot rule out aquatic habits, and its ecotype remains unclear.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: This taxon served as the outgroup to all others in every supertree in our analysis—a position supported by Cloutier et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-020-2100-8] and Stewart et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04990-w].
Ecotype_references: Tiktaalik had many features that helped it breathe and support its weight to some degree on land (Daeschler et al. 2006 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature04639]; Shubin et al. 2020). However, it is still thought to have been more aquatic than terrestrial (Dickson et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-020-2974-5]), though its precise aquatic affinity remains unclear.
Limb_type_references: Tiktaalik had fins, rather than purely terrestrial walking limbs (Shubin et al. 2006 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature04637]). However, because Tiktaalik is outside of both crown Tetrapoda and Apo-Tetrapoda, we refrain from scoring its limbs under our scheme.

Limb_region_measurement_notes: For autopod, measured longest proximodistally continuous series of distal radials. I refrained from measuring the dimensions of individual (proximal and ungual) phalanges due to ambiguous homologies.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Jiang et al. 2016 [https://doi.org/10.1038/srep26232]; Huang et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-020-64854-z]
Ecotype_references: Cartorhynchus is highly modified for an aquatic lifestyle, with fully fledged flippers (Motani et al. 2015 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13866]), suggesting that it was at least highly aquatic. However, the flippers may have been capable of dorsoventral flexion at the carpus, enabling limited locomotion on land (Motani et al. 2015 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13866]). Moreover, although viviparity has been demonstrated in some ichthyopterygians (Motani et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088640]), there is no direct evidence for ovipary or vivipary in nasorostrans or other non-ichthyopterygian ichthyosauromorphs. As a result, occasional necessary sojourns onto land cannot be ruled out, and the precise ecotype of this taxon remains ambiguous.
Limb_type_references: Motani et al. 2015 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature13866]
Limb_region_measurement_notes: For autopod, measured length conservatively (to err on the side of underestimation) given missing elements. Below the intermedium, most of the acropodium was missing, except for two sets of three distal phalanges. In the absence of a proximal phalanx, and intermediate phalanges on which to inflect the segmented line in ImageJ, I measured the acropod with the segmented line two setting two segments: the first from the distal-most tip of the intermedium to the base of the penultimate preserved phalanx of the middle digit, and the second from the base of that digit to the distal-most tip of the distal-most preserved phalanx of the same middle digit. While this forced us to include part of the carpus (eg, the length of a distal carpal) into our measurement, it also underestimated total length by removing all inflections typically set at intervening digits. Thus, while this measurement of acropod length is necessarily an approximation and should be treated with caution, it accurately (and rather conservatively) captures the great length of the autopod with respect to the zeugopod in this species. The approach described above was repeated for the anterior-most and posterior-most preserved digits in order to get acropod dimensions. mD1 was the longest digit.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Chaohusaurus was considered monophyletic and placed outside Ichthyopterygia, basal to Grippidia, following Huang et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.7561] and Moon 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1080/14772019.2017.1394922].
Ecotype_references: Chaohusaurus was highly adapted to life in the water, with flippers or highly flipper-like limbs (Motani et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2014.892011]), suggesting that this taxon was at least highly aquatic. It has also been shown to give birth in the water (Motani et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088640]), strongly hinting at fully or near-fully aquatic habits; however, even this evidence is not dispositive. At least one source suggests that harbor seals occasionally give birth in shallow water (https://seaworld.org/animals/all-about/harbor-seal/care-of-young/), despite not being fully aquatic (see Phoca vitulina entries above). If this claim is true, it indicates that highly but non-fully aquatic taxa may also occasionally give birth subaqueously. Thus, while Chaohusaurus was probably fully aquatic, we cannot rule out highly aquatic habits, and its precise ecotype thus remains uncertain.
Limb_type_references: We suspect that Motani et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2014.892011] are correct in treating this Chaohusaurus as flippered, but we cannot be certain that it possessed soft-tissue flippers incorporating the zeugopodia given the bones alone, as these limbs were not extremely hyperphalangeal and interdigital and wrist movement cannot be confidently discounted. As a result, we treat its flipper status with excessive caution and consider its limb phenotype ambiguous.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: For autopod, I assumed the proximal-most preserved acropodial bones were metacarpals, since their phalangeal count matched those of other, more osteologically mature Chaohusaurus specimens in the dataset. For posterior-most digit (mD5), included faint impression of distal-most phalanx in measurement. mD3 was the longest digit.

Ecotype_references: Although clearly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle (Romer 1956, pp. 344, 524 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Rieppel et al. 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1139/E05-076]), most thalattosaurs seem to retain well-developed jointed limbs, suggesting that they may have retained the ability to make limited trips on land. The degree to which they traveled onto land remains unclear, so we cannot resolve whether thalattosaurs were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Because they lack obvious adaptations for a pelagic existence, most thalattosaurs are considered to have occupied shallower water basins (Müller 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1139/E05-030]), but some thalattosaur genera seem more highly adapted to aquatic habits than others, so we consider their Ecotype2 score ambiguous. Thalattosaurs were marine, rather than freshwater (Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]).
Limb_type_references: The fore- and hindlimbs of Concavispina appear more highly adapted for swimming than those of other thalattosaurs (see Liu et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1666/12-059R1.1]), but whether they constituted true flippers remains unclear.
Included_unguals?: No (this species has a highly derived flipper-like limb morphology, but its unguals are still long, pointed, and slightly recumbent at their distal ends; as a result, they could plausibly bear keratinized ungual sheaths and we exclude them from digit length measurements to err on the side of caution with respect to measuring clawed unguals)
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Forelimb and hindlimb measurements were taken from different images. Manus not preserved. Longest pedal digit was pD5 (150 mm incl ungual), followed closely by pD4 (147 mm incl ungual). When the mD3 ungual (~17.6 mm long) was added to mD3 length (~99.6 mm excluding ungual), mD3 came out to 117 mm long.
Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths on unguals measured; unguals all visible in lateral view.

Ecotype_references: While this taxon was clearly adapted for an aquatic lifestyle (Carroll and Zhi-Ming 1991 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1991.0004]; Chen et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0102361]), its precise aquatic affinity remains unclear.
Limb_type_references: The limbs of hupehsuchians have been described before as paddles (Carroll and Zhi-Ming 1991 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1991.0004]; Chen et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0115244]) but precise joint ranges of motion and soft-tissue morphology associated with the limbs remain unclear.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: The distal tip of the manus of this specimen was likely severed prior to burial (see Chen et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0115244]). I assumed this taxon had the same phalangeal formula as other known hupehsuchians, with 6 phalanges on mD3. I only counted 4 preserved phalanges on mD3 in this specimen, so mD3 was incomplete and unable to be measured.

Ecotype_references: Basal ichthyopterygians were highly adapted to life in the water, with flippers or highly flipper-like limbs (Motani 1998; Motani et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2014.892011]), suggesting that they were at least highly aquatic. They have also been shown to give birth in the water (Motani et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088640]), strongly hinting at fully or near-fully aquatic habits; however, even this evidence is not dispositive. At least one source suggests that harbor seals occasionally give birth in shallow water (https://seaworld.org/animals/all-about/harbor-seal/care-of-young/), despite not being fully aquatic (see Phoca vitulina entries above). If this claim is true, it indicates that highly but non-fully aquatic taxa may also occasionally give birth subaqueously. Thus, while all basal ichthyopterygians were probably fully aquatic, we cannot rule out highly aquatic habits, and its precise ecotype thus remains uncertain.
Limb_type_references: The forelimb of Grippia was highly modified, leading Motani 1998 [Motani R (1998). First complete forefin of the ichthyosaur Grippia longirostris from the Triassic of Spitsbergen. Palaeontology 41: 591–599] to consider it a "forefin" or flipper, and we suspect that that designation is correct. However, given that it is not hyperphalangeal or hyperdactylous and seems to retain some of the articular surfaces associated with its elbow joint, we cannot rule out a soft-tissue morphology excluding the zeugopodia, so the flipper phenotype of this taxon remains unclear.
Included_unguals?: Yes (given derived flipper-like morphology with coin-shaped unguals)
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Longest digit was mD3.

Ecotype_references: Although viviparity (which permits a Fully Aquatic lifestyle in many modern amniotes) has been demonstrated in at least one species of pachypleurosaurs (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), and suggested in some species of nothosauroids (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]), these eosauropterygians retain jointed limbs that may have been able to amble onto land for short periods—it remains unclear to what degree (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]). As a result, while they were clearly adapted to spend much of their time in the water, we cannot resolve whether they were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Pachypleurosaurs are thought to have lived in shallow near-shore environments (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), but whether larger nothosaurs invaded larger or faster water bodies remains unclear.
Limb_type_references: NA
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Distal-most two phalanges of mD3 missing, preventing length measurement. Other digits too incomplete to collect acropod planform measurements.
proxp_measurement_notes: Slight parallax prevented reliable proximal phalanx measurements for mD3.

Thalattosaurs were marine, rather than freshwater (Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Moon 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1080/14772019.2017.1394922] recovers Mixosaurus confidently as one of the most basally branching members of Ichthyosauria.
Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered and demonstrably viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1986, p. 73 [Romer AS, Parsons TS (1986). The Vertebrate Body, Sixth Edition (First Edition 1949). CBS College Publishing: New York. 679 pp.]; Thewissen and Taylor 2007 [Thewiseen JGM, Taylor MA (2007). Aquatic Adaptations in the Limbs of Amniotes. Chapter 18, in: Hall BK (ed.), Fins into Limbs: Evolution, Development, and Transformation. The University of Chicago Press: Chicago. pp. 310–322]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines as “Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: All ichthyosaurs are commonly described as having fully flippered fore- and hindlimbs (eg, Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773]; Motani 2005 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2005)025[0462:DTMIAD]2.0.CO;2]; Maxwell 2012 [https://doi.org/10.1002/jez.b.22459]; Gutarra and Rahman 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1111/brv.12790]); Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]. These flippers were highly integrated structures [Fernández et al. 2020 [http://dx.doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2020.0281], and presumably encased in dense connective tissue like those of modern-day cetaceans (Eriksson et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earscirev.2022.103965]), so that their digits were not independently mobile.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: The distal-most phalanges are not preserved, so the mD3 length and acropod planform measurements taken here are quite likely underestimates. Thus, AZR and ASR are likely underestimates as well. Given that this specimen is polydactylous, digit homologies were ambiguous; mD3 was taken to be the complete digit below the intermedial element level with the posterior margin of the radius; the longest digit (63 mm) was the one below the radiale (== the anterior-most mesopodial).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Specimen labeled as "Nothosaurus youngi", but was reassigned to "Lariosaurus youngi" by Lin et al. 2017 (JVP), so we relabeled it here accordingly. Placement and interrelationships of ‘nothosauroids’ and ‘pachypleurosaurs’ varied among our different supertrees (see Methods and Supplementary Text). These differing placements and interrelationships were synthesized from the following sources: Sander 1989 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1989.0103]; Neenan et al. 2013 [https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms2633]; Hinz et al. 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2019.1585364]; Lin et al. 2021 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2021.1901730]; Xu et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-11309-2]; Xu et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-24930-y].
Ecotype_references: Although viviparity (which permits a Fully Aquatic lifestyle in many modern amniotes) has been demonstrated in at least one species of pachypleurosaurs (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), and suggested in some species of nothosauroids (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]), these eosauropterygians retain jointed limbs that may have been able to amble onto land for short periods—it remains unclear to what degree (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]). As a result, while they were clearly adapted to spend most of their time in the water, we cannot resolve whether they were Highly Aquatic or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Non-pistosaurian sauropterygians are typically interpreted as strictly near-shore species (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]; Houssaye 2009 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1749-4877.2009.00146.x]), though the size of some nothosaurs makes pelagic forays seem plausible. Even so, we follow previous suggestions and treat this species as exclusively near-shore, occupying primarily small or slow-moving water bodies, though we stress that this  scoring remains tentative.

Ungual_measurement_notes: mD3 ungual not sufficiently visible to measure

Limb_type_references: The limbs of hupehsuchians have been described before as paddles (Carroll and Zhi-Ming 1991 [https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.1991.0004]; Chen et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0115244]) but precise joint ranges of motion and soft-tissue morphology associated with the limbs remain unclear.
Included_unguals?: Yes (mD3 ungual appears coin-shaped—see Chen et al. 2014c)
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Longest manual digit was mD3.

Ecotype_references: Placodonts have a highly derived anatomy (ill-suited for walking on land), are present in aquatic sediments, and have extensive bone mass increase (via osteosclerosis), which all suggest an aquatic lifestyle (Klein et al. 2015 [https://doi.org/10.1163/18759866-08401005]; Sues 2019 [Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]). However, it remains unclear whether they were moderately, highly, or fully aquatic, so their ecotype remains ambiguous.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Wang et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/ j.isci.2022.105635] recover Sinosaurosphargis within a monophyletic Saurosphargidae that includes Eusaurosphargis. However, Scheyer et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-017-04514-x] recover Sinosaurosphargis as branching off after Placodontia. We tentatively follow the more recent Wang et al. 2022 topology as it includes a larger sample of taxa and morphological characters.
Ecotype_references: Sinosaurosphargis was recovered from a Triassic marine deposit and has several morphological features—including dorsally retracted nares, a huge, flat, osteoderm-covered trunk, and very short arms—that together seem to suggest a predominantly aquatic lifestyle (see Li et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2011.550368]). However, it remains unclear whether this taxon was moderately, highly, or fully aquatic, so its precise ecotype remains unclear.

Limb_region_measurement_notes: mD3 was incomplete, so I measured mD4 (excluding ungual) instead. Given the incompleteness of the acropod, it was unclear which digit was longest; the actual autopod measurement here (mD4 length) may thus slightly overestimate or underestimate mD3 length. Assumed distal-most preserved phalanx on mD1 was penultimate phalanx. For proximal phalanx measurements, used mD3 as in all other taxa.

Ecotype_references: Basal ichthyopterygians were highly adapted to life in the water, with flippers or highly flipper-like limbs (Motani 1998; Motani et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2014.892011]), suggesting that they were at least highly aquatic. It has also been shown to give birth in the water (Motani et al. 2014 [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088640]), strongly hinting at fully or near-fully aquatic habits; however, even this evidence is not dispositive. At least one source suggests that harbor seals occasionally give birth in shallow water (https://seaworld.org/animals/all-about/harbor-seal/care-of-young/), despite not being fully aquatic (see Phoca vitulina entries above). If this claim is true, it indicates that highly but non-fully aquatic taxa may also occasionally give birth subaqueously. Thus, while all basal ichthyopterygians were probably fully aquatic, we cannot rule out highly aquatic habits, and its precise ecotype thus remains uncertain.
Limb_type_references: The forelimb of Utatsusaurus was highly modified, leading Motani 1997 [https://doi.org/10.1017/S0022336000039482] to consider it a "forefin" or flipper, and we suspect that designation is correct. However, given that it is not hyperphalangeal or hyperdactylous and seems to retain some of the articular surfaces associated with its elbow joint, we cannot rule out a soft-tissue morphology excluding the zeugopodia, so the flipper phenotype of this taxon remains unclear.
Included_unguals?: Yes (given derived flipper-like morphology with flattened coin-shaped unguals)
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Note big gap in slab between base of metacarpal and more distal phalanges; in measuring, I assumed this gap was through a single continuous slab, rather separating two separate slabs. Thus, I measured from the base of  metacarpal iii pre-gap to the base of the first phalanx preserved post-gap, and so on, with a single segmented line in ImageJ. Poor preservation of the distal-most acropodium made it difficult to resolve the longest digit; the anterior- and posterior-most digits were insufficiently preserved to measure along their entire length.
proxp_measurement_notes: The mD3 proximal phalanx was presumably within the missing part of the slab.
