Notes on occurrences: Wayaosaurus was recovered from the Wayao Member of the Falang Formation (Chai et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1080/08912963.2023.2264886]), which dates to the uppermost Ladinian (Lu et al. 2018). Because the precise dates of the Wayao Member have to our knowledge not been resolved, we set its occurrence range conservatively to that of the Ladinian, roughly 237–229 Ma (following Lu et al. 2018 [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.palwor.2017.05.005]).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Wang et al. 2022 [https://doi.org/10.1016/ j.isci.2022.105635] recover Wumengosaurus as one of the most basally branching eosauropterygians. However, others recover it farther up the Eosauropterygia tree, branching off either shortly before Nothosauroidea (Wu et al. 2011 [https://doi.org/10.1080/02724634.2011.546724]) or deep within Pachypleurosauridae (Xu et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-24930-y]). While there is no consensus on the phylogenetic placement of Wumengosaurus within Eosauropterygia, we tentatively follow the most recent analysis (Xu et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-24930-y]) and place it as the sister-group to Luopingosaurus+Honghesaurus.
Ecotype_references: Although viviparity (which permits a Fully Aquatic lifestyle in many modern amniotes) has been demonstrated in at least one species of pachypleurosaurs (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]), and suggested in some species of nothosauroids (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]), these eosauropterygians retain jointed limbs that may have been able to amble onto land for short periods—it remains unclear to what degree (Renesto et al. 2003 [https://doi.org/10.1671/1840-21]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]). As a result, while they were clearly adapted to spend much of their time in the water, we cannot resolve whether they were Moderately, Highly, or Fully/Near-Fully Aquatic. Pachypleurosaurs are thought to have lived in shallow near-shore environments (Cheng et al. 2004 [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature03050]).

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Matsumoto et al. 2019's (https://doi.org/10.1080/14772019.2018.1494220) strict consensus tree places Coeruleodraco in a polytomy with Lazarussuchus, Neochoristodera, Monjurosuchus+Phylidrosaurus, Khurendukhosaurus, and  Hyphalosaurus+Shokawa. This multifurcating unresolved clade is the sister group to Cteniogenys within Choristodera.
Ecotype_references: Choristoderes were highly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle. Many but not all of them gave birth to live young (Ji et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-010-0654-2]; Jiang et al. 2023 [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41559-023-02074-0]), and the necks of champsosaurs in particular were more limited in dorsiflexion than modern crocodilians, casting doubt on their ability to ambush terrestrial prey (Matsumoto et al. 2020 [https://doi.org/10.1111/joa.13600]). However, it remains disputed whether they were capable on walking on land (see Ji et al. 2010 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-010-0654-2] and references therein). As a result, their current designation remains tentative. Choristoderes have only been found in freshwater paleoenvironments (Sues 2019 [Sues H-D (2019). The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution. John Hopkins University Press: Baltimore. 385 pp.]).
Limb_type_references: Based on exceptional soft-tissue preservation, complete moderate-to-extensive webbing has been clearly identified in one species of choristodere (Monjurosuchus splendens [GMV 2135]: Gao et al. 2000 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2000)020[0417:EFMOAS]2.0.CO;2]), and suggested for Hyphalosaurus on the basis of scale morphology in PKUP V1052 (Gao and Ksepka 2008 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1469-7580.2008.00907.x]), though the latter claim has yet to be tested with other modalities of data. To our knowledge, interdigital webbing has not been directly demonstrated in any choristodere taxa except for Monjurosuchus, so the webbing phenotypes of individual species within Choristodera remain unclear.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Longest digits were mD3 and pD4. mD3 antmost digit somewhat damaged.
Ungual_measurement_notes: Phalanges were not sufficiently exposed in the proper views to measure.
proxp_measurement_notes: Phalanges were not sufficiently exposed in the proper views to measure.

Limb_type_references: Based on exceptional soft-tissue preservation, complete moderate-to-extensive webbing has been clearly identified in one species of choristodere (Monjurosuchus splendens [GMV 2135]: Gao et al. 2000 [https://doi.org/10.1671/0272-4634(2000)020[0417:EFMOAS]2.0.CO;2]), and suggested for Hyphalosaurus on the basis of scale morphology in PKUP V1052 (Gao and Ksepka 2008 [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1469-7580.2008.00907.x]), though the latter claim has yet to be tested with other modalities of data. To our knowledge, interdigital webbing has not been directly demonstrated in any choristodere taxa except for Monjurosuchus, so the webbing phenotypes of individual species within Choristodera remain unclear.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: Limbs disarticulated; acropodia too disarticulated to measure.

Sources_for_phylogenetic_placement: Vincent et al. 2017 (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-017-1472-6) refer this specimen to Plesiosauroidea; we thus place it in a polytomy at the base of Plesiosauroidea following the interrelations of Plesiosauria given by Ketchum and Benson 2010 (https://doi.org/ 10.1111/j.1469-185X.2009.00107.x).
Ecotype_references: It is typically assumed that all the fully flippered and demonstrably viviparous marine reptiles of the Mesozoic (e.g., ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines) could not travel on land, or seldom did so without great difficulty. For explicit allusions to this view, see Romer and Parsons 1986, p. 73 [Romer AS, Parsons TS (1986). The Vertebrate Body, Sixth Edition (First Edition 1949). CBS College Publishing: New York. 679 pp.]; Thewissen and Taylor 2007 [Thewiseen JGM, Taylor MA (2007). Aquatic Adaptations in the Limbs of Amniotes. Chapter 18, in: Hall BK (ed.), Fins into Limbs: Evolution, Development, and Transformation. The University of Chicago Press: Chicago. pp. 310–322]; Griebeler and Klein 2019 [https://doi.org/10.1111/pala.12425]. Under this plausible assumption, we classify all ichthyosaurs, plesiosaurs, and mosasaurines as “Fully Aquatic.”
Limb_type_references: Plesiosaurs had fully fledged fore- and hindflippers, as supported by both osteological evidence and exceptional soft-tissue preservation (Huxley 1871 [Huxley TH (1871) A Manual of the Anatomy of Vertebrated Animals. J. & A. Churchill: London. 510 pp. Accessed online via URL = https://archive.org/details/b21933236]; Romer 1956 [Romer AS (1956). Osteology of the Reptiles, 1997 Reprint Edition. University of Chicago Press: Chicago. 772 pp.]; Caldwell 1997 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/4523834]; Caldwell 2002 [https://doi/org/10.1002/ajmg.10773];  Frey et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.18268/BSGM2017v69n1a5]; Vincent et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-017-1472-6]). Musculoskeletal reconstructions suggest that plesiosaurs could not move their digits independently (Krahl and Witzel 2021 [https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.12537]), so we score them as "Strongly Flippered".
Notes on occurrences: This specimen is from the Posidonienschiefer Fm. (Vincent et al. 2017 [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00114-017-1472-6]), which dates from 182.7–175.6 Ma (The Paleobiology Database: https://paleobiodb.org/classic/displayStrata?geological_group=&formation=Posidonienschiefer&group_formation_member=Posidonienschiefer). We use this stratigraphic range in place of the full range for Plesiosauroidea to make the age estimate for this taxon more robust against taxonomic revision.
Limb_region_measurement_notes: mD1 incompletely preserved. No scale provided, so all images are in pixels.

Ecotype_references: A detailed explanation for the aquatic affinities of crocodylians is given in our Supplementary Text.
Limb_type_references: Fluid-preserved YPM_HERR specimens 06531, 06535, and YPM_HERR_016581, and taxidermied specimen YPM_HERR_015068: Webbing reaches in arcs to ungual across mD2–mD4, but extends less distally in mD5 and is absent between mD1 and mD2. Pedal webbing spans all digits with varying extents, not quite reaching the base of the ungual on any of them.

Neonate?: Yes probably (it's very small and long bone epiphyses are unossified)
Ecotype_references: All chelonioids are highly adapted to an aquatic lifestyle, and spend the vast majority of their lives in the water, but nevertheless spend an extended period of time on land at least once each breeding season to nest on shorelines, over which they may travel great distances (Vitt and Caldwell 2014, p. 531, Cheloniidae [in Vitt LJ, Caldwell JP (2014) Herpetology: An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles, Fourth Edition. Elsevier: Amsterdam. 757 pp.]). As a result, we consider them "Highly" rather than "Fully" aquatic.
Limb_type_references: All crown chelonioids (“sea turtles”) were scored as having strongly flippered forelimbs and hindlimbs, following Wyneken 2001 [Wyneken J (2001). The anatomy of sea turtles. Seattle, WA: U.S. Department of Commerce NOAA Technical Memorandum NMFS-SEFSC-470.]. This was supported by direct observation of fluid-preserved specimen YPM_R_02957 (Caretta caretta).

Ungual_measurement_notes: No keratinous sheaths present on unguals measured; mD3 ungual surprisingly broad distally.

Ecotype2: primarily on land, often in water
Ecotype_references: Although eastern box turtles are usually described as terrestrial and some sub-populations live within semi-arid deserts, many Terrapene subpopulations live in wetter environments and frequently wade in, seek out, and sit within shallow temporary water bodies (eg, ponds) as a way to cool down (Donaldson and Echternacht 2005 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/4092902]). Some individuals have been reported to remain in these shallow-water ponds for over 20 consecutive days (Donaldson and Echternacht 2005 [https://www.jstor.org/stable/4092902]). Many individuals have also been reported swimming in shallow salt water or swimming in freshwater streams, sometimes in large aggregations. Their diet primarily consists of terrestrial plant and animal matter (van Dijk 2011 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2011-1.RLTS.T21641A9303747.en]), but they are probably opportunistic omnivores. Given the frequency and extensive reporting of this taxon's sojourns into water, we tentatively classify it as Moderately Aquatic, though whether the species in Moderately or Transiently Aquatic may vary by subpopulation.
Limb_type_references: Terrapene carolina hands and feet are strongly clawed with partial webbing (Ernst and McBreen 1991 [Ernst CH, McBreen JF (1991). Terrapene carolina (Linnaeus): Eastern Box Turtle. In: Zug GR (ed), Catalogue of American Amphibians and Reptiles. pp. 512.1–512.13]), which appears to be slight and span at least across mD1–mD4, but appears very slight or absent across mD4–mD5 (Research-grade iNaturalist observations https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/201883347, https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/202218520, https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/202311028). Webbing across the pedal digits is more distally extensive (https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/202288961) but no sufficiently clear iNaturalist observations exist to resolve its full extent across the pes.

Ecotype_references: Although the green tree monitor is primarily terrestrial (Shea et al 2018 [http://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2018- 2.RLTS.T42485751A101752115.en]), it is an excellent swimmer (Baldwin 2006 [Baldwin B (2006). Successful care and reproduction of green tree monitors (Varanus prasinus) at the San Diego Zoo. Iguana 13: 283–287.]), which is thought to occasionally swim or wade in the water in the wild (Shuter 2014 [Shuter AD (2014). A novel underwater foraging behavior observed in Varanus prasinus at the Wildlife Conservation Society’s Bronx Zoo. Biawak 8: 61–63.]), and one individual has even once been observed foraging underwater in captivity (Shuter 2014 [Shuter AD (2014). A novel underwater foraging behavior observed in Varanus prasinus at the Wildlife Conservation Society’s Bronx Zoo. Biawak 8: 61–63.]). Assuming this is a very rare occurrence, but that very occasional swimming is typical for the species, we consider this taxon Transiently Aquatic.
Limb_type_references: Varanus spp. were scored from fluid-preserved YPM VZ specimens (V. beccarii: YPM_HERR_010047; V. exanthematicus: YPM_HERR_010408; V. griseus: YPM_HERR_003865; Varanus dumerilii: YPM_HERR_010122; V. salvator: YPM_HERR_009466). All observed Varanus specimens appear to lack interdigital webbing.

Ecotype_references: Lenothrix canus is a predominantly arboreal species, with no reported aquatic habits (Pimsai et al. 2014 [Pimsai U, Pearch MJ, Satasook C, Bumrungsri S, Bates PJ (2014). Murine rodents (Rodentia: Murinae) of the Myanmar-Thai-Malaysian peninsula and Singapore: taxonomy, distribution, ecology, conservation status, and illustrated identification keys. Bonn Zoological Bulletin 63: 15-114.]; Ruedas et al. 2019 [https://dx.doi.org/10.2305/IUCN.UK.2019-1.RLTS.T11499A165117683.en]).
Limb_type_references: No webbing has been mentioned for this taxon despite careful morphological description (Pimsai et al. 2014 [Pimsai U, Pearch MJ, Satasook C, Bumrungsri S, Bates PJ (2014). Murine rodents (Rodentia: Murinae) of the Myanmar-Thai-Malaysian peninsula and Singapore: taxonomy, distribution, ecology, conservation status, and illustrated identification keys. Bonn Zoological Bulletin 63: 15-114.]), which lead us to suspect that this species is unwebbed. However, we were not able to directly observe any specimens and there are no sufficiently clear images on iNaturalist for us to confidently classify its webbing phenotype.
